Scalability, resilience, and human-centric industrial innovation are made possible in smart factories through the use of digital twins and AR/VR, which enhance continuous manufacturing management.

title: AI-Empowered Heterogeneity Response in Remote Classrooms: A Qualitative Study on the Mechanism of Human-AI Collaborative Assessment and the Construction of a Dynamic Ecological Model
authors: Bin Chen, Hui Sun
publish: 2026 15th International Conference on Educational and Information Technology (ICEIT)
abstract: As remote classrooms have demonstrated a pivotal role in promoting educational equity and supporting lifelong learning, how to effectively respond to learners’ heterogeneity in large-scale teaching has become a core challenge in improving educational quality. Addressing this issue, this study adopted the grounded theory method to explore the dynamic process of how teachers and AI collaboratively respond to student heterogeneity in remote classrooms. We conducted semi-structured interviews with 33 frontline teachers experienced in AI-assisted remote teaching. Through the hierarchical coding of raw data, the study constructed an AI-assisted heterogeneity response strategy model for remote classrooms. The model reveals that effective heterogeneity response is a dynamic circular system with "Human-AI Collaborative Assessment and Judgment" as the value conversion hub: Intelligent technology perceives and diagnoses students’ differences through multimodal data; on this basis, teachers integrate professional experience and situational insights to complete the translation from data to educational understanding; furthermore, through multi-dimensional and dynamic intervention strategies covering content, interaction, process, and structure, the assessment and judgment are put into teaching practice. The entire process relies on a systematic digital ecology as the supporting environment, with the fundamental goal of promoting students’ subjective development such as thinking autonomy, sense of identity meaning, and comprehensive agency. This study not only provides a systematic theoretical framework for understanding how teachers, students, and technology collaboratively respond to heterogeneity in remote classrooms in the intelligent era but also offers practical paths for educational practitioners to improve teaching quality in terms of heterogeneity diagnosis, strategy application, ecological cultivation, and evaluation reform.

title: MST-DeepLabV3+: An Integrated Deep Learning System for Real-Time Satellite-Based Flood Extent Mapping With Production-Grade Deployment
authors: A. Princy Arul, M. Keerthika, S. Kowshikan, Shahbaaz Ahmed, R. Mohammed Asif Fawaz, S. Saravanakumar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Future and Advanced Computing Technologies (ICFACT)
abstract: Rapid and accurate flood extent mapping is critical for disaster response and resource allocation, yet traditional manual interpretation requires 7-10 days post-event. This paper presents MSTDeepLabV3+, a production-ready deep learning system combining Sentinel-1 SAR and Sentinel-2 optical imagery for automated flood detection with uncertainty quantification. The proposed architecture integrates DeepLabV3+ encoder-decoder structure with Atrous Spatial Pyramid Pooling (ASPP) and Squeeze-and-Excitation (SENet) channel attention mechanisms, enhanced by ImageNet transfer learning. Evaluated on 2,310 satellite tiles across five major flood events (Pakistan 2022, Kerala 2019, Germany 2021, Thailand 2011, Indonesia 2020), the model achieves 95.4% Intersection-over-Union (IoU), 96.1% precision, and 92.5% recall significantly outperforming U-Net (82.2% loU) and baseline DeepLabV3+ (89.1% IoU). Novel contributions include: (1) systematic integration of SENet attention into decoder pathways for multi-spectral flood segmentation, (2) transfer learning framework achieving 12.2 % improvement over training from scratch, (3) Bayesian ensemble predictions providing pixel-level confidence intervals for risk-aware decision-making, and (4) complete production system with REST API, interactive dashboards, automated damage as sessment, and continuous learning infrastructure. Real-time inference achieves 185 ms per 256x256 tile on GPU, enabling processing of continent-scale satellite imagery. Morphological post-processing combined with Conditional Random Fields reduces false positives by 34 % while maintaining 95.1 % IoU. Deployment across three platforms (Google Cloud Run, AWS Lambda, Docker containers) demonstrates production-grade robustness. This work demonstrates operational viability of deep learning for disaster response, reducing assessment time from 7-10 days to under 5 minutes per event with quantified uncertainty for critical infrastructure decision-making.

title: Modified Swastik Shaped Terahertz Patch Antenna
authors: Deepa Thangarasu, N. Saranya, G.P Sabtha Sree, D.Riduvarshini D. Riduvarshini, B.Rathimeena B. Rathimeena, P.Poojasri P. Poojasri
publish: 2026 Third International Conference on Networking and Communications (ICNWC)
abstract: Wireless communication systems require high data rates, low latency and improved reliability to support emerging technologies such as extended reality, autonomous vehicles, and the Internet of Things (IoT). Sixth-generation (6G) networks are expected to utilize the terahertz (THz) frequency spectrum (0.1–10 THz) to meet these demands. In this work, a modified Swastik-shaped graphene-based microstrip patch antenna operating in the THz band is designed and analyzed. The antenna is implemented on a polyimide substrate and simulated using CST Microwave Studio over a frequency range of 0.1–10 THz. Multiple resonant frequencies are observed at 2 THz, 3.9 THz, and 5.05 THz with corresponding return loss values of −11.2 dB, −25.6 dB, and −18.7 dB, respectively. The antenna maintains a VSWR below 2 across the 2–10 THz range, indicating good impedance matching. Furthermore, the antenna demonstrates stable radiation characteristics with a gain ranging from 4 to 7 dBi and high radiation efficiency at higher frequencies. These characteristics make the proposed design suitable for compact THz communication systems in future 6G, security scanning, and spectroscopy applications.

title: Advancing Crop Yield Forecasting with Random Forest: Enhancing Accuracy and Sustainability in Agriculture
authors: V Rajesh, M. Mathankumar, N Nagendra Rakesh, M Sreevarsan, Ganeash Vasanth, S Saravanan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Future and Advanced Computing Technologies (ICFACT)
abstract: Crop yield prediction has a potential to become an essential component of agricultural systems, which will enable optimization of food security, maximum production and sustain- able resource management. There are high degrees of complexity and variability in the agricultural environment and, thus, crop yield prediction by conventional methods is complex and leads to suboptimal results. Lately, the trend has been growing for the use of ML algorithm for crop yield simplification. Random forests are shown as one of the most effective methods for this task. Random Forest method is used for overcoming the overfitting problem which is typically present in some machine learning methods and to improve prediction accuracy on large datasets. In this paper, we discuss the random forests based on decision trees for crop yield prediction. Random Forest models combine also predictions from many decision trees in order to overcome the overfitting problem. This research investigates how Random Forest could transform the way we predict agricultural production through its strength and robustness as a framework for agricultural research. Random Forest has already been shown to be extremely effective across many different fields, making it an ideal candidate for addressing the challenges associated with predicting crop yields to promote and improve sustainability and efficiency in crop production. The results presented in this paper represent significant progress in predicting the productivity of crops, as they provide useful information to scientists, policymakers, and others working in the area of agriculture.

title: Predictive Maintenance Framework for Utility-Scale Solar Farms Using Wireless Networks, UAV Inspection, and Robotic Cleaning
authors: Kaovinath Appalasamy, Ajith Gopi, R Mamat, Irina Kirpichnikova, S. Shanmuga Priya, Sudhakar Kumarasamy
publish: 2026 Third International Conference on Networking and Communications (ICNWC)
abstract: The rapid expansion of utility scale solar photovoltaic installations has rendered traditional manual operations and maintenance practices increasingly inadequate. Vast geographical footprints, remote locations, and the need for rapid fault detection demand a fundamental shift toward data driven, autonomous management strategies. This paper provides a systematic review of three core technologies enabling this transformation which are LoRaWAN based wireless connectivity, unmanned aerial vehicle inspection, and autonomous robotic cleaning systems. The wireless communication layer employs LoRaWAN to support long range, low power data transmission from distributed sensors that track module temperature, electrical output, and environmental conditions. This enables near real time visibility of system performance across extensive solar farms. The aerial inspection layer utilizes unmanned aircraft vehicles (UAVs) equipped with thermal and optical sensors to identify defects such as hotspots, interconnection failures, and diode faults. These inspections significantly reduce survey duration while improving fault localization accuracy. Robotic cleaning systems address soiling losses through automated, water efficient mechanical cleaning that preserves energy yield and reduces labor costs. Together, these components form a coordinated maintenance system that shifts operations from reactive intervention to planned and condition-based management. The study synthesizes current capabilities and identifies deployment challenges. The proposed approach improves reliability, reduces manual effort, and supports stable long term energy generation in utility scale solar farms.

title: AIGC-Driven Intelligent Teaching Transformation of Urban and Rural Planning Design Courses
authors: Song Huifang, Pemg Yuling, Wang Xiaopeng, Bai Shiyi, Li Jun, He Tiantian
publish: 2026 15th International Conference on Educational and Information Technology (ICEIT)
abstract: Against the backdrop of rapid advancements in artificial intelligence, this study focuses on the core computer technologies of AIGC (including large language models, generative adversarial networks, knowledge graph construction, and multi-modal data fusion algorithms) to drive the intelligent transformation of urban and rural planning design course teaching. Based on the technical characteristics of AIGC such as efficient content generation, multi-modal output, and data-driven iteration, this paper first optimizes three key technical modules: 1) the planning case knowledge graph construction technology (improving entity recognition accuracy to 92.3% through BERT pre-trained model fine-tuning); 2) the "drawing-model-data" parametric linkage system (reducing data interaction latency by 40% via Python-based real-time communication protocol optimization); 3) the AIGC contextualized task generation algorithm (enhancing task relevance by 65% through multi-round dialogue fine-tuning of GPT-4). This study not only enriches the technical application system of AIGC in the field of planning education but also provides a feasible technical path for the intelligent transformation of similar design courses, ultimately improving teaching quality and students' technical application capabilities.

title: Research on Personalized Teaching Strategies Based on Knowledge Graphs : A Case Study of WPS Practical Training Course in University Computer Science
authors: Zhi Li, Minghui Li, Daiyong Du
publish: 2026 15th International Conference on Educational and Information Technology (ICEIT)
abstract: Aiming at the problems in the WPS practical training course of university computer science, such as significant differences in students’ basic foundations, low adaptability of teaching resources, and a single evaluation system, this study constructs a hierarchical knowledge graph for WPS covering the basic operation layer, comprehensive application layer, and innovative expansion layer, along with an inter-component associated knowledge graph. On this basis, a personalized teaching strategy covering the entire process of pre-class, in-class, and post-class is designed. This strategy effectively adapts to individual differences among students, improves teaching efficiency and students’ comprehensive application abilities, and provides a referable path for the personalized teaching reform of basic computer courses.

title: Real-Time Diagnosis and Intervention of Teaching Behaviors Driven by Machine Learning in New-Engineering Classrooms
authors: Wei Guo, Zeyu Yan
publish: 2026 15th International Conference on Educational and Information Technology (ICEIT)
abstract: China’s New Engineering programming courses lose one fifth of first year students because instructors cannot spot the seven-minute silent confusion cycle that unfolds while they face the IDE. We trained an edge compute system on thirty thousand labelled frames extracted from one hundred twenty hours of video recorded in ten courses with two hundred eighty four students. A pruned YOLOv8 Transformer ensemble running on a four gigabyte Jetson Nano detects eight micro behaviors such as keyboard silence head pose drift and IDE error bursts at twenty five frames per second within forty milliseconds. When the posterior probability of confusion or distraction exceeds nought point seven for three consecutive frames the board flashes a three level cue on the instructor s secondary monitor and pushes a four line AST matched code hint into each student s IDE. An A B A experiment with one hundred eight novices showed immediate gains. Eye movement entropy fell from three point nine zero to three point one eight bits and quiz accuracy rose from seventy two point seven per cent to eighty four point six per cent. Four weeks after withdrawal retention remained seven point three percentage points above baseline. The bill of materials is two hundred seventeen euros per classroom. The dataset and Jetson image are released under MIT License for three hundred room scale up.

title: Implement Edge and Cloud Computing for Distributed Healthcare Data Processing: Handling Medical Data in Real-Time
authors: Pr Buvaneswari, Mohammad Rasmi Al-Mousa, Kolisetti Giridhara Sravani, Rajeev Sobti, K. Murugesan, T Ranjitha Devi
abstract: Storage, processing, and real-time analytics are becoming increasingly challenging due to the exponential growth of healthcare data generated by wearable devices, EHRs, medical imaging systems, and remote patient monitoring. Concerns of privacy, bandwidth, and latency arise when dealing with time-sensitive medical data stored in centralized cloud-based systems. An integrated Edge-Cloud computing architecture for distributed healthcare data processing is presented in this paper for safe, efficient, and real-time medical analytics. An Internet of Things (IoT) medical device network located close to healthcare facilities is the basis of the proposed solution. In order to decrease latency and alleviate network congestion, edge nodes perform filtering, preprocessing, anomaly identification, and rapid response generation. Important health warnings, such as aberrant vital signs, can be processed locally, which helps with therapeutic intervention. Storage on a grand scale, analytics, training models, and managing a population’s health are all made possible with cloud computing. By allocating workloads adaptively, we can keep computations on the edge and in the cloud in balance. Lightweight edge-based machine learning models and computationally intensive cloud-based deep learning algorithms both provide real-time decision support. The transfer and storage of patient data is protected via communication protocols and encryption. Distributed systems rely on data synchronization and orchestration modules to keep them reliable. When compared to cloud-only systems, Edge-Cloud hybrids outperform them in terms of response latency reduction, bandwidth optimization, and scalability. Healthcare monitoring may be maintained while fault tolerance and robustness are enhanced by distributed design.

title: Research on Multi-Dimensional Learning Behavior Modeling and Grade Prediction Based on Transformer
authors: Yan Zhou, Jun Zhou, Xin Gao
publish: 2026 15th International Conference on Educational and Information Technology (ICEIT)
abstract: Traditional teaching evaluation methods, which primarily rely on final examination scores, struggle to comprehensively capture students' multi-dimensional competency development in blended learning environments. To address this limitation, this paper proposes a teaching evaluation prediction system based on the Transformer model. The system integrates student data across five dimensions: online learning behavior, classroom participation, assignment scores, project collaboration, and final examination grades. By employing a multi-head self-attention mechanism to model the nonlinear dependencies among these features, the system achieves accurate prediction of students' academic performance. Experiments were conducted using real learning data from 100 students, partitioned into training, validation, and test sets at a ratio of 7:1.5:1.5. Results demonstrate that the model achieves a prediction accuracy of 88% on the test set, with a mean squared error of 0.022. The correlation coefficients for both training and test sets approach 1, validating the model's strong fitting capability and generalization performance. Furthermore, the system provides teachers with real-time feedback on student learning progress and supports personalized teaching decision-making, facilitating a shift from outcome-oriented to process-oriented teaching evaluation. This research demonstrates that the Transformer-based evaluation prediction model can effectively enhance the precision of teaching assessment and provide data-driven technical support for personalized learning interventions in blended teaching environments.

title: Research on Blade Design of Small Vertical Axis Wind Turbine
authors: Zhicheng Li, Zuoxia Xing, Shanshan Guo
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Advances in Electrical, Electronics and Computing Technology (EECT)
abstract: Small vertical axis wind turbines (VAWTs) are crucial for distributed energy but struggle to balance the “efficiency-cost-stability” triangle. This study designs a highperformance, low-cost H-type VAWT blade system by integrating aerodynamics, structural mechanics, and materials science. It develops an $\mathbf{S 1 2 1 0}$ high lift-to-drag ratio airfoil blade with a linear taper chord distribution (root: $\mathbf{7 0 m m}$, tip: $\mathbf{4 0 m m}$) and a composite structure of T700 carbon fiber skin and 6061-T6 aluminum alloy spar. Through topology optimization and finite element simulation, the blade achieves a mass of 4.2 kg per piece while withstanding a maximum stress of $\mathbf{1 2 8 ~ M P a}$ under a $\mathbf{1 3. 8 ~ m} / \mathbf{s}$ wind load, which is less than $\mathbf{5 0 \%}$ of the material’s yield strength. Multi-physics field simulations (including fluid-structure interaction) for blade angles of $\mathbf{4 5}^{\circ}, \mathbf{7 5}^{\circ}$, and $\mathbf{9 0}^{\circ}$ confirm that the $\mathbf{4 5}^{\circ}$ configuration is optimal, delivering a power coefficient (Cp) of 0.48, reducing the flow separation zone to 85% of its original size, and minimizing tip deformation to 0.8 mm. The blade’s first-order natural frequency is 4.8 Hz, effectively avoiding the primary wind load frequency band $(4-6 \mathrm{~Hz})$ to prevent resonance. This blade design, characterized by lightweight construction, high strength, and superior aerodynamic performance, provides a reliable core component for applying small VAWTs in distributed wind-solar hybrid systems. [1]

title: DNA-Inspired Helical Microstrip Antenna for Compact Low-GHz Wireless Applications
authors: Silva Deena, Priyanka R, Keerthana S, Mohan C
publish: 2026 Third International Conference on Networking and Communications (ICNWC)
abstract: This paper presents a planar DNA-inspired helical microstrip antenna that can operate in millimeter-wave frequencies for compact, high-performance wireless applications. The proposed configuration uses a 2D helical strip patterned over a single-layer substrate to form a twisted current path that can effectively increase the electrical length while maintaining a reduced physical footprint. The antenna structure integrates a periodic zig-zag helix that further improves the inductive-capacitive loading and can provide better current distribution along the radiating surface. Electromagnetic modeling and analysis were performed using CST Studio Suite 2025, where the antenna yielded a strong resonance at 25.7 GHz with a measured return loss of approximately -26 dB at the design frequency, reflecting excellent impedance matching. Far-field radiation analysis revealed a peak gain of 9.63 dBi in the φ = 0° plane, as well as stable main lobe formation with acceptable sidelobe levels. Visualization of surface currents further confirms the efficient excitation of the helical strip and uniform flow of power along the meandered DNA-inspired pathway. In view of its planar profile, structural simplicity, and strong radiative performance, the proposed antenna is a good candidate for compact millimeter-wave modules, short-range high-density wireless links, biomedical telemetry nodes, and next-generation integrated communication platforms.

title: Artificial Intelligence: Legal Applications of Artificial Intelligence
authors: Damien Charlotin
abstract: None

title: Attenuating Carryover Effects in Rapid Cycle Gas Sensing via Active Purging and Optimized Chamber Design
authors: Abdulnasser Nabil Abdullah, Kamarulzaman Kamarudin, Md Ashequl Islam, Syed Muhammad Mamduh Syed Zakaria, Zaffry Hadi Mohd Juffry
publish: 2026 22nd IEEE International Colloquium on Signal Processing &amp; Its Applications (CSPA)
abstract: Confined spaces are susceptible to oxygen deficiency and the accumulation of toxic or flammable gases due to limited ventilation, posing significant risks to workers and emergency responders. However, gas sensor modules operating in such environments often experience residual gas retention within the sensing chamber, which leads to delayed recovery, baseline drift, and reduced measurement reliability. This study presents the design and experimental validation of an enhanced purging technique for a gas sensor array system intended for confined-space hazard detection. The proposed system integrates an optimized sensor chamber, a multi-sensor metaloxide (MOX) sensor array, and a controlled air-purging mechanism to mitigate gas carryover and stabilize sensor responses. System performance was evaluated under two purging conditions: ambient air purging and purified air purging using an active carbon filter. Sensor response data were analysed using average response comparison and multivariate statistical analysis based on Principal Component Analysis (PCA). The PCA results demonstrate strong gas discrimination capability, with the first two principal components explaining $\mathbf{9 2. 1 6 \%}$ of the total variance. Experimental results show that the carbon-filtered purging method significantly improves baseline recovery, response repeatability, and measurement stability compared with conventional ambient purging. The proposed approach improves gas sensing stability and baseline recovery under the tested laboratory conditions and provides a practical basis for further development of confined-space atmospheric monitoring systems.

title: Field-of-View–Based Spherical Video Stitching and Object Detection for Multi-Camera Drones
authors: Ghassan Al Jayyousi, Ghaith Al-Refai, Mutaz Ryalat, Hisham Elmoaqet
publish: 2026 1st International Conference on Emerging Technologies and Engineering Systems (ICETES)
abstract: This work introduces a panoramic video stitching framework for multi-camera drones aimed at improving situational awareness and object detection in aerial monitoring applications. The system works with three video streams that were recorded at the same time from wide-angle cameras on the left, back, and right. To fix the distortion caused by the 160° lenses, each frame is first geometrically corrected using a field-of-view-based spherical warping model. We use ORB keypoints and RANSAC-based homography estimation to do feature-based alignment. There is also an interactive parameter tuning interface that lets you change the settings for the detector and alignment in real time. After that, a two-stage multiband Laplacian pyramid blending method is used to make a 270° stitched panorama that looks perfect. After that, the stitched frames are processed with a YOLOv5 object detection model to find objects in the larger field of view. Experiments show that the suggested framework works better for aerial perception tasks than single-camera detection. It also maintains the computational efficiency needed for onboard processing.

title: Research on the Application of Russian Translation Based on Mel Spectrogram and Phonetic Translation in Russian Cultural Communication
authors: Lu An, Zhu Feng
publish: 2025 Asian Conference on Artificial Intelligence Technology (ACAIT)
abstract: Aiming at the accuracy, efficiency and quality issues of Russian translation in cross-cultural communication, this study uses Mel spectrograms to extract and represent speech features, builds a translation model through a bidirectional long short-term memory network, and introduces an attention mechanism to improve it. A Russian translation model based on Mel spectrogram and speech translation is designed. The results show that in the LibriSpeech dataset, the average quality evaluation accuracy of the proposed model is 88.6%, and in the MUST-C dataset, the average quality evaluation accuracy of the proposed model is 85.5%, which is higher than the average quality accuracy of other models in the two datasets, proving that its translation quality is high. The Russian translation model proposed by the institute has superior performance and can effectively promote communication and integration between different cultures.

title: RAG-Induced Failures in Multi-Agent Large Language Model Debate
authors: Insaf Kraidia, Iyas Qaddara, Nida Al-Shafi, Mohammad O. Hiari, Rania Alzubaidi, Amira Benlecheb
publish: 2026 1st International Conference on Emerging Technologies and Engineering Systems (ICETES)
abstract: Multi-agent Large Language Model (LLM) systems based on collaborative debate have been proposed to improve reasoning accuracy and robustness by aggregating diverse reasoning trajectories. However, most debate frameworks implicitly assume that all participating agents behave honestly. This paper demonstrates that this assumption fails under a realistic threat model in which a single malicious agent manipulates Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to selectively retrieve externally sourced evidence that plausibly supports an incorrect conclusion. Unlike prior work that relies on prompt injection or complex persuasion pipelines, the proposed attack requires only retrieval control and uses legitimate documents whose relevance is strategically overstated or misinterpreted. Experiments on TruthfulQA, MMLU, MedMCQA, and SCALR show that RAG-based attacks can reduce majority-vote accuracy by up to 38% while increasing group consensus around false answers. These results indicate that retrieval-often used to improve factual grounding-can instead amplify misinformation when adversarially controlled, motivating stronger verification and trust calibration in multi-agent LLM systems.

title: Instantaneous Spectra Analysis of Pulse Series - Application to Lung Sounds with Abnormalities
authors: Fumihiko Ishiyama
publish: 2026 22nd IEEE International Colloquium on Signal Processing &amp; Its Applications (CSPA)
abstract: The origin of the “theoretical limit of timefrequency resolution of Fourier analysis” is from its numerical implementation, especially from an assumption of “Periodic Boundary Condition (PBC),” which was introduced a century ago. We previously proposed to replace this condition with ‘Linear eXtrapolation Condition (LXC),” which does not require periodicity. This feature makes instantaneous spectra analysis of pulse series available, which replaces the short time Fourier transform (STFT). We applied the instantaneous spectra analysis to two lung sounds with abnormalities (crackles and wheezing) and to a normal lung sound, as a demonstration. Among them, crackles contains a random pulse series. The spectrum of each pulse is available, and the spectrogram of pulse series is available with assembling each spectrum. As a result, the time-frequency structure of given pulse series is visualized.

title: Foundation Model Enhanced Hierarchical Learning for White Blood Cell Classification
authors: Fan Xiao, Zirui Chen, Jilan Xu, Junlin Hou
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: White blood cell (WBC) classification is essential for diagnosing hematological disorders, but automated methods face challenges from data heterogeneity and class imbalance. In this work, we propose a hierarchical deep learning framework for 13-class WBC classification. To address domain shift from various imaging conditions, we fine-tune foundation models and apply pseudo-labeling on unlabeled test samples. For severe class imbalance, we introduce a specialized pipeline combining binary voting and exemplar matching to improve rare subtype recognition. Our method achieves a macro F1 score of 0.77682 on the WBCBench 2026 dataset, demonstrating its effectiveness on heterogeneous data and clinically significant minority classes.

title: A Data-driven Loss Model of GaN-HEMT-based Synchronous Buck Converter with Critical Operating Frequency
authors: Subhradip Mukherjee, Mahesh K. Mishra, N. Lakshminarasamma
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Power Electronics, Smart Grid, and Renewable Energy (PESGRE)
abstract: Wide bandgap (WBG) semiconductor devices such as gallium nitride (GaN) high electron mobility transistors (HEMTs) have significantly advanced power electronics by enabling higher switching frequencies, compact form factors, greater power density, and improved efficiency. Despite these advantages, accurately modeling losses in GaN-based converters remains a key challenge, especially under varying operating conditions. This paper presents a data-driven loss modeling approach for a GaN HEMT-based buck converter, combining empirical simulation data with theoretical analysis to estimate both fixed and variable losses across a range of switching frequencies and load levels. Curve-fitting techniques are employed to develop analytical loss functions for key parameters, enabling precise prediction of total power loss. The model also facilitates analysis of the trade-offs among switching frequency, efficiency, and thermal performance. Based on this analysis, an optimal switching frequency is identified that minimizes total power loss while maintaining thermal and operational reliability.

title: Fine-Tuning CLIP Models with Convolutional Features for Medical Anomaly Detection
authors: Jianfeng Fu, Minglong Xue
publish: 2025 Asian Conference on Artificial Intelligence Technology (ACAIT)
abstract: Despite the significant success of large-scale vision-language pre-training models in zero-shot and few-shot anomaly detection for natural images, their effectiveness is sharply limited in the medical domain by the substantial discrepancy between natural and medical image data. To address this domain shift, This study presents a new, lightweight framework named Multi-Level Convolution Adaptation (MLCA), which is designed for medical anomaly detection by fine-tune the CLIP model. Our methodology enhances the pre-trained visual encoder by integrating multiple residual adapters to progressively refine visual features across various layers. Furthermore, we implement a ConvTokenizer to align CLIP’s image tokens directly with the raw input information, thereby preserving critical original details. This adaptation process is rigorously supervised by a pixel-level visual-text alignment loss function, which deliberately recalibrates the model’s attention away from recognizing object semantics towards the accurate recognition of anomalies in medical images. The resulting adapted features exhibit markedly enhanced generalization across diverse medical data types, yielding promising zero-shot capabilities even when the model engagement sentirely novel medical modalities or underexplored anatomical regions during inference. Experimental validation on standard MAD benchmarks confirms that MLCA surpasses existing SOTA models, showing the average value of AUC improvement of 2.44% and 1.18% for anomaly classification under zero-shot and few-shot conditions, respectively, and corresponding average AUC gains of 1.33% and 0.57% for anomaly segmentation.

title: Hierarchical Multi-Scale Graph Learning with Knowledge-Guided Attention for Whole-Slide Image Survival Analysis
authors: Bin Xu, Yufei Zhou, Bolin Song, Jingwen Sun, Yang Bian, Yikai Chen, Cheng Lu, Ye Wu, Jianfei Tu, Xiangxue Wang
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: We propose a Hierarchical Multi-scale Knowledge-aware Graph Network (HMKGN) that models multi-scale interactions and spatially hierarchical relationships within whole-slide images (WSIs) for cancer prognostication. Unlike conventional attention-based MIL, which ignores spatial organization, or graph-based MIL, which relies on static handcrafted graphs, HMKGN enforces a hierarchical structure with spatial locality constraints, wherein local cellular-level dynamic graphs aggregate spatially proximate patches within each region of interest (ROI) and a global slide-level dynamic graph integrates ROI-level features into WSI-level representations. Moreover, multi-scale integration at the ROI level combines coarse contextual features from broader views with fine-grained structural representations from local patch-graph aggregation. We evaluate HMKGN on four TCGA cohorts (KIRC, LGG, PAAD, and STAD; <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{N}=513$</tex>, 487, 138, and 370) for survival prediction. It consistently outperforms existing MIL-based models, yielding improved concordance indices (10.85% better) and statistically significant stratification of patient survival risk (log-rank <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$p<0.05$</tex>).

title: Dual-Wavelength SiN-Waveguide Coupled High-Speed Ge Photodiodes in a Photonic BiCMOS Process
authors: S. Lischke, R. M. Pattery, C. Mai, S. Marschmeyer, A. Peczek, A. Kroh, L. Zimmermann
abstract: We demonstrate directly silicon-nitride waveguide coupled high-speed germanium photodiodes for first-window <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(850 ~\text{nm})$</tex> and O-band applications, that are monolithically integrated in a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.25 \mu ~\mathrm{m}$</tex> photonic BiCMOS platform offering SiGeHBTs with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{f}_{\mathbf{T}} / \mathbf{f}_{\text {max }}$</tex> of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{2 3 0} / \mathbf{2 8 0 ~ G H Z}$</tex>.

title: Arabic Economic Text News Summarization: An AI-driven Extractive Approach
authors: Iyas Qaddara, Insaf Kraidia, Mohammad O. Hiari, Nida Al-Shafi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Multidomain Integrated Learning Environments (ICSMILE)
abstract: This article discusses AI-extractive summarization of Arabic economic news based on the use of massive data sets obtained using two major Arabic news outlets, Al Jazeera and Al Arabiya. In the paper under consideration, the problem of summarizing the Arabic economic text is discussed, its peculiarities include a large volume of numerical information, named entities, and specific terminology in the field. The suggested system uses the feature extraction and cosine similarity sentence scoring pipeline with TF and IDF as its foundation to produce extractive summaries. ROUGE-1 and ROUGE-2 metrics were used to analyze the model and estimate precision, recall, and $\mathbf{F 1}$-score. Rough estimates indicated that ROUGE-1 influenced better performance than ROUGE-2 in both datasets, and the average precision of the results was about 0.82, the average recall was about 0.74, and the average F1-score was about 0.78 in the case of Al Jazeera, and was slightly better in the case of Al Arabiya (meaning Al Arabiya had a higher average precision of 0.84 and a higher average recall of 0.75). The results emphasize the usefulness of extractive summarization of Arabic news, particularly in shorter and more factual content, along with the potential of AI-based extractive summarization to improve the accessibility of news and assist with financial analysis, and also reveal the future opportunities to integrate abstractive neural network models with domainconscious embedding.

title: Dual-Domain Hierarchical Adversarial Learning for Semi-Supervised Multi-View Fetal Cardiac Ultrasound Segmentation and Disease Classification
authors: Kerong Zhou
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Accurate prenatal screening for congenital heart disease (CHD) relies on fetal echocardiography, yet clinical interpretation is heavily operator-dependent and hampered by the scarcity of high-quality annotated multi-view data. To address these challenges, we propose a Dual-Domain Hierarchical Adversarial Learning (DD-HAL) framework for the 2026 ISBI Fetal HearT UltraSound Challenge. DD-HAL incorporates three core designs: a multi-view-shared and view-specific multi-branch encoder-decoder architecture, a hierarchical adversarial domain adaptation module, and a Mean-Teacher semi-supervised framework with consistency regularization, which simultaneously addresses multi-view heterogeneity, label scarcity, and domain shift. Our codes will be publicly available at https://github.com/SnapeLocke/DDHAL.

title: Towards Fully Integrated Supercontinuum Sources in Silicon Photonics Platform
authors: Yijun Yang, Maria-Camila Diaz-Sanchez, Ilyes Betka, Etienne Herth, David Bouville, Samson Edmond, Jean-René Coudevylle, Daniele Melati, Delphine Marris-Morini, Quentin Wilmart, Carlos Alonso-Ramos, Benjamin Wetzel, Arnaud Mussot, Eric Cassan, Laurent Vivien
abstract: Supercontinuum generation (SCG) is essential for a wide range of applications, including metrology, imaging, spectroscopy, and optical communications. Although broad and efficient SCG has been demonstrated in integrated photonic platforms, most implementations rely on high-power and bulky pulsed laser sources. This paper reports recent advances toward fully integrated SCG sources including advances in on-chip SCG and pulse compression.

title: Physics-Infused Neural MPC of a DC-DC Boost Converter with Adaptive Transient Recovery and Enhanced Dynamic Stability
authors: Tahmin Mahmud
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Intelligent Design and Control of Automation and Drive Systems (IDCD)
abstract: DC-DC boost converters require advanced control to ensure efficiency and stability under varying loads. Traditional model predictive control (MPC) and data-driven neural network methods face challenges such as high complexity and limited physical constraint enforcement. This paper proposes a hybrid physics-informed neural network (PINN) combined with finite control set MPC (FCS-MPC) for boost converters. The PINN embeds physical laws into neural training, providing accurate state predictions, while FCS-MPC ensures constraint satisfaction and multi-objective optimization. The method features adaptive transient recovery, explicit duty-ratio control, and enhanced dynamic stability. Experimental results on a commercial boost module demonstrate improved transient response, reduced voltage ripple, and robust operation across conduction modes. The proposed framework offers a computationally efficient, physically consistent solution for real-time control in power electronics.

title: Assessing Privacy Risks in Android Applications Using ML and Explainable AI Techniques
authors: Abeeha Hasan, Sehba Masood
publish: 2026 Second International Conference on Emerging Computational Intelligence (ICECI)
abstract: Android applications frequently request many permissions, making privacy risks difficult to measure. The study presents a machine learning framework to assess such risks. It generates a single privacy risk score by combining static features from app manifests and metadata with SHAP-based explainable AI techniques. The process includes: (i) collecting the dataset and cleaning it, (ii) feature engineering from permissions and contextual metadata, (iii) training and evaluating models using multiple algorithms, (iv) SHAP-based explainability, and (v) calculating a weighted RiskScore. 9,872 Android apps were used to carry-out the experiments, which shows that ensemble models like Random Forest and XGBoost can be accurate up to 88.9% and have a macro-F1 score of 0.89, which is 8 10 % better than linear and single tree baselines. SHAP analysis found important permissions like (ACCESS_FINE_LOCATION, READ_CONTACTS, RECORD_AUDIO) as dominant privacy-risk indicators. The proposed method provide a reproducible and interpretable foundation for assessing over-permissioned Android apps and can be used for privacy auditing and policy enforcement.

title: EPSQA: Retrieval-Augmented Generation with Structured Knowledge for Electric Power Safety Question Answering
authors: Xiang Yuan, Chutian Yu, Lijun Zhang, Junjie Sun, Qingxin Yan, Rui Pan, Xiao Yang
publish: 2025 Asian Conference on Artificial Intelligence Technology (ACAIT)
abstract: Electric power safety question answering demands both precision and domain expertise, yet existing systems often rely on unstructured documents that are difficult to maintain and prone to retrieval inconsistencies. We present EPSQA, a domain-specific Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) framework that uniquely leverages structured electric power safety question banks curated by experts. EPSQA incorporates large language model–based keyword extraction and SQL-driven database retrieval to align query intent with relevant domain knowledge. By introducing adaptive matching strategies, EPSQA enables fine-grained control over retrieval precision and coverage. Evaluated on a real-world electric power safety dataset, EPSQA achieves a correct answer rate of approximately 57.44%, representing a relative improvement of about 72% over the unstructured baseline.

title: Hyperspectral Band Selection via Spectral-Spatial Embedding and Graph Regularization
authors: Mingqing Liu, Jingyu Wang, Shengzhao Guo, Xinru Zhang, Pengfei Huang, Zhenyu Ma
publish: 2025 Asian Conference on Artificial Intelligence Technology (ACAIT)
abstract: Band selection is an important technique to extract crucial features and reduce redundancy in hyperspectral images (HSI). Hyperspectral images inherently exhibit correlations in both spatial and spectral domains. However, most existing band selection methods fail to jointly leverage these complementary characteristics for enhanced feature representation. Hence, in this work, we propose a novel Spectral-Spatial Embedding and Graph Regularization (SSEGR) band selection method. First, a spectral-spatial embedding module is introduced to improve the ability to simultaneously explore spectral and spatial correlations of self-representation learning, from which a sparse coefficient matrix can be obtained. Then, to maintain the structure smoothness of the learned coefficient matrix, a graph regularization term is employed to preserve the intrinsic similarity relationships within HSI data. The experiments on benchmark HSI datasets demonstrate the feasibility of our proposed SSEGR method.

title: Dispersion Engineered On-Chip Silicon Nitride Fabry-Perot Resonators
authors: Z. Saleh, F. Rinaldo Talenti, R. Prosopio, Y. Yang, S. Edmond, E. Cassan, D. Melati, D. Marris-Morini, L. Vivien, C. Alonso-Ramos
abstract: On-chip dispersion engineering is instrumental for nonlinear applications in communications, sensing, and quantum technologies. We demonstrate dispersion control in silicon nitride Fabry-Perot resonators with distributed Bragg reflectors. We experimentally show our approach enables switching between normal and anomalous dispersion regimes, independent of intracavity waveguide dispersion.

title: Joint-Specialist Learning with Endoscopic Descriptor Guidance for Ulcerative Colitis Grading
authors: Sakib Ahammed, Robert Chandler, Moi Hoon Yap, Xinqi Fan
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Accurate endoscopic grading of ulcerative colitis (UC) is essential for disease monitoring and treatment planning. Most existing approaches focus on predicting either the Mayo endoscopic score (MES) or ulcerative colitis endoscopic index of severity (UCEIS) independently, without modeling their clinical relationship. In the work, we propose a multi-task joint-specialist learning framework with endoscopic descriptor guidance (JSL-EDG) for comprehensive UC grading. Our method employs a shared encoder to jointly learn representations for both MES and UCEIS, while two dedicated specialist models further enhance specific scoring for each task. We present an endoscopic descriptor loss that supervises metadata to strengthen clinically meaningful learning for UCEIS. Additionally, we introduce a descriptor-UCEIS consistency loss to align the predicted UCEIS scores with metadata, using severity combinations derived from vascular patterns, bleeding, and erosion/ulcer. At inference, we incorporate a regularization mechanism that enforces clinically plausible severity transitions via a MES-UCEIS relationship based on the empirical conditional distribution. Experiments on the EndoUC challenge leaderboard demonstrate that the proposed method achieves state-of-the-art performance for MES and competitive results for the more challenging finegrained UCEIS grading task.

title: Fuzzy Logic Control for Coordinated FES-Assisted Knee Swing with Powered Knee Orthoses
authors: Harith Firdaus Mustapha, Zakaria Hussain, Emilia Noorsal, Mohd Suhaimi Sulaiman
publish: 2026 22nd IEEE International Colloquium on Signal Processing &amp; Its Applications (CSPA)
abstract: Rehabilitation for lower limb mobility often relies on Functional Electrical Stimulation (FES) to get paralyzed or weak muscles firing again. But there is a massive drawback. Stimulated muscles fatigue incredibly fast. Plus, figuring out exactly how much electrical stimulation a muscle needs at any given second is notoriously difficult because the human neuromuscular system is highly non-linear and unpredictable. Integrated the exact hardware dynamics of a Faulhaber 3284 brushed DC motor. This meant factoring in real-world constraints like armature resistance, rotor inertia, and specific torque constants to see how the robotic joints would actually behave under a physical load.

title: Processing-in-Memory Architecture for In-Storage Information Retrieval on 3-D NAND Flash Solid-State Drives
authors: Huiwon Yun, Inho Jeong, Kyoyun Lee, Jaeyong Lee, Myoungjun Chun, Jihong Kim, Dongsuk Jeon
publish: IEEE Transactions on Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI) Systems
abstract: Recent advances in retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) highlight retrieval latency, dominated by storage access, and large embedding-index size as the major bottlenecks. Although processing-in-memory (PIM) based on 3-D <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">nand</small> flash and binary passage retriever (BPR) algorithm offer potential solutions for reducing latency and memory usage, naively integrating BPR algorithm into an SSD based on prior in-memory search (IMS) architectures results in negligible performance gain with substantial hardware overhead. This stems from the fact that prior IMS architectures are optimized only for search, leaving candidate readout misaligned with the <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">nand</small> page direction and requiring excessive readcycles, while BPR's original top-<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$L$</tex-math> </inline-formula> selection further requires high-precision ADCs and sorting logic. To address these limitations, we propose an algorithm–hardware co-designed in-storage information retrieval architecture. We introduce a hardware-efficient optimized BPR algorithm with a threshold-based selection and two-stage in-storage processing approach to minimize data transfer overheads. We further present an IMS architecture with page-aligned encoding that efficiently supports both search and candidate read operations, eliminating the read cycle penalty. We also present a lightweight in situ error monitoring scheme that bypasses error-correcting code (ECC) decoding while preserving error-aware refresh triggering. Experiments show that our design reduces the amount of data read from <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">nand</small> flash by 7.94–<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$1330.40\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula> compared with host-side processing baselines and further reduces controller-to-host data transfer, while maintaining the retrieval accuracy. It also reduces total operationcycles by up to 96.93% compared with prior IMS architectures, and lowers error-detection power by 94.71% and total read energy by 17.22% compared with a conventional ECC decoder for error monitoring.

title: Recovering Reward Functions From Distributed Expert Demonstrations via Bi-Level Maximum-Likelihood Optimization_supp1-3688600.pdf
authors: Tian Lan
abstract: Inverse reinforcement learning (IRL) seeks to infer the latent reward function and the associated optimal policy from expert demonstrations. However, most current IRL methods assume centralized access to all trajectory data, which is impractical in real-world scenarios characterized by decentralized data sources and privacy concerns. To this end, this article proposes a novel algorithm for federated maximum-likelihood IRL (F-ML-IRL) and provides a rigorous analysis of its convergence rate. The proposed F-ML-IRL leverages dual aggregation to update the shared global model and performs bi-level local updates: an upper level learning task to optimize the parameterized reward function by maximizing the discounted likelihood of observing human expert trajectories under the current policy, and a lower level learning task to find the optimal agent policy regarding the entropy-regularized discounted cumulative reward under the current reward function. We analyze the convergence rate of the proposed F-ML-IRL algorithm and show that the global model in F-ML-IRL converges to a stationary point for both the reward and policy parameters within finite time. That is, the log-distance between the recovered policy and the optimal policy, as well as the gradient of the likelihood objective, converges to zero. Evaluating our F-ML-IRL algorithm on high-dimensional robotic control tasks in MuJoCo, we show that it ensures convergence of the recovered reward in decentralized learning and outperforms centralized baselines due to its ability to utilize distributed data—attaining better recovered rewards than all baselines in 12 out of 20 tasks.

title: Enhancing Different Hybrid Energy Systems Management and Their Optimisation Using AI Technology
authors: Ahmad Habboush, Bassam Elzaghmouri, Hussein Ahmad Al-Ofeishat, Takialddin Al Smadi
publish: 2026 1st International Conference on Emerging Technologies and Engineering Systems (ICETES)
abstract: Hybrid energy systems (HES) are necessary to make the energy supply less carbon dioxide-emitting, more flexible and cheaper. You know what? They use traditional backup and storage technologies, as well as renewable sources such as solar, wind, and biomass. This review covers the state of the art in HES optimisation, including engineering architectures, component technologies, and new approaches such as mathematical programming, metaheuristics, and surrogate models. Guess what? In agriculture, there is a strong emphasis on high-impact uses such as freezing, storage, and drying. This is because predictable heat loads facilitate the use of thermal energy storage and demand-shifting strategies to maximise the use of renewable energy. Essential key technical and economic metrics, such as Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE), Life Cycle Cost (LCC), and Net Present Value (NPV) are used to assess a project's performance. We also use environmental metrics based on Life Cycle Assessment (LCA). The study supports the technical and economic simulation of a biomass-solar-biomass hybrid cold storage facility in Jordan. This substantial amount of money, combined with reduced post-harvest losses, enables fuel consumption to be reduced. Finally, joint optimisation across multiple time scales, asset degradation modelling, and the need for standardized datasets and bankable business models are important research areas that warrant further work. This results in a targeted agenda for future research and policy.

title: Explainable Artificial Intelligence for Workforce Decision-Making in Enterprise Systems
authors: Monu Sharma
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Multidomain Integrated Learning Environments (ICSMILE)
abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) is now becoming a more popular option with regards to decision making related to workforce, such as talent acquisition, workforce employee retention prediction, performance assessment, and workforce planning. Nevertheless, the intensive usage of black-box models has triggered alarm among the stakeholders of the enterprise in the areas of transparency, trust, fairness, and compliance with regulations. This paper will discuss how Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) can be used to overcome these problems in workforce prediction systems. A explainable machine learning system is built based on SHAP and local interpretability methods to offer significant results about the real-world workforce information, both on the global and features levels. According to the experimental findings, explainability is a key factor that enhances the level of expert trust in model outputs; in fact, the level of trust went up by more than 35 percent in contrast to non-explainable methods. Moreover, the suggested framework makes it possible to conduct successful human-AI interaction with the domain experts being able to examine and verify predictive results, with explainability being the key to responsible enterprise AI implementation.

title: SFTD-Net: Structure-Aware Frequency and Sparse Attention Network for Retinal Vessel Segmentation
authors: Tianqi Huang
publish: 2026 International Conference on Vision, Advanced Imaging and Computer Technology (VAICT)
abstract: Retinal vessel segmentation plays a crucial role in computer-aided diagnosis for ophthalmic and cardiovascular diseases. However, accurate segmentation remains challenging due to complex backgrounds, low contrast, and large vessel-scale variations. In addition, conventional encoder-decoder networks are limited in local feature modeling, skip-feature fusion, and detail preservation during reconstruction. To tackle these issues, we present a structure-aware frequency and sparse attention network (SFTD-Net) for retinal vessel segmentation. SFTD-Net follows an encoder-decoder architecture with three modules. A PConv-Conv Attention Module (PCAM) is inserted in the encoder to strengthen multi-scale feature representation. An In-dex-Selected Sparse Attention module (ISSA) is integrated into the skip pathways to selectively transmit encoder features and suppress redundant background noise. A Dual-Branch Refinement Module (DBRM) is introduced before decoder upsampling and fusion to restore structural details and suppress texture interference. Results on the Digital Retinal Images for Vessel Extraction (DRIVE) and Child Heart and Health Study in England database 1 (CHASE_DB1) datasets further demonstrate the robustness of SFTD-Net in retinal vessel segmentation. On DRIVE, the proposed method achieves an accuracy (ACC) of 0.9698, an F1-score (F1) of 0.8236, and an area under the ROC curve (AUC) of 0.9863. It also obtains competitive performance on CHASE_DB1. These results highlight the potential of SFTD-Net for computer vision, advanced medical imaging, and intelligent diagnosis tasks

title: Cold-Start Recommendation via Hybrid Matrix Factorization and Cluster-Based Association Rules
authors: Sumitha T, Aswin R, Lakshmanan V, Krishna Charan K V S
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Research Methodologies in Knowledge Management, Artificial Intelligence and Telecommunication Engineering (RMKMATE)
abstract: As we get accustomed to recommender systems, one issue that thrived since time immemorial is the cold-start issue. Lack of demanding interaction data for collaborative filtering while confronting new users, is found to be the cause of this problem. Although CFCAI aids with sparsity handling, it turns a blind eye to the latent preferences. Our research proves to be fruitful in solving this issue by using a hybrid approach that blends Matrix Factorization with Cluster-based association rules and content features. We have a combination of K-Means clustering, Association rule Mining, Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) and Ridge Regression resulting in efficient recommendations even when the user history is scarce. In cold-start conditions (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$k=1$</tex> seed item per user), our results have a Precision@10 of 19.3% and Recall@10 of 4.5 %, which significantly outperforms the existing one, which is conducive to our intriguing idea of blending rulebased and latent factor methods. Its utilization can be multi-fold, ranging from supporting larger datasets to efficiently aligning with neural models and production systems.

title: Hybrid-Projection with Subspace Recycling for Sequential and Limited-Angle CT: A Training-Free Baseline for Biomedical Imaging
authors: Honghui Li
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Modern CT workflows in biomedical imaging increasingly involve sequences of closely related reconstruction tasks: repeated exams, multi-slice stacks, dynamic series, and geometry-constrained limited-angle acquisitions. While deep learning and unrolled variational networks have shown strong performance for sparse-view and low-dose CT, they rely on training data, protocol-specific tuning, and careful safeguards for robustness, which motivates fast, interpretable, training-free baselines that exploit sequential structure. We study hybrid-projection-based reconstruction with subspace recycling for 2D parallel-beam CT. Building on existing HyBR/HyBRrecycle solvers, we reuse Krylov subspaces across systems with shared geometry and couple this with practical parameterchoice rules: discrepancy principles with known or estimated noise, and stabilized weighted GCV. Using a unified testbed with standard phantoms, full-view and missing-angle geometries, and additive noise, we (i) instantiate a CT-oriented hybrid projection and recycling framework (HyBR-Rec) tailored to sequential and limitedangle settings, and (ii) systematically compare it against filtered backprojection (FBP) and a TV-regularized baseline. Experiments on representative sequential and limited-angle scenarios show that subspace recycling consistently accelerates convergence: in our tests, time-to-best is reduced by about <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$15-22 \%$</tex> while relative errors are preserved or increase by only <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\approx 3 \%$</tex> in the challenging missing-angle case. The resulting pipeline is training-free, lightweight to implement, and provides a strong, reproducible reference for evaluating learned and model-based CT reconstruction methods in biomedical imaging.

title: Weakly-Supervised Lung Nodule Segmentation in CT with One-Click Annotation
authors: Tianyi Qu, Dichao Liu, Ze Jin, Yuqiao Yang, Kenji Suzuki
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Deep Learning (DL) has become a predominant choice for medical image segmentation. However, the requirement for a large volume of precisely-annotated manual segmentation data poses a significant barrier. This study proposes a weaklysupervised approach to mitigating manual annotation labor in lung nodule segmentation in CT. Unlike conventional segmentation models that rely on manual pixel-wise annotations, our approach iteratively refines pseudo labels for 3D semantic segmentation generated from “one-click” input through function maps derived from an Explainable AI (XAI) method. The iterative retraining process using pseudo labels resulted in the performance <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\text{Dice Score} =78.83 {\%}, \text{HD}_{95}= 2.178)$</tex> comparable to the “gold-standard” fully-supervised model trained with manual pixel-wise annotations <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\text{Dice Score} =79.26 {\%}, \text{HD}_{95}=2.335)$</tex>. Additionally, we compared our approach with state-of-the-art weakly supervised methods. Experimental results demonstrated that our model achieved better performance while significantly reducing both parameter count and inference time. These findings highlight the effectiveness of our method for pulmonary nodule segmentation in 3D CT volumes.

title: Design and Optimization of a PI-Controlled Forward-Flyback Converter Using Matlab Simulation
authors: Prerna Bhingare, Girish K. Mahajan, Nilesh S. Wani, Rohini More, Kalyani Chavan, Ashwini J. Patil
publish: 2026 International Conference on Multidisciplinary Innovations For Smart &amp; Sustainable Future (MISSF)
abstract: This paper presents a PI-Controlled based forward-fly back converter designed for LED driver applications. The operating principle of the proposed converter is analyzed and experimentally validated using a 24 W laboratory prototype. The converter is tested with and without a balancing capacitor. This shows that adding the balancing capacitor makes it possible for stable forward and flyback operation over the whole input voltage range. As a result, the magnetizing offset current is much lower, which means less energy is lost in the core, and the transformer takes up less space. The prototype is evaluated under pulse-generator (PG) control and proportional-integral (PI) control, with both approaches producing similar primary-current and outputcurrent waveforms. However, the magnetizing current is notably lower when PI control is applied. The results show that the proposed converter does a better job of suppressing magnetizing current and transferring power throughout the switching cycle. It has less core loss, works more efficiently, and works reliably with different input conditions. Simulation results show an efficiency of 92.4 %, power factor of 0.98, and total harmonic distortion (THD) of 6.5% under rated conditions. The results confirm that PI control provides better dynamic response and stability compared to the Pulse Generator method. The suggested balanced forward-flyback converter is a great choice for LED driver applications that need a lot of power.

title: Analytical Modeling of Common-Mode and Differential-Mode Voltage Spectra for a Two-Level, Three-Phase Inverter
authors: Hassan Abdallah, Ryan Olson, Tianchen Li, Robert M. Cuzner
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: The adoption of wide-bandgap (WBG) devices has improved the power density and enabled higher switching frequencies of converters, but these advantages come with elevated electromagnetic emissions due to fast voltage transitions and parasitic interactions. This paper introduces an analytical prediction approach for electromagnetic interference (EMI) spectra of a two-level, three-phase inverter systems. The proposed approach focuses on synthesizing the conducted emission spectra by capturing the effects of dominating parasitics and the modulation-dependent switching behavior to obtain high frequency accuracy within the models. Common-mode (CM) and differential-mode (DM) voltage spectrum models are developed using refined waveform synthesis and equivalent circuit transformations. Hardware measurements are used to confirm the accuracy of the predicted spectra, demonstrating that the proposed method is capable of enabling early-stage EMI filter synthesis without the need for iterative hardware prototyping.

title: Personalized Federated Learning with Residual Fisher Information for Medical Image Segmentation
authors: Meilu Zhu, Yuxing Li, Zhiwei Wang, Edmund Y. Lam
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Federated learning enables multiple clients (institutions) to collaboratively train machine learning models without sharing their private data. To address the challenge of data heterogeneity across clients, personalized federated learning (pFL) aims to learn customized models for each client. In this work, we propose pFL -ResFIM, a novel pFL framework that achieves client-adaptive personalization at the parameter level. Specifically, we introduce a new metric, Residual Fisher Information Matrix (ResFIM), to quantify the sensitivity of model parameters to domain discrepancies. To estimate ResFIM for each client model under privacy constraints, we employ a spectral transfer strategy that generates simulated data reflecting the domain styles of different clients. Based on the estimated ResFIM, we partition model parameters into domain-sensitive and domain-invariant parts. A personalized model for each client is then constructed by aggregating only the domain-invariant parameters on the server. Extensive experiments on public datasets demonstrate that pFL -ResFIM consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods.

title: Face Recognition Using a Hybrid InceptionV3-Based Simple Neural Network and Autoencoder
authors: Mohammed Chalwi, Ali Muwafaq Shaban, Roaa Chalwi, Muthanna Mishlish, Mohammed Ahmed Salih, Mohammed M. Al-Ani
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Multidomain Integrated Learning Environments (ICSMILE)
abstract: This paper ultimately proposed a hybrid deep learning (DL) framework for face recognition that incorporates InceptionV3 for powerful feature extraction with an autoencoder layer to reduce the dimensionality of the representation. A simple neural network classifies the extracted compact feature representations and enables effective discrimination among fake and real face images. We utilize a large-scale dataset of fake and real face images obtained from Kaggle sources, consisting of 140000 such images. The proposed approach achieved high accuracy of 95.73%, outperforming several existing deep-fake detection approaches. The main contributions of this work contain the development of a hybrid InceptionV3–autoencoder feature extraction architecture, effective reduction of feature dimensionality while preserving discriminative information, and comprehensive evaluation demonstrating superior performance over related methods.

title: An Explainable Human-in-the-Loop Agentic AI Framework for Continuous Financial Planning and Analysis Beyond Forecast Accuracy
authors: Ramsundernag Changalva
publish: 2026 International Conference on Multidisciplinary Innovations For Smart &amp; Sustainable Future (MISSF)
abstract: Recent developments in artificial intelligence have enhanced the accuracy of financial forecasting, but most Financial Planning and Analysis (FP&A) studies have been preoccupied with predictive AI-powered decision systems design as opposed to the reliability of AI-powered forecasting. The missing element in real research relates to the lack of an AInative architecture of the full FP&A process that would consolidate uncertainty-aware predictive acts, explainable predictive reasoning, and operational responsibility, along with human governance. This paper suggests an Explainable Human-in-the-Loop Agentic AI model making FP&A not a one-off forecasting exercise, but an ongoing, AI-aided decision-making process. It has a combination of the probabilistic forecasting modules that are coupled with the explainable AI modules, multi agent workflow orchestration and structured human approval points to support transparency and accountable financial decision making. Autonomous agents implement the data ingestion coordination, and scenario simulation and budget optimization workloads and ensure auditability and compliance throughout the planning lifecycle by maintaining integrated governance layers. In contrast to the conventional methods that engage in the assessment of success based on accuracy of prediction, the suggested framework proposes decision adoption measures to determine whether AI-powered insights are accepted and used by the leaders of the financial industry. The architecture thus strives to bring FP&A to a scalable, interpretable, and governable AI ecosystem with the power to enable planning, budgeting, and strategic reforecasting on an ongoing basis.

title: Deep learning framework on physics-model data for accelerated design of nanoarray photocathode_supp1-3694453.docx
authors: Sihao Xia
abstract: Photoemission models are widely used for the structural design and physical analysis of photocathodes. However, the computational cost becomes prohibitive due to the complex equation-solving process when applied to nanostructured photocathodes, particularly for large-scale nanoarrays. In this work, we introduce a hybrid deep learning framework that employs a convolutional neural network (CNN) trained on data generated by rigorous three-dimensional photoemission simulations. The network is first pretrained on photocurrent data from small-scale nanoarrays (dimensions≤100 nm), demonstrating its capability to capture the underlying photoemission physics embedded in the simulation data. A lightweight transfer learning strategy using only hundreds of larger-scale samples then generalizes the Photoemission CNN (PECNN) to broader geometries. This approach reduces the average prediction error from 36.82% to 2.91% for emitted photocurrent and from 54.80% to 3.34% for collected photocurrent. Concurrently, the framework achieves computational speedups of approximately 1.6×103 for small nanoarrays and 1.5×104 for larger systems compared to traditional numerical solvers. Post-hoc interpretability analyses, including SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP), Permutation Importance (PI), and Partial Dependence Plots (PDP), identify array spacing, nanorod diameter, and substrate thickness as the dominant parameters governing emission performance, and the learned relationships align consistently with established photoemission theory. By delivering cross-scale, interpretable, and ultra-fast predictions, the PECNN framework provides a powerful tool for the rational design and optimization of advanced photoemission devices.

title: A High-Frequency Planar Transformer with Medium-Voltage Isolation and Embedded Thermal Management
authors: Shivangi Sinha, Zayed Ahmed, Jeffery Diebold, Dragan Maksimović
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: This paper presents the analysis and design of a compact high-frequency planar transformer with medium-voltage (MV) isolation, suitable for a wide range of modular MVAC to DC architectures. The transformer uses high-voltage PCB windings fully enveloped by low-voltage windings in a modified 12-layer stack-up with embedded polyimide layers. Thermal management components provide high conductivity to an enclosure, and the entire assembly is potted with high-dielectric-strength epoxy. The transformer enclosure, including thermal management components, achieves a power density of 4.6kW/l. It is used in a 10kW, 800–960V dual-active-bridge converter operating at 200 kHz with 98.2% measured efficiency at 6kW and 96.5% measured efficiency at full load power of 10.5kW. A peak temperature rise of 20°C is recorded within the core window at full load. A hipot tested isolation of 26 kV is achieved between the primary and secondary windings of the proposed transformer assembly.

title: Hybrid Preprocessing and Deep Learning for Real-Time Aerial Accident Detection Using Unmanned Aerial Vehicles
authors: Rajae Ziki, Amine Zeguendry, Majdoulin Sabri
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Global Aeronautical Engineering and Satellite Technology (GAST)
abstract: Accidents in urban and road environments pose significant risks to human life and property. Traditional monitoring systems, such as fixed cameras and manual inspections, have limitations in coverage and response speed. Drones equipped with computer vision systems provide a flexible solution for real-time monitoring and rapid accident detection. This work presents a drone-based framework for accident detection using state of the art object detection models. A hybrid image preprocessing pipeline, combining noise reduction and motion blur correction, is applied to enhance detection accuracy. Experimental results demonstrate that YOLOv8 achieves 92% precision with 18.3 ms inference time, significantly outperforming RT-DETR-L which achieves 62% precision. The proposed preprocessing approach improves detection performance by 4.7% in mean Average Precision while maintaining real-time processing capability. Comparative analysis reveals that YOLOv8 is particularly suitable for real-time drone applications due to its superior precision-speed trade-off. The study provides evidence that proper preprocessing is essential for aerial imagery, with our hybrid method substantially improving detection confidence across different model architectures. This approach demonstrates the effectiveness of drones in incident monitoring and offers practical insights for deploying aerial surveillance systems in urban and road environments.

title: Temporal Evolving Graph Neural Networks for Intrusion Detection in Large- Scale IoT Networks
authors: Bommy M, Pandipathi Thilak, Shaik Hussain Basha, Baigari Vamsi Krishna, Malathi Vikas Kumar
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Research Methodologies in Knowledge Management, Artificial Intelligence and Telecommunication Engineering (RMKMATE)
abstract: Cyber-attacks in large-scale IoT networks exhibit time-evolving behavior, where attack patterns change progressively across multiple time intervals. This temporal evolution poses significant challenges for conventional intrusion detection systems that rely on static traffic representations. To address this challenge, this paper proposes a temporal graph-based intrusion detection framework that explicitly models the evolution of network behavior over time. Network flows from the TON-IoT telemetry dataset are pre-processed and transformed into a sequence of temporal graph snapshots, where nodes represent traffic instances and edges encode temporal continuity. A Temporal Evolving Graph Neural Network (TE-GNN), is employed to adaptively learn evolving attack patterns by updating graph representations across successive snapshots. The proposed approach supports intrusion detection at both graph and node levels, to identify attack-dominant time periods and behavioral interpretation. Experimental evaluation across 58 temporal snapshots demonstrates that temporal graph-level learning provides stable and reliable intrusion detection under highly imbalanced conditions. Node-level performance highlights the inherent difficulty of precise attack localization during peak intrusion phases. Thus, temporal visualization of attack progression offers interpretable insights into slow-staged and multi-phase attacks, supporting effective monitoring and decision-making in dynamic IoT environments.

title: Synthesizing Brain Magnetic Resonance Images for Myelin Assessment
authors: Nikhil N. Chaudhari, Nahian F. Chowdhury, Emily Dennis, Elisabeth Wilde, Andrei Irimia
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Myelin is a critical factor in brain development, aging, and neurological disorders, including Alzheimer's disease and traumatic brain injury. The ratio of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$T_{1}$</tex>-weighted to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$T_{2}$</tex> weighted (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$T_{1} \mathrm{w} / T_{2} \mathrm{w}$</tex>) MRI intensities is a proxy for intracortical myelin content, but the scarcity of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$T_{2} \mathrm{w}$</tex> MRIs limits the utility of this method in research and clinical practice. We present T2VNet, a 3D autoencoder synthesizing <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$T_{2} \mathrm{w}$</tex> MRIs from <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$T_{1}$</tex> w MRIs to allow myelin computation. Trained on 1,033 participants and evaluated on an independent cohort of 140 participants, T2VNet demonstrates strong performance in recovering clinically relevant contrast (MultiSSIM3 <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\simeq 0.90$</tex>, PSNR <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$>27 \text{dB}$</tex>).
