abstract: High-power, low-voltage DC (LVDC) loads, such as AI data centers, electric vehicle fast-charging hubs, and hydrogen electrolyzers, are accelerating the deployment of solid-state transformers (SSTs) that directly interface medium-voltage AC (MVAC) to LVDC, streamlining power delivery. This paper proposes a submodule-level decentralized control strategy for an SST comprising a cascaded H-bridge (CHB) active rectifier on the MVAC side and per-module quadruple-active-bridge (QAB) converters on the LVDC side with local controllers. The proposed strategy eliminates the need for high-fidelity, real-time cross-isolation communication by synchronizing all active bridges to a common heartbeat signal and enabling the primary- and secondary-side controllers to generate independent local control signals (phase shifts). The resultant phase shifts naturally govern average and double-line frequency power transfer between active bridges, enabling each side to meet its control objectives using only local measurements. Detailed controller design considerations are presented based on a stability analysis. Comprehensive electromagnetic-transient simulations demonstrate tight DC-link voltage regulation, high-quality grid currents, and robust operation under load transients and grid disturbances, without real-time communication among the submodule controllers. These results indicate that the proposed scheme can significantly enhance the reliability, scalability, and flexibility of cascaded bridge-based medium-voltage converters.

title: EMI Reduction and Performance Assessment in a Multiphase Buck Converter using Digital Current Control Techniques under Periodic Frequency Modulation
authors: Teja Golla, Santanu Kapat, Philip T. Krein
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: A bulky EMI filter is needed in a multiphase buck converter for higher current applications, even with phase interleaving, to attenuate conducted EMI due to discontinuous input current. In this paper, periodic frequency modulation techniques are explored in a multiphase buck converter, and their impacts on steady-state parameters and transient performance are investigated under digital current mode control. A frequency-modulated digital current control architecture is proposed, and a relevant sampling and controller update process is demonstrated. A framework is developed for analyzing power spectral density (PSD) under various periodic frequency modulation techniques. Design aspects of these techniques are discussed in context. A two-phase buck converter prototype and experimental case studies are presented for nominal 1 MHz switching frequency. Periodic bifrequency modulation and quantized triangular modulation techniques are considered. Both techniques are shown to have insignificant impacts on closed-loop stability, steady-state parameters, and transient performance. However, quantized triangular modulation results in substantial EMI reduction compared to bifrequency modulation and to unmodulated cases.

title: Brain-CLIP: An Individual-Preserving Contrastive Framework for Bidirectional Cross-Modal Brain Connectome Translation
authors: Yaokai Yang, Nan Zhao, Zeyu Zhang, Jing Zhang, Lu Zhang
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Understanding the complex relationship between structural and functional brain networks is essential for uncovering the mechanisms that govern brain organization. However, most existing studies model this relationship in a unidirectional manner, either predicting functional connectivity (FC) from structural connectivity (SC) or vice versa, while very few approaches enable true bidirectional translation between the two modalities. To address this gap, we introduce Brain-CLIP, a novel framework inspired by the multimodal contrastive learning paradigm exemplified by CLIP, in which heterogeneous modalities are aligned in a shared latent space through semantic correspondence. Similarly, Brain-CLIP aligns SC and FC in a common embedding space to achieve bidirectional translation. Furthermore, we propose an Individual-Preserving CLIP Loss that maintains precise subject-level alignment between SC and FC representations, enabling the model to capture individual-specific connectomic patterns rather than merely population-level correspondences. Comprehensive evaluations on two large-scale datasets, HCP and ADNI, demonstrate that Brain-CLIP substantially outperforms existing methods. (Code is available here: https://github.com/BrainX-Lab/Brain-CLIP)

title: A Novel 3L-SNPC Power Module for EV Motor Drives
authors: Siqi Dai, Shuangxi Zhu, Jianwei Lv, Suhang Wei, Xuchen Sun, Cai Chen, Yong Kang, Wei Jiang, Pengyang Wang, Bin Lai
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: SNPC(Simplified Neutral Point Clamped) topology as a promising three-level converter topology, implementing such a topology using existing commercial power module inevitably leads to significant parasitic inductance, which limits its further development. So it is necessary to integrate the entire topology within a single module. In this paper, a power module for the SNPC topology is designed. By independently optimizing the commutation loops of the former stage and latter stage, an overlapped busbar structure is introduced to reduce the difference in parasitic inductance between different phases. The proposed module design is validated through simulation and experiments.

title: Cross-Attention Fusion of Structural and Deformation-Based Mri Features for Alzheimer's Disease Classification
authors: Shijia Zhang, Xiyu Ding, Brian Caffo, Junyu Chen, Xinyi Zhang, Hadi Kharrazi, Zheyu Wang
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Accurate assessment of Alzheimer's disease (AD) using neuroimaging is important for understanding disease progression and supporting clinical research. Structural MRI (sMRI) is widely used for this purpose, but most deep learning approaches primarily rely on intensity-based features, which may not fully capture subtle morphological variations. Jacobian determinant maps (JSM) provide complementary information by describing localized brain deformations; however, conventional multimodal fusion strategies such as early or late fusion may not fully capture the interactions between these modalities. To address this, we explore a cross-attention fusion approach that explicitly models the relationship between sMRI intensity and JSM-derived deformation features for AD classification. Using data from the Alzheimer's Disease Neuroimaging Initiative (ADNI), we compare cross-attention with pairwise self-attention and four baseline fusion methods. The proposed approach achieved mean ROC-AUC scores of 0.903 (0.033) for AD vs. cognitively normal (CN) and 0.692 (0.061) for mild cognitive impairment (MCI) vs. CN under five-fold cross-validation. The proposed approach achieved the best performance among fusion strategies and achieved higher or comparable performance with only 1.56 million parameters. These findings suggest that cross-attention fusion may serve as a potentially useful approach for integrating structural and deformation-based MRI features in AD classification tasks.

title: Identifying Optimal nnU-Net Configuration for Cerebral Microbleed Segmentation
authors: Junmo Kwon, Sang Won Seo, Hwan-ho Cho, Hyunjin Park
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: The nnU-Net has become the de facto standard in medical image segmentation. While the generalizability of nnUNet has widely been studied across various domains, we question the optimality of the automated nnU-Net configuration specifically for cerebral microbleed (CMB) segmentation. We identified that the standard nnU-Net setup, particularly the recommended input patch size, is primarily optimized for the heterogeneous nature of multi-organ segmentation, resulting in suboptimal performance for CMB segmentation. To address this limitation, we propose a patch selection algorithm based on a local minima search that fully leverages the imaging characteristics of CMB lesions. Experiments on two independent cohorts demonstrate that our approach improves the overall performance without altering the segmentation backbone, highlighting the important role of preprocessing. Our code is available at https://github.com/junmokwon/CMBPreprocessing

title: Self-Supervised Vision Transformer for Surgical Phase Recognition in Endoscopic Submucosal Dissection
authors: Gyanateet Dutta, Aya Hammad, Thomas Archer, Qi Dou, Noor Mohammed, Sharib Ali
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Surgical phase recognition is critical for context-aware interventions, but deep learning models require large, costly annotated datasets. This work presents DINO-Endo, an efficient framework pairing a self-supervised DINOv2 Vision Transformer (ViT-S/14) backbone with lightweight temporal models. Through sequential domain adaptation—pretraining on unannotated porcine videos followed by human endoscopic videos—our approach produces highly discriminative features that enable simple temporal decoders to achieve state-of-the-art performance. We demonstrate 89.5% accuracy on patient dataset and 90.0% on complex porcine dataset, outperforming existing methods while using 14-times fewer parameters than foundation models like V-JEPA2. Our comprehensive evaluation reveals that powerful SSL features reduce temporal modeling complexity, with dataset characteristics determining optimal decoder architecture. This work establishes that domain-specific self-supervised pretraining combined with efficient temporal heads achieves state-of-the-art results while reducing annotation dependency and computational requirements for clinical deployment.

title: Design and Analysis of Leakage Inductance Integrated Transformer Structures Utilizing Interleaved Windings
authors: Kota Okada, Sihoon Choi, Jun Imaoka, Masayoshi Yamamoto, Takayuki Ikari, Akinori Okubo, Yuichi Iwasaki, Takahiro Tokita
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: Power electronics converters have been required to achieve high efficiency and high power density. Magnetic components which occupy a significant volume in converters have been miniaturized to satisfy the requirements. One approach to realize the miniaturization is to reduce the number of magnetic components through the integration of multiple windings within a single magnetic core. This paper proposes two types of integrated transformer structures with interleaved windings that incorporate leakage inductance. This series inductance value can be designed based on the number of primary turns located on either the outer or center leg. A design method based on magnetic circuits for each inductance and the maximum flux density within the core is presented. The feasibility of the proposed magnetic components is validated using a 48V-24V 50W prototype LLC resonant converter.

title: Large Language Models in Cybersecurity AI-Driven Threat Detection and Prevention
authors: Deepika Sharma, Anurag Shrivastava, Bharti Sahu, Ronak Agrawal, Sorabh Lakhanpal, Ankam Sreejyothsna
publish: 2026 International Conference on Multidisciplinary Innovations For Smart &amp; Sustainable Future (MISSF)
abstract: The Large Language Models (LLMs) have been made considerably more efficient, and the AI helps to identify and eliminate the possible threats to the safety of the cyberspace. The intrusion identification, the automation of vulnerability tests, and the use of complex attack patterns are helpful in identifying a vulnerability and assist in bettering the assault intelligence, which is used in the localization of complex patterns of attacks. However, the significant threats of the implementation of ML-based applications, such as adversarial attacks, data privacy, and model bias, are to be addressed in the relevant manner. LLMs in Cybersecurity: Overview of recent advances The article concerns the future of using LLMs in cybersecurity in terms of how the powerful models can be used to prevent threats before they occur, detect anomalies and facilitate the process of automated response. It also brings out some of the problems and ethical issues of introducing LLM to security systems. By enlisting the strength of the progress of AI, organizations will be able to improve their cyber defense and minimize exposure to the ever-changing character of cyber threats. The authors of the study believe that LLAMs have potential solutions to cybersecurity but more research is needed to reduce vulnerabilities and maximize their use in practice.

title:
Robust T–S Fuzzy H
                    <sub>∞</sub>
                    Control for Maximum Power Point Tracking in Uncertain Photovoltaic Systems
authors: Rabii Azdoud, Abdelmounaim Khallouq, Ali Margal, Soukaina El Daoudi, Fatima Zahra Loudifa, Abderrazzak El Abdallaoui, Asma Karama
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Global Aeronautical Engineering and Satellite Technology (GAST)
abstract: This work addresses the problem of maximum power point tracking (MPPT) in photovoltaic (PV) systems operating under parametric uncertainties and environmental variations. The MPPT is formulated using Takagi–Sugeno (T– S) fuzzy modeling approach, as a reference tracking problem. A robust H<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">∞</inf> control law is designed to guarantee closed-loop stability while attenuating the effect of modeling uncertainties and external disturbances. Stability and performance conditions are established via Lyapunov-based analysis and expressed as Linear Matrix Inequalities (LMIs). The proposed strategy’s performance is evaluated using MATLAB/Simulink simulations under varying irradiance and temperature profiles, showing reliable convergence to the maximum power point.

title: Predicting Future Land Use Patterns in Kurnool District, India using Cellular Automata and Artificial Neural Network Models
authors: Sunandana Reddy Machireddy, Keerthan Kumar Mallipeddi, Sathish Kumar Reddy Baasireddy, Umesh Kodidala
publish: 2026 International Conference on Multidisciplinary Innovations For Smart &amp; Sustainable Future (MISSF)
abstract: The focal point of this study is the concept of land use and land cover (LULC) changes in Kurnool district, India, using Cellular Automata (CA) and Artificial Neural Network (ANN) models in geographic information system environment during the period of 2019-2025. The satellite images were used to study and categorize the land into those categories like water bodies, vegetation, cropland, and dry land using Landsat 8 satellite images. The analysis of changes in 2019-2022 and the forecasting of 2025 were performed using GIS software (QGIS and ArcGIS) and MOLUSCE plugin using Cellular Automata (CA) and Artificial Neural Network (ANN) models. The results indicated that the outputs produced a minor change in the water bodies, increase in the cropland areas and dry lands and generally a good match between the simulated and the real 2025 maps (71% accuracy, Kappa = 0.623). This work will help the policy makers and planners to understand land transformation and make better decisions to ensure sustainable development.

title: Wearable Flexible Triangular Microstrip Antenna for Sub-6 GHz 5G Applications
authors: Asma Oirgari, Ibtissam Bahlaouane, Saida Ibnyaich, Samira Chabaa, Abdelouhab Zeroual
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Global Aeronautical Engineering and Satellite Technology (GAST)
abstract: In this paper, a flexible triangular patch antenna is designed, simulated, and fabricated. A textile substrate with a presumed relative permittivity of 1.6 was considered. The antenna was fabricated using denim (jean) textile as the substrate with dimensions of 30 × 34 × 0.8 mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup> and copper tape as the conductive material, while it was designed and simulated using ANSYS HFSS, and the results were validated using CST Microwave Studio software. The textenna (textile + antenna) attained a resonance frequency of 4.30 GHz and a bandwidth from 2.74 GHz to 5.00 GHz, exhibiting superior resonance characteristics in the Sub-6 GHz range. These findings verify that the proposed antenna is highly competitive as an inexpensive, compact, and easily integrable solution for Sub-6 GHz 5G, WiMAX, and wearable applications.

title: Pretext Task Adversarial Learning for Unpaired Low-Field to Ultra High-Field MRI Synthesis
authors: Zhenxuan Zhang, Peiyuan Jing, Zi Wang, Ula Briski, Coraline Beitone, Yue Yang, Yinzhe Wu, Fanwen Wang, Liutao Yang, Jiahao Huang, Zhifan Gao, Guang Yang, Peter J. Lally
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Given the scarcity and high cost of high-field MRI, synthesizing high-field images from low-field MRI offers great potential for augmenting limited data in downstream tasks (e.g., segmentation). Low-field MRI typically suffers from low SNR and spatial resolution, making cross-domain synthesis challenging due to feature misalignment and anatomical distortion. To address this, we propose a Pretext Task Adversarial (PTA) framework for high-field MRI synthesis. It consists of: (1) a Slice-wise Gap Perception (SGP) network for aligning inter-slice inconsistencies via contrastive learning; (2) a Local Structure Correction (LSC) network for refining local details from rotated and masked inputs; and (3) a pretext-guided adversarial training that enhances realism through additional supervision and discrimination. Experiments on low-to-ultra-high-field MRI demonstrate state-of-the-art performance (FID =16.892, IS =1.933, MS-SSIM =0.324), generating anatomically consistent high-field-like data to improve downstream training. The code is available at: https://github.com/Zhenxuan-Zhang/PTA4Unpaired_HF_MRI_SYN.

title: Swarm Intelligence Based Approach Towards Construction of an Optimal Portfolio of Stocks
authors: Karthikeyan P, Manjula K A
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Research Methodologies in Knowledge Management, Artificial Intelligence and Telecommunication Engineering (RMKMATE)
abstract: Nature-inspired metaheuristic algorithms are popular for their capability to solve complex issues. Among the Nature-inspired computing techniques, Swarm intelligence techniques, especially Ant Colony Optimisation (ACO) algorithm are a widely used technique. ACO is inspired by how ants find paths. This study presents a novel approach to portfolio optimization based on the principles of Ant Colony Optimisation (ACO) algorithm. We applied this method to historical stock data of eight selected companies in India. The basic principles of search in ACO is used along with the principles of Modern Portfolio Theory to create the best possible portfolio. Our aim is to achieve the highest Sharpe Ratio, even when the market is highly unpredictable. In this study, the portfolio is created using data from eight major Indian stocks collected from the NSE, for the years from 2015 to 2024. A Sharpe Ratio of 1.45 is achieved, with an expected annual return of 18.2% and a risk level (volatility) of 10.5%. These results indicate that the new method based on ACO is an effective tool for developing an optimized portfolio.

title: A 22-kW Three-Phase Wireless Charging System Capable of Independent Phase Control
authors: Hyeon-Young Lee, Yeon-Jun Park, Min-Seok Chae, Jun-Sik Hwang, Seojin Kim, Hyeon-Gyu Choi, Euihoon Chung
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: This paper proposes a new three-phase wireless power transfer (WPT) system designed to address the efficiency degradation caused by coil misalignment. The proposed system independently controls the dc-link voltage and duty cycle of each primary-side inverter phase, this enables each phase to deliver power at an independently optimized level that reflects the phase-specific coupling variations under misalignment. Since the optimal dc-link voltage varies significantly across phases under misalignment, a systematic optimization process is essential. To this end, this study presents a coil modeling technique, a mathematical analysis method, and a numerical iteration–based optimization procedure. Simulation results verify the validity of the proposed mathematical model, and a 22-kW hardware prototype experimentally demonstrates the effectiveness of the method, achieving 96.74 % efficiency under aligned conditions and 95.17 % efficiency under misaligned conditions. Furthermore, the strong agreement between the simulated and measured waveforms confirms that the proposed design procedure provides a systematic and highly reproducible methodology for high-performance WPT system development.

title: FreshNet 360: A Hybrid Clustering-Deep Learning Framework for Multi-Stage Food Freshness Grading Using Image-Based Quality Signatures
authors: Sampurna Roy, Tanupriya Choudhury, S Christalin Nelson, Minakshi
publish: 2026 Second International Conference on Emerging Computational Intelligence (ICECI)
abstract: Maintaining the freshness of perishable food products is a very imperative issue in the field of retail, supplychain management as well as consumer safety. The conventional methods of freshness determination also largely depend on manual examination which is subjective, inconsistent and laborious. To obtain multi-stage food freshness grading, this paper suggests FreshNet 360, a unified, hybrid based machine learning architecture that combines both unsupervised clustering and deep machine learning. An image pipeline was designed with a systematic order that consisted of noise removal, grayscale conversion, edge detection, and generalized augmentation which produced a solid dataset of various types of fruits and vegetables. To obtain natural groupings in the data, three clustering algorithms (K Means, Agglomerative Hierarchical Clustering and DBSCAN) were used and the results were mapped onto five freshness levels Fresh, Slightly Aged, Stale, Spoiled and Rotten. Eight deep leaning models, VGG16, VGG19, MobileNetV2, ResNet50, DenseNet121, InceptionV3, Xception, and a custom AlexNet were trained and benchmarked with these labels. Experimental results demonstrate that MobileNetV2 achieves the highest performance with K-Means clustered data (accuracy: 0.77), while AlexNet excels with Agglomerative clustering (accuracy: 0.84). The study highlights the effectiveness of clustering-enhanced label generation in improving classifier stability, enabling scalable, reliable, and automated food freshness detection for realworld applications. The full link to the github repo of this is https://github.com/sampurna7373/FreshNet-360.

title: Prediction of Crop Yield Using AI Approach
authors: Megavath Aravind Kumar, Swarup Nandi
publish: 2026 Second International Conference on Emerging Computational Intelligence (ICECI)
abstract: Agriculture remains vital to both the economy and food security of India, but accurate crop yield forecasting remains hampered by climatic variability, soil heterogeneity, and limitations in traditional estimation methodologies. Traditional methods are mainly based on historical records, which hardly consider real-time environmental conditions and thus cannot produce reliable predictions. This paper, therefore, proposes a hybrid machine learning-based framework for crop yield prediction using multi-source agricultural data. The given framework combines weather parameters, soil nutrient data, and historical data of crop yield, collected from Medak district. Individual models such as Decision Tree (DT), Random Forest (RF), Support Vector Machine (SVM), and Artificial Neural Network (ANN) are trained and evaluated to assess their predictive capabilities. A hybrid ensemble model has been further developed in order to address the limitations of single model approaches by synergistically integrating the advantages of these techniques. To validate the generalizability of our findings, we utilized the standard metrics of $\mathbf{R}^{\mathbf{2}}$ score, Mean Absolute Error(MAE), Root Mean Square Error(RMSE), and accuracy besides $\mathbf{k}$-fold cross-validation to ensure robustness. The experimental results indicate that the proposed hybrid ensemble model achieves a maximum prediction accuracy of 95.82%, outperforming individual machine learning models in terms of stability and error reduction. The key contributions of this research are three-fold: (i) the integration of multi-source district-level data such as weather and soil nutrients for crop yield prediction (ii) the design of a hybrid ensemble model that combines DT, RF, SVM, and ANN to address the limitations of individual models when data is limited, and (iii) the evaluation of the model using accuracy, $\mathbf{R}^{\mathbf{2}}$, MAE, and RMSE, which outperforms the individual models. The dataset is for a period of ten years, from 2015 to 2025, covering important nutrients in the soil.

title: Reinforcement Learning-Enhanced Black-winged Kite Algorithm for Cloud Workflow Scheduling
authors: Mohammad Qasim, Md. Saquib Jawed, Raza Abbas Haidri
publish: 2026 Second International Conference on Emerging Computational Intelligence (ICECI)
abstract: The growing complexity of dynamic and heterogeneous cloud environments has significantly increased the need for efficient scheduling algorithms. Metaheuristic (MH) algorithms have attracted significant attention for solving scheduling problems over the past decade. This study presents a recently developed Black-winged Kite Algorithm (BKA) along with three reinforcement learning (RL)-enhanced BKA variants: Q-learning-based BKA (BKA-Q), SARSA-based BKA (BKA-SARSA), and deep Q-network (DQN)-based BKA (BKA-DQN) to tackle the workflow scheduling problem. The performance of RL-enhanced BKA variants compares to standard BKA across the LIGO, Montage, and Epigenomics workflows in cloud environments. Experimental findings demonstrate that the BKA-DQN variant improves the makespan results compared to the standard BKA and the other two RL-enhanced BKA variants. The BKA-DQN variant achieves a superior makespan and faster convergence across all workflows. The DQN-enhanced BKA improves the makespan performance by $\mathbf{1. 5 \%}$ to $\mathbf{5. 4 \%}$ across different scientific workflows compared to the other algorithms. Statistical analysis further validates BKA-DQN’s strong potential for solving workflow scheduling problems.

title: Obscuration to Clarity: Bone Suppression for Enhanced Localization in Pneumothorax Segmentation of Chest Radiographs
authors: Ananya Shukla, Amog Rao, Siddharth S., Rina Bao
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Chest radiography (CXR) is a primary modality for assessing cardiopulmonary conditions, but its effectiveness is limited by anatomical obstructions (e.g., ribs, clavicles) that hinder accurate pneumothorax segmentation, boundary delineation, and severity estimation. While deep learning-based bone suppression improves soft-tissue visibility, its utility for precise pixel-wise localization remains underexplored. This study investigates the downstream application of bone suppression for pneumothorax segmentation, integrating it as a preprocessing step to mitigate bony obscuration. We evaluate its impact across CNN and Vision Transformer models on two public datasets, where models trained on bone-suppressed CXRs significantly outperform (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$p<0.05$</tex>) non-suppressed counterparts, achieving up to 17 % improvement in Mean Average Surface Distance (MASD), 4.9% in Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC), and 5.9 % in Normalized Surface Dice (NSD), alongside a 9.5% gain in Matthew's Correlation Coefficient (MCC). These results demonstrate bone suppression as an architecture-independent enhancement for pneumothorax localization, improving the reliability of automated CXR interpretation.

title: LAS: Reducing Redundant Memory Access in ANNS via Adaptive Learned Indexing
authors: Yi Wang, Huan Liu, Jianan Yuan, Jiaxian Chen, Tianyu Wang, Chenlin Ma, Rui Mao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems
abstract: Approximate Nearest Neighbor Search (ANNS) has become a key paradigm for large-scale high-dimensional data retrieval. As a typical ANNS technique, Inverted File with Product Quantization (IVFPQ) provides a promising solution by significantly reducing storage costs through PQ compression. However, IVFPQ suffers from severe redundant memory access issues during retrieval. This paper proposes LAS, a novel ANNS accelerator specifically designed to reduce redundant memory access in IVFPQ. LAS presents two core techniques : dimensionality reduction component and adaptive learned index forest (ALIF). The dimensionality reduction component filters out a large number of non-neighbor vectors in the early retrieval stage. ALIF improves the accuracy of neighbor prediction through a multi-segment indexing design, further reducing redundant accesses caused by prediction errors. Meanwhile, ALIF employs an adaptive search strategy based on query-cluster relationships to dynamically adjust the search range of different clusters. Experimental results demonstrate that LAS achieves significant improvements in retrieval efficiency and effectively reduces redundant memory access in IVFPQ compared to representative works.

title: A Quasi-Single-Stage 48 V–to–0.8 V Voltage Regulator Module Based On Half-Bridge Current Doubler With Zero Bias TLVR
authors: Simone Mazzer, Roberto Rizzolatti, Mario Ursino, Kevin Zufferli, Gerald Deboy, Stefano Saggini
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: This work introduces a quasi-single-stage 48 V, 350 A VRM voltage regulator module (VRM). A single module combines a four-phase half-bridge current doubler (HB-CD) and a single-phase non-isolated buck converter. The former performs the direct conversion from the 48 V, while the latter from an intermediate bus voltage (IBV) of 5 V. Phase coupling is realized thanks to TLVR, which the buck converter actively supplies to drive the output, achieving zero DC-bias flux in the HB-CD integrated magnetic (ZB-TLVR). Optionally, buck power stages can be further coupled by a conventional, second level TLVR (Cascaded TLVR). A system demonstrator for 48 V/0.8 V has been built. With multifrequency, cascaded ZB-TLVR operation, the converter is capable of fast transient response in an overall efficient (90% peak, up to 84.7% TDC) solution.

title: 800 V On-Board Charger with Reduced DC-Link Capacitance via Embedded Active Power Decoupling
authors: Qishan Pan, Guanjiang Liu, Dejun Zhang, Haoyu Wang
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: On-board chargers (OBCs) face challenges from bulky DC-link electrolytic capacitors, oversized isolation transformers, and efficiency loss in the isolated DC/DC stage over wide battery voltage ranges. Conventional active power decoupling (APD) circuits can reduce DC-link capacitance requirements. However, they typically rely on additional external switches and energy storage components dedicated to a single function. To improve, this work introduces a two-stage 800V OBC architecture with reduced DC-link capacitance via embedded active power decoupling. The proposed design employs an integrated APD unit, thereby lowering capacitance demand, minimizing transformer volume, and enhancing the performance of the isolated DC/DC stage under wide gain conditions. A 3.3kW prototype, supporting an output voltage range of 600–900V, achieves a peak full-load efficiency of 95.96%, while consistently maintaining high performance across the entire operating range.

title: Optimized TPS with New Symmetric Mode Boundaries for Bidirectional DAB Converters in Ultra-Wide Range SiC EV Charging Applications
authors: Mahmoodreza Eskandarpour Azizkandi, Wensong Yu, Md Tanvir Ahammed, Liming Liu, Hongrae Kim
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: This paper proposes a novel optimized triple-phase-shift (TPS) with symmetric mode boundaries for bidirectional dual-active-bridge (DAB) converters, enabling practical and efficient ultra-wide voltage range (150 V - 900 V) and high frequency (150 kHz) operation for SiC-based EV chargers. Unlike state-of-the-art TPS methods, which suffer from asymmetric mode plots, complex multi-case equations, lack of reverse power flow coverage, and impractical real-time implementation, the proposed approach defines a normalized voltage ratio and absolute normalized power to unify and cover both forward and reverse power flow under a single, super-simplified, and computationally efficient control law and reveal a symmetrical relationship between buck and boost regions, eliminating the need for separate equations. Experimental validation on a 12 kW, 150 kHz prototype demonstrates the proposed TPS strategy enables full ZVS even at very low power, and fast real-time execution—confirming its practicality for SiC EV charging applications.

title: Barrier-Engineered Drain-Adaptive Thyristor 1T-DRAM for Disturb Suppression
authors: Hyeon-Seo Do, Chae-Ryang Heo, Sung-Woong Chung
abstract: Capacitorless 1T-DRAM is attractive for high-density memory, but its implementation is limited by the write/disturb trade-off, sneak current, and array-level IR-drop/RC delay. Here, we propose a barrier-engineered drain-adaptive thyristor 1T-DRAM in a 4F² cell. An array configuration utilizing a ground source plate is proposed to suppress IR drop. More importantly, the adaptive barrier gate dynamically modulates the internal barrier, lowering it for write/read operations while maintaining a high barrier in disturb/sneak states. The proposed operation is explained from a device-physics perspective in terms of capacitive coupling, barrier-pinning, and depinning. TCAD simulations demonstrated that the disturb current was reduced by several orders of magnitude with the adaptive barrier gate at Vd = 1 V. These results provide a route toward low-voltage, disturb-immune, and array-scalable capacitorless DRAM.

title: IEEE Computer Society Call for Papers
authors: N/A

title: Spatiotemporal Satellite User Resource Forecasting in MEO–LEO Networks via a CNN–LSTM Model
authors: Yi Jing, Chunxiao Jiang, Jiawei Wang, Jiachen Sun, Sicong Zhou, Yafeng Zhan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network Science and Engineering
abstract: Large-scale low Earth orbit (LEO) constellations are driving satellite communications toward increasingly dynamic and user-centric operation, making short-horizon demand forecasting important for proactive resource reservation and scheduling in cooperative MEO–LEO networks. To support this task, this paper constructs a satellite user request-map dataset from desensitized operational business records by transforming raw logs into grid-structured spatiotemporal demand maps through temporal slicing and spatial aggregation. Based on a cross-layer MEO–LEO workflow, we further develop a CNN–LSTM hybrid predictor that captures spatial patterns within each demand snapshot and temporal dependencies across historical windows, so as to support short-horizon forecasting of future demand maps. Experiments on 5-minute demand maps with a 30-minute forecasting horizon show that the proposed method achieves 90.59% mean total-traffic accuracy, outperforming Heuristic, ConvLSTM, and Transformer-Latent by 3.02, 3.43, and 3.66 percentage points, respectively. These results indicate that the proposed scheme can effectively characterize the spatiotemporal regularities of satellite service traffic and provide practical support for proactive reservation and scheduling in cooperative MEO–LEO systems.

title: Hierarchical Multi-Domain Collaboration Based on DRL: An Intelligent and Generalized Architecture for Multi-Satellite Collaborative Communications
authors: Yuanzhi He, Biao Sheng
abstract: Multi-satellite collaborative communications (MSCC) is emerging as a cornerstone of 6G satellite networks to ensure ubiquitous coverage and high quality of service (QoS). However, the non-stationary channel conditions, limited link resource constraints, highly dynamic topology, and rapidly increasing problem scale pose significant challenges to traditional optimization methods. Deep reinforcement learning (DRL) offers a promising solution, yet current algorithms are tailored to specific problems and lack a holistic perspective. To bridge this gap, this paper presents a comprehensive survey and proposes an intelligent, generalized architecture for multi-satellite collaboration. First, we synthesize a set of algorithmic design principles along the dimensions of problem type, observability, and system scale, providing a systematic guide for algorithm selection. Subsequently, guided by these principles, we establish a cloud-edge-terminal hierarchical federated DRL framework. This architecture leverages geostationary earth orbit (GEO) satellites as intermediate aggregators and global context providers, bridging the gap between the ground cloud’s massive computing power and low earth orbit (LEO) edge’s real-time inference needs. This design effectively mitigates partial observability and feeder link congestion, facilitating the resolution of communication optimization issues spanning from the physical layer to the network layer. We propose a hierarchical multi-satellite collaborative framework where LEO agents, guided by GEO-aggregated global context, employ the multi-agent proximal policy optimization (MAPPO) based algorithm to dynamically optimize semantic modality selection and access control. Simulation results validate that this approach effectively manages multi-level image and text semantic transmission, enhancing system adaptability. Finally, we outline future directions, including communication-computing synergy and embodied intelligence, to guide the evolution of autonomous satellite networks.

title: Centrally Built-in Power-Terminal Matrix Transformer for Vertical Power Delivery in LLC DCX
authors: Tongrui Sun, Wenjie Chen, Panming Li, Wei Zhou, Jiarun Xie, Xingwei Huang, Kangping Wang, Xu Yang
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: In low-voltage, high-current LLC-DCX converters, vertical power delivery (VPD) tightly couples power terminal placement with transformer geometry, constraining achievable current density and scalability. This paper proposes a centrally built-in power-terminal (CBiPT) matrix transformer, in which the power terminals are embedded within the transformer to reduce external PCB power routing. The proposed structure supports both horizontal and vertical matrix extension, enabling high turns ratio and high current density while maintaining a compact footprint. The proposed structure is designed, optimized, and thermally analyzed, based on which a 48 V–0.75 V LLC-DCX prototype is built. The prototype achieves a peak efficiency of 94.2%. Under immersion liquid cooling, it delivers up to 800 A at full load, corresponding to a current density of 1.12 A/mm².

title: Sustainable Federated Learning in 6G Networks: The Case for Cybersecurity
authors: Gokcan Cantali, Wissem Soussi, Gurkan Gur, Burkhard Stiller
abstract: 6G networks are expected to integrate distributed artificial intelligence methods, such as federated learning (FL), across layers from core infrastructure to end-user applications. While FL offers significant advantages by enabling collaborative model training without sharing local and sensitive data, its deployment in large-scale, complex 6G environments raises important sustainability and security concerns. In particular, FL's communication overhead, including carbon footprint, requires careful optimization to ensure sustainable operation. Simultaneously, the cybersecurity implications of sustainable FL remain underexplored. In this work, we examine the sustainability challenges associated with FL and analyze them from a cybersecurity perspective through a use case, highlighting relevant trade-offs and key research directions for future 6G networks.

title: High-sensitivity PDMS film-based Fabry–Pérot interferometer for magnetic field sensing via magnetic fluid volume effect
authors: Youlian Wang, Yinping Miao, Xiaolan Li, Shouchuan Guo, Zhi Zhang, Jianquan Yao
abstract: reflective fiber-optic magnetic field sensor based on the magnetic fluid (MF) magnetic volume effect is proposed. The sensor incorporates a Fabry-Pérot (F-P) interference structure formed by a single-mode fiber (SMF) and a polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) film. By detecting the volume changes of MF under varying magnetic field strengths, the sensor achieves highly sensitive magnetic field measurement. When exposed to an external magnetic field, the nanoparticles in the MF undergo oriented alignment, resulting in anisotropic volume changes. These changes are transmitted to the F-P air cavity via the PDMS film, enabling precise measurement of magnetic field intensity. Experimental results demonstrate that with a 48.4 μm-thick PDMS film, the sensor achieves axial and radial magnetic field sensitivities of -2.3 nm/mT and -2.73 nm/mT, respectively, within the 0-12 mT range. Furthermore, we observed that reducing the PDMS film thickness significantly enhances magnetic field sensitivity. After optimization to 46.92 μm, the sensor achieved a radial sensitivity of -9.40 nm/mT, a detection limit of 0.0021 mT, and excellent linearity (R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> = 0.9988). The sensor combines the advantages of simple structure, high sensitivity and low fabrication cost, which provides a new solution for magnetic field measurement in the fields of power equipment monitoring and aerospace.

title: Spatial Channel Characterization Based on Scalar Frequency Response Measurements in UAV-Assisted Systems
authors: Zhixin Huang, Fengchun Zhang, Wei Fan
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: Unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV)-based channel sounding and antenna measurement have been attracting significant interest. However, acquiring complex channel frequency responses (CFRs) with accurate phase is often challenging due to strict UAV payload constraints and synchronization complexities. This letter proposes a novel array signal processing framework to retrieve the power-angle-delay profile (PADP) using only scalar frequency responses (SFRs) in line-of-sight (LOS) environments. By exploiting the strong LOS path typically present in air-to-ground links, which naturally favors the minimum-phase condition, the proposed method first reconstructs the phase response via the Hilbert transform. Furthermore, with a-priori knowledge of the LOS path distance, angle of arrival (AoA), and array geometry, a LOS path calibration and relative phase compensation procedure is introduced to restore the original complex CFRs of all array elements. Experimental validation in a controlled LOS scenario demonstrates the effectiveness of the proposed algorithm, offering a low-cost phaseless solution for constrained UAV-assisted measurement applications.

title: Seamless Power Transfer of Bidirectional Converters in Microgrids Applications
authors: Emanuel Serban, Cristian Lascu
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: Power converters utilizing bidirectional topologies are essential for maintaining continuous microgrid operation and ensuring reliable power delivery to both DC and AC loads. This paper introduces a hybrid AC–DC microgrid architecture that delivers reliable power to various loads while providing enhanced operational flexibility and robust resilience under dynamic and fault conditions. The design and inclusion of a bidirectional DC-DC converter provides critical functionalities such as prioritization of essential loads (e.g., life-support systems), extension of the DC voltage operating range, and galvanic isolation.During dynamic microgrid’s operating conditions, the high-frequency transformer within the DC–DC converter can exhibit increased sensitivity to pulsewidth modulation (PWM) mismatches, saturation and delayed response in power transfer detection. To address these challenges, a microgrid controller is proposed and integrated within the DC–DC converter to enable seamless power transfer during charge and discharge transitions. The proposed communication-free control strategy employs a voltage gradient detection method for rapid event identification and smooth bidirectional power transfer. Each module within the DC microgrid autonomously monitors local parameters and, based on positive or negative voltage gradient variations, determines its operating mode accordingly. The effectiveness of the proposed strategy is validated through experimental laboratory studies conducted under dynamic charging and discharging scenarios.

title: Deep-GyralNet: Enabling Machine Learning in Gyral Folding Pattern Extraction on Cortical Surface
authors: Chao Cao, Jiale Cheng, Minheng Chen, Jing Zhang, Tianming Liu, Lu Zhang, Dajiang Zhu, Gang Li
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: The 3-hinge gyrus (3HG), where three gyri converge, acts as a structural and functional hub in the brain. Connecting these hubs forms the GyralNet, but traditionally extraction requires complex geometric steps, limiting scalability. To address this challenge, we propose Deep-GyralNet, a novel deep learning framework for efficient 3HG and GyralNet extraction. Deep-GyralNet leverages a Spherical U-Net to learn gyral crest representations from cortical morphological features, coupled with a Connectivity-Aware Path Enforcement loss that enforces topological continuity of predicted ridges. A minimum spanning tree-based refinement is then applied to ensure structural connectivity and yield a topologically valid GyralNet. Experiments on the Human Connectome Project dataset demonstrate that Deep-GyralNet achieves an average completeness of 98%, while reducing processing time by 94% compared to the conventional pipeline. These establish DeepGyralNet the first deep learning solution for fast and accurate 3HG identification, enabling large-scale studies of gyral folding networks in brain development, aging and disorders.

title: A 6.78-MHz kW-Level 15-cm Air-Gap Misalignment-Tolerant Capacitive Wireless Power Transfer System for EV Charging Employing Roadway-Vehicle DC-DC Converters
authors: Asif Mushtaq Bhat, Ayush Dixit, Sreyam Sinha
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: In capacitive wireless power transfer (CWPT) systems for EV charging, coupling variations arising from roadway-to-vehicle coupler misalignment or varying vehicle ground clearance leads to significant change in output power and increased losses. This paper introduces a roadway-vehicle DC-DC converter (RVDC) based CWPT system that mitigates this issue by using two DC-DC converters, one on the roadway-side and one on the vehicle-side. These converters control the input and output DC link voltages of the system during coupling variations to maintain a constant output power while ensuring a resistive loading of the inverter and thereby reducing losses. A comprehensive design methodology for the proposed RVDC based CWPT system is presented. A 6.78-MHz, 15-cm air-gap, 2.2-kW RVDC-based CWPT prototype suitable for EV charging is designed and built. Experimental results with 1-kW output power demonstrate that the inverter loading phase and output power remain constant for up to 50% lateral misalignment between roadway and vehicle-side coupler plates.

title: Magnetic Coupling Path Reconfiguration in Multi-Node Domino Wireless Power Transfer Systems for Insulator Monitoring
authors: Wei Wang, Penghui Li, Zheng Lu, Zihao Zheng
abstract: In domino wireless power transfer (WPT) systems, inefficient magnetic energy circulation arises from complex coupling networks and redundant mutual inductance among resonators, which limits power delivery to downstream nodes, particularly in high-voltage insulator strings with multiple sensors. A magnetic coupling path reconfiguration strategy based on dynamic relay switching is proposed in this paper. By controlling the connection and disconnection of key nodes in the linkage, the target load operates under optimal coupling conditions, enabling controllable energy flow. Results show that, disconnecting node 2 can significantly enhance the downstream coupling strength, increasing load input power by 1.5–2 times, as verified in four- and five-coil structures. A feasible solution for long-term stable power supply to multiple sensors on high-voltage insulator strings is provided.

title: Algorithmic Exploration of Matching Network Topologies for Power Amplifiers
authors: Sergio López de Pablo, Joel Mesas, Pedro de Paco, Jordi Verdú
publish: IEEE Transactions on Microwave Theory and Techniques
abstract: In this article, a novel exploration algorithm for the design and optimization of matching networks (MNs) in power amplifiers (PAs) is presented. The methodology is based on the automatic generation and optimization of both input and output MNs (IMNs/OMNs) according to a set of design rules and constraints that ensure technological feasibility. All candidate MN topologies are optimized and ranked using a unified cost function, enabling the automatic selection of the most suitable solution while facilitating a comprehensive tradeoff analysis among key performance metrics such as power-added efficiency (PAE), gain, bandwidth, physical size, and expected yield. As a result, the designer obtains a global view of the solution space and can objectively compare different MN configurations within a consistent optimization framework. To validate the proposed algorithm, a PA covering 1.1–1.9 GHz—fully encompassing the Galileo navigation bands E5, E6, and L1—and targeting high efficiency with harmonic control, was designed, fabricated, and experimentally characterized.

title: Energy- and AoI-Aware Hierarchical Personalized Federated Learning for Distributed Edge Systems with Selective LLM Module Exchange
authors: Faranak Solat, Joohyung Lee
abstract: Distributed edge systems require federated learning (FL) frameworks that can jointly support timely model updates, user-level personalization, and communication-efficient coordination. However, conventional hierarchical federated learning (HFL) methods mainly focus on reducing communication overhead and often struggle to maintain model freshness and local adaptability in multi-tier environments with heterogeneous data and resource constraints. To address these challenges, we propose an Energy- and Age-of-Information (AoI)-Aware Hierarchical Personalized Federated Learning (EA-HPFL) framework. The proposed method introduces two key ideas: 1) selective module exchange, in which clients communicate only lightweight adapter, head, or other trainable sub-module updates instead of full model parameters; and 2) deterministic AoI- and energy-aware aggregation, in which freshness and energy indicators guide client- and edge-level aggregation. This design enables efficient hierarchical coordination while preserving local personalization under non-IID data distributions. Experimental results on WESAD, HAR, and Amazon-EN show that EA-HPFL provides a favorable trade-off among communication efficiency, update freshness, and personalized performance across diverse edge settings. In particular, the proposed framework maintains competitive accuracy while substantially reducing the overhead associated with full-model exchange, and the revised TinyLlama-based Amazon-EN evaluation further confirms its effectiveness in an LLM-oriented setting. These results demonstrate the practical value of AoI-aware coordination and selective sub-module exchange for scalable and adaptive FL in real-time intelligent edge systems.

title: Performance Analysis of Hybrid-Electric Propulsion Systems for Regional Commuter Aircraft
authors: Gustavo Muller Hauck, Cleverson Bringhenti, Mauricio Andrés Varela Morales, Jesuino Takachi Tomita, Antonio Bruno De Vasconcelos Leitao, Franco Jefferds Dos Santos Silva, Konstantinos Kyprianidis
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: Aviation plays a fundamental and valuable role in the modern world, yet it faces significant challenges, including high fuel costs and substantial environmental pollution. These issues have prompted the aviation industry to establish emission standards and develop less-polluting propulsion systems, such as those utilizing synthetic fuels, fuel cells, and electrification. Among these, electrification holds promise as a potential solution for reducing emissions in commuter aircraft, despite the mass limitations posed by batteries. In this study, a methodology was developed and implemented within in-house software to simulate the performance of a commuter aircraft with a hybrid-electric propulsion system. The analysis focused on key metrics like fuel economy and climb time to cruise altitude. The EMB-120 Brasilia was chosen as the base aircraft for this research. Its long-standing use by the Brazilian Air Force (FAB), the authors' extensive familiarity with its performance, and the availability of experimental data for lift and drag coefficients made it an ideal model for our simulations. To evaluate the performance of the hybrid propulsion system and compare it with the standard case, a gas turbine was utilized as the primary engine. Mathematical models were developed for the PW118 gas turbine, which powers the real aircraft that was considered the standard case, and for the PT6A-68C, which was suggested as a substitute for the hybrid system. To evaluate the aircraft's performance, a standard mission was simulated on a short-range route of 926 km (500 NM), flying at an altitude of 7,620 m (25,000 ft), a common mission that is used by Brazilian Air Force. For hybrid simulations, battery packs were tested with specific energies ranging from 0.125 kWh/kg to 0.750 kWh/kg, in multiples of the initial value. Batteries with 0.250 kWh/kg were considered the current state-of-the-art, while the 0.750 kWh/kg packs represent an extreme upper bound associated with far-future technological developments. The results demonstrated significant performance gains depending on the chosen battery technology and the power split, the hybrid system achieved fuel savings of up to 18% to 19% and reduced climb time by 17% to 25%.

title: Modeling Coincident Peak Pricing in Electricity Markets: Challenges and Peak Shaving Effectiveness
authors: Qian Zhang, Sadie Zhao, Lucy Diao, Conleigh Byers, Yiling Chen, Derya Cansever, Le Xie
publish: IEEE Transactions on Energy Markets, Policy and Regulation
abstract: Coincident Peak (CP) pricing is widely used in U.S. electricity markets to allocate capacity and transmission costs. This paper develops a behavioral game-theoretic framework for CP-driven load shifting that couples a nonlinear cost-allocation model with day-ahead (one-shot) and real-time (sequential-learning) decision processes. We examine two update rules, namely best-response dynamics (BRD) and fictitious-play dynamics (FPD), across continuous and finite action spaces to quantify how flexibility, action resolution, and participation influence peak outcomes. Using ERCOT peak-day data, we find that FPD reliably reduces system peaks, whereas BRD is more variable and can increase peaks under tight-capacity conditions. Finer action resolution improves peak shaving, while the number of participants is largely neutral when aggregate flexibility is fixed. Meanwhile, information-provider signals can induce herding, whereas response-aware or diverse signals improve peak shaving. These results highlight both the potential and limits of CP pricing: smoothing information and enabling granular control are as important as the amount of available flexibility. The framework offers practical guidance for system operators and consumers: For ISOs, broadcasting smoothed CP signals and setting minimum controllable-capacity thresholds enhance coordination. For consumers, greater flexibility and finer control resolution improve both cost savings and peak-shaving performance.

title: Lightweight Privacy-Preserving and Fault-Tolerant Truth Discovery for Mobile Crowdsensing Systems_supp1-3694180.pdf
authors: Xiaofen Wang
abstract: As a paradigm for encouraging users to contribute data spontaneously, mobile crowdsensing (MCS) has received considerable attention recently. It is crucial to evaluate the truthfulness of MCS data by proper truth discovery mechanisms. Although recent truth discovery schemes can determine truthful information, they either provide limited privacy preservation or have heavy computation and communication overheads. Moreover, most of them are not resilient to malicious faults and active attacks. To tackle the above problems, we propose two fault-tolerant and privacy-preserving truth discovery solutions. Our first scheme is mainly used for scenarios with a relatively stable number of users, where participants do not frequently join or leaves. Integrating ring signature with the perturbation technique, we design an anonymous and privacy-preserving truth discovery scheme, namely RsAnonTD, which can achieve privacy preservation and resist active attacks. To address the challenge with dynamically changed workers, we devise a multi-client inner product functional encryption scheme with a lightweight zero-knowledge proof protocol (namely McFeKDeTD) for defending against active attacks. The security analysis shows that both schemes can preserve the privacy of sensory data, weights, and estimated truths while resisting active attacks, thereby guaranteeing fault tolerance. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our designs achieve superior performance than other schemes in terms of accuracy, convergence speed, and system overheads. For example, compared with the state-of-the-art approach RPTD-II, which has a security level comparable to ours, our proposed schemes, RsAnonTD and McFeKDeTD, reduce the computational overheads approximately by 98% and 69%, respectively.

title: Bias- and Frequency-Dependent Optimization of Gate Length for Efficiency in GaN-on-SiC HEMTs With a Fit Predictive Model
authors: Jiongjiong Mo, Beibei Lv, Xinran Chen, Xuran Zhao
publish: IEEE Microwave and Wireless Technology Letters
abstract: The efficiency of GaN-on-SiC high-electron-mobility transistors (HEMTs) is limited by the combined influence of the trap-induced current collapse, knee-voltage walkout, and parasitic capacitances. While the gate-length scaling is known to affect these mechanisms, the optimum geometry for maximum power-added efficiency (PAE) under the realistic bias and frequency conditions has remained unclear. Here, we investigate the gate length impacts on efficiency. Comprehensive DC, pulsed-IV, small-signal, and large-signal measurements reveal a nonmonotonic dependence of efficiency, from which a bias-frequency regime map for gate-length optimization has been established, and a predictive model that links PAE to small-signal f<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">max</sub> and applied bias was introduced. The model reproduces measured data with high accuracy and offers a compact route to efficiency prediction from routine RF characterization. Together, the regime map and predictive model provide a physically grounded framework for bias- and frequency-aware design of GaN HEMTs for millimeter-wave power amplification.

title: Evaluation of Commercially Available MHz Magnetic Materials Using a Virtual Datasheet Framework
authors: Burkhard Ulrich
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: The adoption of GaN and SiC devices enables power converters to operate in the MHz range, requiring careful selection of magnetic materials to manage core loss and improve power density. To address the lack of a comprehensive overview of available materials, this article presents a comparative evaluation of 45 commercially available materials from 15 different manufacturers, all specified for operation above 1 MHz. An interactive virtual datasheet approach, based on prior work but extended to the MHz range, is introduced to process manufacturer-supplied data. The tool enables performance evaluation and direct comparison of core loss characteristics across materials using power loss density data and standard metrics such as the performance factor. The results provide practical guidance for selecting high-frequency core materials in power converter applications where core loss is a key constraint in magnetic component design.

title: A Noval Integrated-Core Lateral-Flux Coupled Inductor for Vertical Power Delivery in Voltage Regulator Modules
authors: Yu-Chen Chung, Chien-Chun Chan, Huang-Jen Chiu, Yu-Chen Chang
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: The design of voltage regulator modules is becoming increasingly challenging, and the size of magnetic components remains a critical factor in achieving high power density. The zero-bias trans-inductor voltage regulator (ZBTLVR) topology eliminates the DC flux among multiple magnetic cores, enabling opportunities for highly integrated implementations. This work analyzes the equivalent circuit model of the ZBTLVR and proposes a five-phase integrated magnetic core based on a lateral-flux coupled inductor for vertical power delivery applications. Finite-element simulations using ANSYS MAXWELL are performed to investigate the magnetic characteristics and phase coupling behavior of the proposed core, and a modified equivalent circuit model is introduced to account for the coupling effects. The simulation results are consistent with theoretical analysis. Finally, experimental validation of the magnetic core is carried out under 12 V input, 1 V output, 1 MHz switching frequency, and 200 A output current.

title: A Calibrated Memorization Index (MI) for Detecting Training Data Leakage in Generative MRI Models
authors: Yash Deo, Yan Jia, Toni Lassila, Victoria J Hodge, Alejandro F Frangi, Chenghao Qian, Siyuan Kang, Ibrahim Habli
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Image generative models are known to duplicate images from the training data as part of their outputs, which can lead to privacy concerns when used for medical image generation. We propose a calibrated per-sample metric for detecting memorization and duplication of training data. Our metric uses image features extracted using an MRI foundation model, aggregates multi-layer whitened nearest-neighbor similarities, and maps them to a bounded Overfit/Novelty Index (ONI) and Memorization Index (MI) scores. Across three MRI datasets with controlled duplication percentages and typical image augmentations, our metric robustly detects duplication and provides more consistent metric values across datasets. At the sample level, our metric achieves near-perfect detection of duplicates.

title: Morphology-Embedded Graph Optimization for EM Neuron Tracing
authors: Qihua Chen, Xuejin Chen
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Reconstructing neurons from large-scale electron microscopy (EM) 3D images for connectomics is a significant challenge, particularly for neurons with complex morphologies. Current automatic reconstruction pipelines first generate oversegmented fragments, followed by tracing algorithms that merge them into more complete neuronal structures. However, these merging approaches rely on local features, failing to model long-range dependencies and global structural constraints, which often leads to biologically implausible morphology. To address this, we propose the MorphologyEmbedded Graph Optimization Network for global neuron tracing. The model employs a path-tracing-based message passing mechanism, analogous to human experts, to model long-range connectivity, overcoming the local limitations of traditional graph-cut methods. Additionally, it fuses multiscale morphological representations into the graph to model fine-grained and global biological plausibility. Experiments demonstrate this method significantly reduces reconstruction errors compared to the Multicut algorithm and also outperforms state-of-the-art graph neural networks, enhancing the biological plausibility of the results.

title: Near-Optimal Reinforcement Learning With Shuffle Differential Privacy
authors: Shaojie Bai, Mohammad Sadegh Talebi, Chengcheng Zhao, Peng Cheng, Jiming Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network Science and Engineering
abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) is a powerful tool for sequential decision-making, but its application is often hindered by privacy concerns arising from its sensitive interaction data. This challenge is particularly acute in advanced networked systems, where learning from operational and user data can expose systems to privacy inference attacks. Existing differential privacy (DP) models for RL are often inadequate: the centralized model requires a fully trusted server, creating a single point of failure risk, while the local model incurs significant performance degradation that renders it unsuitable for many networked applications. This paper addresses this gap by leveraging the emerging shuffle model of privacy, an intermediate trust model that provides strong privacy guarantees without a centralized trust assumption. We present Shuffle Differentially Private Policy Elimination (SDP-PE), the first generic policy elimination-based algorithm for episodic RL under the shuffle model. Our method introduces a novel exponential batching schedule and a “forgetting” mechanism to balance the competing demands of privacy and learning performance. Our analysis shows that SDP-PE achieves a near-optimal regret bound, demonstrating a superior privacy-regret trade-off with utility comparable to the centralized model while significantly outperforming the local model. The numerical experiments also corroborate our theoretical results and demonstrate the effectiveness of SDP-PE. This work establishes the viability of the shuffle model for secure data-driven decision-making in networked systems.

title: LNO piezoelectric resonator temperature characteristic
authors: Alex Marques, Martin Lott, Alexandre Rheinhardt, Marie Bousquet, Gaëtan Perez, Ghislain Despesse
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: One of today’s challenges in the field of power electronics is to increase power-density, which is mainly achieved by increasing the switching frequency. New semiconductor technologies, such as GaN HEMT devices, allow us to increase the switching frequency and achieved greater power-density. Nevertheless, limits are reached with passive materials such as inductors. In this context, piezoelectric resonators (PR) are used to offer an interesting alternative compare to inductor beyond MHz switching frequency. Nevertheless, there is a lack of information on the practical limits in terms of the maximum current that Lithium Niobate (LNO) PRs can withstand in steady state with and without air cooling and on their frequency shift under self-heating induced by current. This paper aims to provide insights on the temperature-dependent shift in the characteristics of LNO resonators and to quantify the current limit of PRs under various practical operating conditions.

title: Design and Simulation of Regenerative Braking Strategy for BLDC Motor with Buck Converter
authors: Veeraiah. Nagulla
publish: 2026 International Conference on Multidisciplinary Innovations For Smart &amp; Sustainable Future (MISSF)
abstract: Regenerative braking act as a pivotal role in enhancing the efficiency of electric vehicles (EVs) by recovering the kinetic energy otherwise lost during conventional braking and redirect it back to the battery. This paper presents a detailed simulation study of regenerative braking in a Brushless DC (BLDC) motor-driven electric vehicle using MATLAB/Simulink. The model integrates a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$500 ~\mathrm{W}, 48 ~\mathrm{V}$</tex> BLDC motor, a six-cell battery pack rated at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 5 0 ~ A h}$</tex>, and a voltage source inverter who is governed by hall sensor-based commutation logic. A control approach combining PI speed regulation and braking logic ensure seamless transition between motoring and regenerative modes. During normal operation, the motor speed sustained at 3000 RPM under a reference command. At braking onset, the motor operates as a generator, with the back electromotive force (EMF) exceeding the terminal voltage, thereby channel current back into the battery. The simulation results shows that battery current become negative during braking, confirming the recharging process, while the state of charge (SOC) and terminal voltage increases correspondingly. Torque reversal in the BLDC motor further validate energy recovery. This study confirms the feasibility of integrating regenerative braking in BLDC motor-driven EVs, providing more energy efficiency, extended driving range, and reduced dependency on external charging. The proposed framework also highlights the significance of efficient control logic for practical EV application.

title: Harmonic-Fused Dual-Domain Network for Low-Concentration Magnetic Particle Imaging Reconstruction
authors: Haicheng Du, Guanghui Li, Ziwei Chen, Penghua Zhai, Yu An, Jie Tian
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Reconstruction of low-concentration magnetic particle imaging (MPI) is a critical challenge for clinical translation, due to low signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), severe artifacts, and blurred details in the reconstructed harmonic images. The ISBI-2026 LCR-MPI Challenge is focused on achieving high-fidelity fifth-harmonic reconstruction from low-concentration data. The network takes normalized third- and fifth-harmonic images as dual-channel input and integrates a spatially adaptive denormalization-based fusion module to leverage the structural prior of the third harmonic for guiding the restoration of noisy fifth-harmonic images. To further improve the signal-to-noise ratio, a spatial-frequency dual-domain fusion scheme based on a symmetric encoder-decoder architecture with fast Fourier convolution is adopted. Additionally, a learnable gated skip connection is incorporated to adaptively regulate network information flow and enhance feature representation capability. Experiments on the challenge dataset demonstrate that DuLCR-Net outperforms the baselines, providing an effective solution for low-concentration MPI reconstruction and advancing its clinical applicability.

title: A Computationally Efficient Method of Power Module Junction Temperature Estimate Considering Ripple Elements for Yearlong Reliability Simulation of Power Electronics Converters
authors: Shahabuddin Khan, Purushottam Khadka, Siraj Ahmed Shahnewaz, Liwei Wang, Buck Brown, Zheyu Zhang
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: The reliability of power electronic converters is strongly influenced by the power module, where junction-temperature magnitude and cycling accelerate the dominant aging mechanisms in semiconductor devices. Consequently, accurate junction-temperature estimation is essential, and electro-thermal modeling is widely applied on the basis of the power-device loss model and the thermal impedance of the module packaging and cooling system. Previous studies have focused on developing various models to improve accuracy, while few studies emphasize computation efficiency, which is equally important considering reliability assessment with a yearlong mission profile. This paper proposes a computationally efficient algorithm to compute the junction temperature of the power module with transient ripple components. First, a decoupled framework is introduced where the loss model, dependent only on junction temperature, is separated from the thermal model, eliminating the need for co-simulation. Second, an algorithm to decouple the average temperature through a one-dimensional thermal-resistance model and the ripple component through a thermal-impedance model, along with an adaptive simulation-time-resolution setting is proposed. Finally, a case study of a T-type power module for solar applications demonstrates the effectiveness of the proposed method, achieving a normalized root mean square error of 1.51% and a simulation time speedup of 700 ×.

title: AI Transformation: Ready or Already?
authors: Felix Dobslaw, Lucas Gren, Markus Borg, Erik Sterner
abstract: Generative AI promises substantial productivity gains, but many initiatives fail. This column argues that outcomes depend on readiness, not technology alone. We examine AI transformation requirements across organizational, individual, and technological dimensions, highlighting why engineering practices, human factors, and strategic clarity determine success.

title: Anchor-Driven Compact Gaussian Splatting for Dynamic Scene Reconstruction
authors: He Huang, Qi Yang, Mufan Liu, Wenjie Huang, Yiling Xu, Zhu Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Existing 4D Gaussian Splatting methods typically rely on per-Gaussian deformation from a canonical space to target frames, which overlooks the strong redundancy among spatially and temporally adjacent Gaussian primitives and leads to suboptimal efficiency. To address this limitation, we propose ADC-GS++, an anchor-driven compact Gaussian splatting frame work for efficient and high-quality dynamic scene reconstruction. Specifically, ADC-GS++ organizes Gaussian primitives into an anchor-based canonical representation, enabling attribute sharing across local regions. To efficiently model dynamic scenes, we introduce a static–dynamic decomposition mechanism and further employ a coarse-to-fine deformation strategy driven by dynamic anchors at multiple granularities. In addition, a unified rate-distortion optimization is adopted to achieve a balanced trade-off between storage efficiency and reconstruction fidelity. Furthermore, a temporal significance-based anchor refinement strategy is employed to dynamically grow and prune anchors, allowing robust adaptation to complex and large-scale motions. Extensive experiments on multiple real-world dynamic scene datasets demonstrate that ADC-GS++ significantly improves rendering speed over deformation-based approaches by 300% 700%, while maintaining competitive rendering quality. More over, ADC-GS++ achieves a more favorable rate-distortion trade off, resulting in substantially reduced storage consumption across different bitrate settings.

title: Truthful and Trustworthy IoT AI Agents via Immediate-Penalty Enforcement under Approximate VCG Mechanisms
authors: Xun Shao, Ryuuto Shimizu, Zhi Liu, Kaoru Ota, Mianxiong Dong
abstract: The deployment of autonomous AI agents in Internet of Things (IoT) energy systems requires decision-making mechanisms that remain robust, efficient, and trustworthy under real-time constraints and imperfect monitoring. While reinforcement learning enables adaptive prosumer behaviors, ensuring economic consistency and preventing strategic manipulation remain open challenges, particularly when sensing noise or partial observability degrades the operator’s ability to verify actions. This paper introduces a trust-enforcement framework for IoT energy trading that combines an α-approximate Vickrey–Clarke–Groves (VCG) double auction with an immediate one-shot penalty. Unlike reputation- or history-based approaches, the proposed mechanism restores truthful reporting within a single round, even when allocation accuracy is approximate and monitoring is noisy. We theoretically characterize the incentive gap induced by approximation and derive a penalty threshold that guarantees truthful bidding under bounded sensing errors. To evaluate learning-enabled prosumers, we embed the mechanism into a multi-agent reinforcement learning environment reflecting stochastic generation, dynamic loads, and heterogeneous trading opportunities. Experiments show that improved allocation accuracy consistently reduces deviation incentives, the required penalty matches analytical predictions, and learned bidding behaviors remain stable and interpretable despite imperfect monitoring. These results demonstrate that lightweight penalty designs can reliably align strategic IoT agents with socially efficient energy-trading outcomes.

title: SE-Blackboard: A Shared-State Architecture for Multi-Agent Software Engineering Pipelines
authors: Erxi Liu, Qiang Zhu, Xinru Dong
abstract: Multi-agent large language model (LLM) systems are increasingly used for software engineering tasks, yet the role of communication architecture in these systems remains poorly understood. Most existing approaches rely on message passing, where agents communicate through sequential natural language summaries, potentially introducing information loss across pipeline stages. This paper introduces SE-Blackboard, a modular framework that applies a shared-state Blackboard architecture to multi-agent software engineering pipelines.We conduct controlled experiments on 50 SWE-bench Lite issues, comparing three communication architectures: message passing, shared-state blackboard, and a hybrid design. Our results show that communication architecture significantly affects information preservation and intermediate pipeline performance. The blackboard configuration improves Coder-stage information fidelity (IFS) by 62% compared with message passing (0.586 vs. 0.362), which translates into a 27.5 percentage-point improvement in correct file targeting (82.6% vs. 55.1%). However, once the correct file is identified, all configurations converge to a ∼22% conditional resolve rate, revealing a downstream bottleneck in LLM diff generation. As a result, the overall resolve-rate improvement is modest (16% vs. 12%) despite large upstream gains. These findings highlight communication architecture as an important design dimension for multi-agent software engineering systems and suggest that improvements in upstream information quality translate into task-level gains only when downstream generation bottlenecks are addressed.

title: IEEE CAS Society Meets Industry in Europe [CASS Industry Outreach]
authors: Francois Rivet, Patricia Desgreys, Sonal Shreya, Mustak Erhan Yalcin, Andreas Demosthenous, Laura Bégon-Lours, Clément Dufossée, Roberto La Rosa, Dick Carrillo Melgarejo, Maide Bucolo, Costas N Psychalinos, Tomasz Marciniak, Florence Podevin
