Numerical results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method in diverse scenarios.

title: Optimal Temporal SNR Window for Multi-Sensor Fusion: Theory and Jamming-Aware GNN Architecture for Electronic Warfare
authors: Simiao Fei, Xueer Hao, Yinfeng Wu, Lin Huo, Huicun Li
abstract: Multi-object tracking in electronic warfare (EW) relies heavily on temporal fusion—accumulating observations across frames through filtering or recurrent models. Yet this strategy relies on the premise that inter-frame data association is sufficiently accurate to benefit from accumulation. We challenge this assumption by analyzing the temporal signal-to-noise ratio SNRt = Δp/σ (inter-frame displacement over measurement error) and deriving an Optimal SNR Window Theorem. Under nearest-neighbor association, temporal modeling is harmful when SNRt ≲ 5%, beneficial between ≈5% and ≈45%, and saturated beyond; these boundaries are operating-region markers under the assumed association model. In EW scenarios with heavy jamming and false-target injection, SNRt can fall to ∼2.3%—well inside the harmful region.We therefore design a single-frame graph attention network (GAT) with jamming-aware gating that bypasses temporal association entirely. On a multi-sensor benchmark (900 frames, 37.5% jammer ratio, 1 200 injected false targets), the proposed method achieves OSPA of 2717±449 and MOTA of 14.0%±9.3% across seven seeds, outperforming both classical filters (JPDA, LMB/GLMB) and all temporal GNN variants, which produce negative MOTA. A sweep across six noise levels confirms the predicted non-monotonic benefit curve, peaking at SNRt = 11.5% with negative gain at the 2.3% operating point.

title: End-To-End Imitation Learning for Socially Aware Navigation using Memory-based Trajectory and Action Planning_supp1-3694511.mp4
authors: ALEJANDRO OLIVAS

title: Degradation-Adaptive Denoising: Aligning Diffusion Models with Physics of Video Snapshot Compressive Imaging
authors: Mingjin Zhang, Mingrui Li, Jie Guo, Yunsong Li
abstract: Video Snapshot Compressive Imaging (SCI) captures multiple video frames in a single exposure, enabling efficient reconstruction of high-speed scenes for motion analysis and event detection. Existing SCI in coded aperture compressive temporal imaging (CACTI) methods predominantly rely on feedforward deep networks with fixed denoising strategies. However, they lack alignment with the SCI physical inverse model and struggle to balance motion detail recovery and static background smoothing. In this paper, we propose PCD-Diffusion for Video SCI, the first diffusion-based reconstruction framework for Video SCI, which reformulates the inverse problem as a progressive denoising process. Specifically, we design a Physically-Constrained Dynamic Diffusion (PCD-Diffusion) model, introducing a region-adaptive diffusion schedule and spatiotemporal residual estimation. This method explicitly aligns the denoising process with SCI’s spatially non-uniform and temporally evolving residual distribution. Additionally, a motion prior-guided diffusion schedule and a Gauss-guided spatiotemporal adaptive residual estimation dynamically steer the denoising trajectory, ensuring accurate motion detail restoration and physically consistent reconstructions. Extensive results on simulated and real datasets verify the superior reconstruction fidelity and temporal coherence of the proposed PCD-Diffusion framework over existing approaches. Code will be released upon publication.

title: Effects of Heat Treatment on Microstructure, Thermal Transport and Stability in SRF Niobium
authors: Marissa Salisbury, Santosh Chetri, Pashupati Dhakal
abstract: Efficient heat transfer and thermal stability are critical for preventing thermal quench and ensuring the high performance and reliability of superconducting radiofrequency (SRF) cavities, especially under high rf fields. In this work, we investigate thermal conductivity and heat capacity of high-purity cold-worked niobium used in SRF applications, with a particular focus on the effects of high-temperature heat treatments. Our measurements reveal a pronounced sensitivity of thermal transport and thermodynamic properties to the underlying microstructure such as grain size and dislocation density. The results show the critical role of controlled heat treatment processes in optimizing the thermal performance of SRF niobium, providing valuable insights for improving cavity fabrication and processing.

title: Adaptive Optimal Output Regulation Based on Adaptive Dynamic Programming and Output-Feedback Hybrid Iteration: Theory and Applications Across Diverse Time Scales From PMDC Motor Drives to IBR-Based Microgrids
authors: Sitong Liu, Masoud Davari, Weinan Gao, Omar Qasem, Tianyou Chai
abstract: This article investigates the adaptive optimal output-feedback control problem for discrete-time multiagent linear systems. An adaptive dynamic programming (ADP) algorithm, hybrid iteration (HI), is proposed to solve the adaptive optimal output-feedback control problem. The primary contribution of this work is the advancement of an approach to addressing adaptive optimal output regulation problems for multiagent systems via output-feedback control. Unlike existing ADP-based strategies, such as policy iteration (PI) and value iteration (VI), the proposed output-feedback HI strategy learns the optimal output-feedback control policy over two phases. In the first phase, a data-driven scheme is deployed to learn an admissible output-feedback control policy based on online data. In the second phase, given the admissible control policy, the optimal output-feedback control policy is learned via PI with an accelerated convergence rate. Illustrative examples demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed output-feedback HI approach to controlling systems with slow and fast dynamics, including long and short time scales (or time constants), respectively, are provided and studied. In this regard, experimental results from rapid control prototyping of a permanent-magnet DC motor drive utilizing a DC–DC buck converter (as an example of a system with slow dynamics, or equivalently, with long time scales) are presented. Additionally, testing results from power hardware-in-the-loop simulations of a modern microgrid utilizing inverter-based resources (as an example of a system with fast dynamics, or equivalently, with short time scales) reveal the efficacy and practicality of the proposed ADP-based adaptive optimal output regulation employing HI.

title: Accurate and Efficient Equivalent Circuit Modeling for Single-Port SAW Resonators
authors: Tinglun Ao, Chen Chen, Guopeng Hui, Haotian Liu, Minglei Li
abstract: An optimized equivalent circuit modeling framework for single-port surface acoustic wave (SAW) resonators is proposed in this article, addressing key limitations of conventional Butterworth–Van Dyke (BVD) and modified BVD (MBVD) models in representing spurious resonances and packaging parasitics. It incorporates: 1) simplified parasitic circuits to quantify packaging effects and 2) explicit resonance loops for spurious modes. The extraction of equivalent circuit parameters within this framework is guided by an iterative strategy that combines analytical derivation and least-squares optimization. Model validation across 15 commercial SAW resonators (303–868 MHz) shows significantly improved precision: the fit MBVD model reduces root mean squared error (RMSE) by roughly one order of magnitude, while the extended MBVD (EMBVD) further lowers RMSE by 30%–50%. The parasitic-integrated BVD (PBVD) model achieves high-accuracy packaging characterization with a relative RMSE (RRMSE) of 15.1% and root-mean-squared relative error (RMSRE) of 0.51%. In practice, EMBVD prevents oscillation mode hopping by capturing spurious resonances, while PBVD guides the optimization of devices’ packaging and PCB layout. Furthermore, the extracted equivalent circuit parameters provide clear criteria for sensor-specific resonator selection: balancing sensitivity against resolution for mass-loading sensing, or prioritizing low motional resistance (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${R}_{\text {m}}\text {)}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> and static capacitance (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${C}_{{0}}\text {)}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> to ensure oscillation stability for viscoelastic sensing. The proposed framework is also extendable to two-port SAW resonators, further broadening its applicability. This work thus facilitates the transition from precise resonator characterization to robust system design.

title: Forward Recovery Aware of Antiparallel Diodes for GaN-Based Synchronous Rectifier Turn-On Delay in MHz-Class LLC
authors: Dong Jiao, Zhengming Hou, Jih-Sheng Lai
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: Synchronous-rectifier (SR) turn-on delay becomes a non-negligible loss mechanism in MHz-class GaN LLC converters because reverse current must initially flow through the GaN HEMT’s third-quadrant conduction path, which exhibits a relatively large dynamic drop. A widely used mitigation is to place an antiparallel diode across the SR device so that the reverse current can commutate to the diode before the SR channel turns on. However, experimental results often show inconsistent outcomes, and the underlying reason for the diode’s success or failure has not been clearly identified. This work demonstrates that forward recovery, the transient voltage overshoot that occurs during diode turn-on, is the dominant factor determining whether current commutation can occur within the limited SR turn-on interval. The SR delay is decomposed into two distinct intervals. Mode-1 is governed by loop parasitics and C<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">oss</inf> discharge, during which no diode can conduct regardless of its characteristics. Mode-2 begins when V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">ds</inf> reaches zero; only in this interval can the antiparallel diode take over current. A diode is effective only if its forward-recovery peak is lower than the GaN body-diode effective drop under the operating current and di/dt. A forward-recovery measurement method is presented to isolate intrinsic diode behavior from inductive overshoot. Hardware tests on an 800-kHz, 400-W LLC prototype confirm approximately 1 W loss reduction using a low-inductance SiC SBD, while ultrafast PN diodes provide no benefit due to large forward-recovery peaks. The study establishes a practical, physics-based selection criterion for antiparallel diodes in GaN SR applications.

title: Adapt: Multibeamformer with Tunable Weight Fusion and Patch-Wise Learning for Ultrasound Imaging
authors: Gopika Gopikrishna, Mahesh Raveendranatha Panicke, Timothy Li, Ng Aik Ben, Simon Chong-Wee Se
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: In the recent past, deep learning (DL)-based beamformers have been employed to accelerate adaptive beamforming for ultrasound imaging. However, most of these approaches are trained to mimic a particular beamformer type for a predefined depth of imaging and require complete retraining to adapt to a new beamformer and depth. In this work, we propose a novel deep learning framework, ADAPT (Adaptive Depth-Agnostic Patch-wise Tunable-multibeamformer), that employs a depth-wise patching strategy for computational efficiency and depth-independent learning. In order to develop a beamformer agnostic framework, the proposed approach first trains identical neural networks separately to derive the individual beamforming algorithm model weights, which are then fused via a tunable weighted averaging mechanism. This provides clinicians with dynamic and adaptable diagnostic perspectives, blending the acceptability of delay and sum (DAS) beamformer with the contrast of filtered delay multiply and sum (FDMAS) beamformer and the resolution of minimum variance (MV) beamformer simultaneously at very low complexity. ADAPT achieves 73 % speedup in training, 94 % reduction in computations and 94 % reduction in activation memory over a model trained over full depth.

title: Thermal Buckling Analysis of Simply Supported Functionally Graded Materials Plates for Aeronautical Applications
authors: Abdelkrim Moufakkir, Saad Abouzahir, Hassan Belahrach, Ahmed Oulad-Said, Abdellah Elbouzidi, Mustafa Benyoucef
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Global Aeronautical Engineering and Satellite Technology (GAST)
abstract: In this paper, the thermal buckling behavior of small size and wide thin and thick functionally graded materials (FGM) plates under constant thermal loading is explored in this paper. The materials properties are reflected to be continuously changing across the thickness as well as being temperature-dependent in both linear and nonlinear ways. The equations governing equilibrium are obtained according to the classical and first order theories of shear deformation. Analytical simplified case solutions and a finite difference numerical solution method, which is written in MATLAB, are used to evaluate critical buckling temperatures in isotropic and anisotropic configurations with simplified cases and more complex geometries, respectively. Systematic examination of the effect of the geometric parameters such as aspect ratio (a/b) and thickness ratio (a/h) is done. The findings illustrate that thermal stability is very sensitive to the slenderness of the plates and their material gradient. The accuracy and reliability of the proposed formulation are confirmed with the help of a comparative validation with already existing literature.

title: Progressive Orthogonal Multimodal Similarity Learning for Metabolite-Disease Association Prediction
authors: Qiao Ning, Yanpeng Liu, Yuanjie Li, Shaohang Qiao, Lu Jiang, Qian Ma, Pengfei Wang, Hui Li, Shikai Guo
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: The identification of potential associations between metabolites and diseases is crucial for understanding the onset and progression of diseases. Although numerous methods have been developed to predict metabolite-disease associations (MDAs), few methods fully mine the complementary information between different similarity relation or learn higher-order relationships between metabolites or diseases. To address these challenges, we propose a method for MDAs prediction with Progressive Orthogonal Multimodal Similarity Learning (POMSL). POMSL first constructs two hypergraphs within single-modal similarity with KNN and K-means to capture higher-order complex relationships. Then, hierarchical contrastive learning is applied to enhance the consistency of multi-similarity features by performing contrastive learning for inter-similarity and intra-similarity views. Next, a progressive orthogonal multimodal similarity integration strategy is developed to ensure the effective fusion of cross-similarity complementary information and to enhance the divergence of multi-similarity features. Finally, a bilinear decoder is used to predict MDAs. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that POMSL performs excellently in MDA prediction and, more importantly, validate the effectiveness of hierarchical contrastive learning and progressive orthogonal multimodal similarity learning. The source code and dataset are available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/1521250466LYP/POMSL</uri>.

title: Scalable Energy-Aware Task Scheduling on Heterogeneous Voltage Frequency Islands: From Optimal ILP to Practical Heuristics with Real-World Validation
authors: Sanchit, Sakshi, Maanas Singal, Dheeraj Kumar Dhaked, V.L. Narayanan, Jagpreet Singh
abstract: This paper presents a comprehensive approach to energy-efficient DAG scheduling on Voltage Frequency Islands (VFI) architectures, combining optimal Integer Linear Programming (ILP) formulation with a novel Energy-Aware List Scheduling with Dynamic Slot Reuse (EALS-DSR) heuristic. The work addresses the critical challenge of minimizing makespan and overall energy consumption through intelligent task allocation, voltage/frequency assignments, and scheduling optimization. Our contributions include: (1) a detailed ILP formulation with theoretical guarantees for optimal solutions, (2) the EALS-DSR heuristic providing near-optimal solutions with polynomial complexity O(n²p), (3) comprehensive experimental validation on LUD, FFT, and Random DAGs, and (4) real-world molecular dynamics simulation case study. To minimize makespan and overall energy consumption, a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) can be scheduled on voltage frequency islands by first splitting the available processing units into numerous islands with different voltage and frequency levels. This paper presents a comprehensive approach combining Integer Linear Programming (ILP) with a novel Energy-Aware List Scheduling with Dynamic Slot Reuse (EALS-DSR) heuristic. The proposed methodology includes four main components: (a) DAG modeling, (b) job allocation based on slots, (c) voltage/frequency assignments to each slot, and (d) scheduling optimization. We extend the original ILP formulation with theoretical analysis, comprehensive experimental validation, and real-world case studies including molecular dynamics simulation. Results demonstrate that the ILP approach improves performance by 10-24% over existing methods, while EALS-DSR achieves 15-25% energy savings with excellent scalability for large-scale problems. The molecular dynamics case study shows 37.4% energy reduction and 3.86× efficiency improvement, validating practical applicability.

title: SkyLLM: Enabling Trustworthy UAV RF Surveillance With Foundation Models for Open-World Signal Recognition
authors: Weijie Zhang, Guan Gui, Xianbin Wang, Hikmet Sari, Fumiyuki Adachi
abstract: UAV RF surveillance is becoming a critical layer of low-altitude airspace security, yet most fingerprinting pipelines still assume that every signal belongs to a known class. In open-world deployments, where new platforms, protocol updates, and changing propagation conditions are routine, that assumption makes conventional classifiers brittle and often overconfident on unseen emitters. Foundation models offer a compelling alternative because their pretrained priors can support richer sequence understanding beyond task-specific training. We present SkyLLM, a foundation-model-driven framework that aligns RF time-series with the token space of a frozen LLM through patch-based embedding, cross-channel fusion, and prompt conditioning, so that waveform fragments can be interpreted in a representation space better suited to novelty-aware decision making. Experiments on real UAV RF data show that SkyLLM preserves strong discrimination while delivering markedly more reliable unknown rejection than lightweight baselines across increasingly open settings. More broadly, the study suggests that future trusted spectrum intelligence will depend not only on better classifiers, but on representation layers that remain stable as the airspace evolves. SkyLLM therefore offers a practical path toward 6G-era UAV monitoring systems that must combine recognition, uncertainty awareness, and operational trust.

title: DNN-Koopman-MPC Strategy for a Nonuniform Quay Crane: Theory and Experimental
authors: Huapeng Zhang, Wei Xie, Weidong Zhang, Hao Jiang, Bin Zhao
abstract: This article presents a novel real-time trajectory tracking strategy for nonuniform quay crane systems, aiming to suppress container swing and enhance transportation efficiency. The primary contribution of this work is threefold. First, it introduces a deep neural network (DNN)-based framework within the generalized Koopman operator theory to globally linearize the highly nonlinear and nonuniform crane dynamics. This approach overcomes the limitations of traditional local linearization and manual dictionary selection in existing Koopman methods, leading to a more accurate and generalizable linear predictor. Second, a model predictive control (MPC) scheme is developed based on this DNN-Koopman model, enabling multivariable constraint handling for safe and optimal operation. Third, to address the computational challenge of real-time MPC optimization under hardware constraints, the quantum-behaved particle swarm optimization algorithm is integrated, ensuring fast convergence to feasible and near-optimal solutions. The stability of the closed-loop system is rigorously proven using the Lyapunov theory. Extensive hardware experiments on a 1:9.675 scale Shanghai Zhenhua Heavy Industries Company Limited quay crane prototype demonstrate that the proposed DNN-Koopman-MPC strategy achieves rapid and stable trajectory tracking while effectively suppressing swing, even under disturbances.

title: IEEE Bulgaria Section Jt. Chapter CAS04/SSC37 Hosts CASS Distinguished Lecturer Prof. Ricardo Reis [CASS Chapter Highlights]
authors: Valeri Mladenov
abstract: As part of the IEEE Circuits and Systems Society Distinguished Lecturer (DL) Program, Prof. Ricardo Reis visited Bulgaria in November 2025 and delivered two invited lectures hosted by the Bulgarian CASS Chapter and Bulgarian universities. Invited by Prof. Valeri Mladenov, Chair of the IEEE Bulgaria Section Joint Chapter CAS04/SSC37, the lectures addressed both emerging trends and advanced methodologies in microelectronics and physical design automation. The events attracted strong interest from the academic community and contributed to strengthening collaboration between the IEEE Circuits and Systems community and academic institutions in Bulgaria.

title: SLD: Segmentation-Based Landmark Detection for Spinal Ligaments
authors: Lara Blomenkamp, Ivanna Kramer, Sabine Bauer, Theresa Schöche
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: In biomechanical modeling, the representation of ligament attachments is crucial for a realistic simulation of the forces acting between the vertebrae. These forces are typically modeled as vectors connecting ligament landmarks on adjacent vertebrae, making precise identification of these landmarks a key requirement for constructing reliable spine models. Existing automated detection methods are either limited to specific spinal regions or lack sufficient accuracy. This work presents a novel approach for detecting spinal ligament landmarks, which first performs shape-based segmentation of 3D vertebrae and subsequently applies domain-specific rules to identify different types of attachment points. The proposed method outperforms existing approaches by achieving high accuracy and demonstrating strong generalization across all spinal regions. Validation on two independent spinal datasets from multiple patients yielded a mean absolute error (MAE) of 0.7 mm and a root mean square error (RMSE) of 1.1 mm.

title: Design and Implementation of Three-Phase Six-Switch Power Factor Corrector With DC-Link Capacitor Voltage Balance
authors: Shen Yan-Chen, Tsorng-Juu Liang, Wen-Chung Chen, Kai-Hui Chen, Kenneth Hong Yung Sheng
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: High-power power factor correction (PFC) topologies are becoming more significant gradually. Among these topologies, three-phase six-switch power factor corrector has the advantages of less components and a simple control method. However, the topology suffers from voltage imbalance problem between DC bus capacitors. In this paper, the average current mode control is implemented to achieve PFC by sensing the inductors feedback signals. Moreover, the voltage error signals between two DC bus capacitors are used to adjust the DC offset level of the sinusoidal modulation signal for achieving voltage balance between the DC bus capacitors. Finally, an experimental prototype of three-phase six-switch power factor corrector is implemented with AC input phase voltage of 220 V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">rms</inf> / 60 Hz, DC bus voltage of 800 V, switching frequency of 50 kHz, and a rated power of 10 kW. The experimental results show that the maximum efficiency is 98.5% at 8 kW output power condition. Additionally, when injecting imbalance compensation at full load 10 kW, the total current harmonic distortion is 4.7% with a power factor of 0.991. Also, the bus voltage imbalance problem is reduced significantly.

title: A Multi-Mode PFM/APWM Controlled Modular Resonant Converter for Magnetron Applications
authors: Reza Emamalipour, John Lam
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: A 300VDC/4kVDC 1600W modular Parallel-Input Series-Output (PISO) resonant converter has been designed for the Magnetron application. CLL resonant converters have been used in the high voltage circuit and an LLC resonant circuit has been utilized in the low voltage circuit to power up the Filament winding. To facilitate the design of the converter and achieve a high voltage level while meeting the isolation requirements, the power and the output voltage of the high-voltage (HV) terminal are split between multiple modules and each HV module generates 1kV at the output. To reduce the number of semiconductor devices, each HV module consists of a half-bridge inverter and a voltage-doubler diode rectifier. In addition, to reduce the overall size of the converter, planar magnetics along with a high switching frequency have been used in the resonant tank. Furthermore, to achieve a smooth soft-start and a wide voltage gain profile throughout the whole loading spectrum, a multi-mode PFM/APWM operation has been incorporated in the design of the control system. All the switches and diodes are able to achieve soft-switching to enhance the circuit efficiency. Experimental results are also provided on a proof-of-concept prototype to highlight the merits of the proposed converter.

title: Resource-Efficient Automatic Refinement of Segmentations via Weak Supervision from Light Feedback
authors: Alix de Langlais, Benjamin Billot, Théo Aguilar Vidal, Marc-Olivier Gauci, Hervé Delingette
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Delineating anatomical regions is a key task in medical image analysis. Manual segmentation achieves high accuracy but is labor-intensive and prone to variability, thus prompting the development of automated approaches. Recently, a breadth of foundation models has enabled automated segmentations across diverse anatomies and imaging modalities, but these may not always meet the clinical accuracy standards. While segmentation refinement strategies can improve performance, current methods depend on heavy user interactions or require fully supervised segmentations for training. Here, we present SCORE (Segmentation COrrection from Regional Evaluations), a weakly supervised framework that learns to refine mask predictions only using light feedback during training. Specifically, instead of relying on dense training image annotations, SCORE introduces a novel loss that leverages region-wise quality scores and over/under-segmentation error labels. We demonstrate SCORE on humerus CT scans, where it considerably improves initial predictions from TotalSegmentator, and achieves performance on par with existing refinement methods, while greatly reducing their supervision requirements and annotation time. Our code is available at: https://gitlab.inria.fr/adelangl/SCORE.

title: Period-Doubling Bifurcation in Spatiotemporally Mode-Locked Mid-Infrared Fiber Laser
authors: Hang Ren, Ying Yang, He Cao, Zhanhong Lip, Jun Guo, Jiachen Wang, Jinzhang Wang, Fanlong Dong, Shuangchen Ruan, Chunyu Guo
abstract: In this paper, to the best of our knowledge, we report the period-doubling bifurcation phenomenon observed in a spatiotemporally mode-locked mid-infrared fiber laser for the first time. The laser is established with a Er3+:ZBLAN fiber. Conventional soliton is generated using the laser under a pump power of 3.66 W, of which the center wavelength, pulse duration, and repetition frequency are 2.8 μm, 300 fs, and 45.02 MHz, respectively. The conventional soliton is switched to period-doubling bifurcation by simply increasing the pump power. We theoretically investigate the mechanism of period-doubling bifurcation. It is found that besides pump power, nonlinear phase shift is also a critical factor for the generation of period-doubling bifurcation in the spatiotemporally mode-locked mid-infrared fiber laser. The work can be informative for understanding the dynamics of spatiotemporally mode-locked fiber lasers.

title: Architecture in Practice: Scale, Latency, Decisions, and Change: A Multi-Episode SE Radio Digest
authors: Karthik Vaidhyanathan
abstract: Presents a panel discussion on the topic of Architecture in Practice: Scale, Latency, Decisions, and Change: A Multi-Episode SE Radio Digest.

title: LMPT: Conditional Point Transformer for Anatomical Landmark Detection on 3D Point Clouds
authors: Matteo Bastico, Pierre Onghena, David Ryckelynck, Beatriz Marcotegui, Santiago Velasco-Forero, Laurent Corté, Caroline Robine-Decourcelle, Etienne Decencière
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Accurate identification of anatomical landmarks is crucial for various medical applications. Traditional manual landmarking is time-consuming and prone to inter-observer variability, while rule-based methods are often tailored to specific geometries or limited sets of landmarks. In recent years, anatomical surfaces have been effectively represented as point clouds, which are lightweight structures composed of spatial coordinates. Following this strategy and to overcome the limitations of existing landmarking techniques, we propose Landmark Point Transformer (LmPT), a method for automatic anatomical landmark detection on point clouds that can leverage homologous bones from different species for translational research. The LmPT model incorporates a conditioning mechanism that enables adaptability to different input types to conduct crossspecies learning. We focus the evaluation of our approach on femoral landmarking using both human and newly annotated dog femurs, demonstrating its generalization and effectiveness across species. The code and dog femur dataset will be publicly available at: https://github.com/Pierreoo/LandmarkPointTransformer.

title: PDN Impedance-Aware Phase Sequence Optimization for Multiphase Converters
authors: Deqiu Yang, Wei Chen, Hongfeng Fan, Ning Dai, Xin Lou, Gary Yao
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: Multiphase buck converters are the industry standard for powering high-current, low-voltage processors. Conventional interleaving, in which phases are fired sequentially, is widely adopted to reduce output ripple, but its effectiveness relies on the assumption of an ideal power delivery network (PDN). This paper shows that parasitic impedance between phase outputs can impose severe current stress on the output capacitors and increase the output voltage ripple. To address this issue, two PDN-aware phase-sequencing strategies, termed "Odd–Even" and "Symmetric-Pairing," are proposed. Circuit-level simulations using a PDN model extracted from ANSYS Q3D demonstrate that the proposed sequences, especially Odd–Even, can reduce total capacitor ESR losses by up to 65% and simultaneously reduce the output voltage ripple by 23.2% compared with the conventional sequential approach. An 8-phase hardware prototype is constructed to experimentally verify the proposed concepts. These findings provide a practical co-design guideline that links converter control strategy and physical layout in high-density power solutions.

title: Joint Modeling of Spatio-Temporal and Cross-Scale Consistencies for Multi-View Depth Estimation
authors: Ying Zhu, Kehui Tan, Hong Liu, Buyun Chen
publish: 2025 Asian Conference on Artificial Intelligence Technology (ACAIT)
abstract: Multi-view depth estimation plays a critical role in 3D perception tasks, especially in collaborative robotics scenarios where multiple agents must build a better scene understanding in a collaborative manner. However, existing methods often struggle to fully exploit the complementary strengths of single-view and multi-view cues. To address this limitation, we propose BiSTCA, a novel Bi-directional Spatio-Temporal Cross-Attention architecture for multi-view depth estimation. BiSTCA explicitly models spatio-temporal dependencies across frames by facilitating bidirectional information flow between spatial and temporal dimensions, thereby facilitating more accurate and coherent depth predictions across views. To mitigate inconsistencies arising from independently generated multi-scale outputs, we further introduce a cross-scale consistency loss, which regularizes predictions across different resolutions. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our framework quantitatively and qualitatively on the KITTI Odometry dataset, and demonstrate that BiSTCA outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving a 4.65% improvement in Abs Rel and an 8.61% improvement in Sq Rel. Ablation studies validate the effectiveness of the proposed components, confirming our framework’s robustness and adaptability in complex real-world scenarios.

title: Review of Mechanical Design and Dynamic Qualification Practices for 3U CubeSats
authors: Hiba Bootia El Oumami, Othmane Noureddine, Younes Moubachir, Zouhair Guennoun, Jamila Hassar, Zakaria Moudden
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Global Aeronautical Engineering and Satellite Technology (GAST)
abstract: CubeSats have emerged as cost-effective and versatile platforms for scientific, educational, and commercial missions. Within this category, the 3U configuration offers an optimal balance between payload capacity, system complexity, and mission flexibility. This paper reviews the mechanical design and dynamic qualification of 3U CubeSats, emphasizing structural materials, manufacturing methods, and assembly strategies. Modal analysis and vibration qualification are discussed as critical processes to ensure compliance with launch vehicle requirements and long-term mission reliability. Reported modal analyses indicate that the natural frequencies are primarily governed by the stiffness-to-mass ratio and the quality of structural construction, highlighting the influence of both design and manufacturing precision on dynamic behavior. By consolidating published data and identifying structural and dynamic performance trends, this work provides guidance for advancing CubeSat design practices and supporting future mission success.

title: A Photovoltaic and Battery-Driven Full-range Buck and Boost DC-DC Converters for the Drone Wireless Power Transmission System
authors: Mahmoud F. Elmorshedy, Seshagiri Rao Vemparala, M. S. Bhaskar, Dhafer Almakhles, Md Rabiul Islam, Kashem M Muttaqi
abstract: Drones are increasingly employed for a wide range of applications, including military surveillance along national borders and the maintenance of telecommunications infrastructure in remote locations and inaccessible regions, where ensuring uninterrupted operation remains a major challenge due to the lack of reliable charging infrastructure. To address this limitation, this article proposes and experimentally validates an integrated three-port photovoltaic (PV)-battery-wireless power transfer (WPT) charging station for autonomous drone recharging in off-grid environments. PV panels are cost effective and sustainable power source for remote regions with WPT technology. The architecture combines solar PV generation with battery energy storage through coordinated DC-DC power conversion to provide a regulated DC-link for a high-frequency WPT interface, enabling contactless and maintenance-free drone charging. To maximize system efficiency and ensure optimal PV energy harvesting under variable irradiance conditions, the maximum power point tracking (MPPT) technique is adopted for the PV panels, while a closed-loop proportional-integral controller ensures the DC voltage remains stable at the desired level under dynamic load and source variations. The battery energy management system enables bidirectional power flow by managing charging and discharging cycles, to buffer solar intermittency and ensure uninterrupted power delivery to the WPT subsystem. The performance of the complete system, including system design, control architecture, and power management strategy, is validated through detailed simulations and experimental results obtained from a laboratory-scale prototype. The results demonstrate robust dynamic performance, fast MPPT convergence, stable DC-link regulation, and reliable wireless power delivery, thereby confirming the feasibility and effectiveness of the hybrid PV-battery-powered WPT charging station for sustained drone operation in remote and challenging environments.

title: Eosinophil Counting with Green Learning
authors: Qixin Hu, Yixing Wu, Mate Levente Nagy, Hwan Dong, C.-C. Jay Kuo
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Quantifying eosinophils in gastrointestinal (GI) biopsies is critical for diagnosing eosinophilic esophagitis. The gold standard, manual counting by pathologists, is laborious, timeconsuming, and suffers from significant variability between physicians. Although deep learning (DL) models have automated this process with high accuracy, they often operate as black boxes, lacking transparency and interpretability. Furthermore, these neural networks require substantial computational resources for training and inference. This paper introduces a novel Green Learning (GL) framework for eosinophil counting, prioritizing transparency and computational efficiency. Our pipeline diverges from backpropagation-based DL. It uses unsupervised sparse-coding-based feature extractors. A dictionary selection module then identifies the most representative dictionaries, which are sent to the decisionlearning model for final classification and counting. We prove that this GL framework can achieve an accuracy competitive with the state-of-the-art DL models while drastically reducing the model size and complexity. This work represents a step towards green transparent AI in digital pathology, offering a computationally frugal and interpretable alternative for efficient clinical deployment.

title: Comparison of DC biased Inductances with Small- and Large-Signal AC-Excitation
authors: Pierre Küster, Lennart Hoffmann, Jens Friebe
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: Numerous permeabilities are defined in literature under various DC- and AC-excitation levels. In most cases, these are linearized values, which leaves the question which effective inductance is actually present during operation. The paper shows hysteresis measurements of DC biased inductances with different alternating field excitations (large-signal measurements) and the differential as well as the effective inductance derived from this. The results are compared with impedance measurements (small-signal measurements) and discussed. The measurements are performed on an MnZn toroidal core, an MnZn PM-core with air gap, and also on a powder core. In addition to investigating inductance as a function of the excitation, flux density as a function of DC current is also discussed. The article concludes with a comparison of the inductances investigated in a buck converter.

title: EMBC Special Issue: Advancing Electrolaryngeal Speech Enhancement Through Speech–Text Representation Learning
authors: Ding Ma, Jinyi Mi, Fengji Li, Lester Phillip Violeta, Jiajun He, Wenchin Huang, Kazuhiro Kobayashi, Tomoki Toda
abstract: Objective: laryngectomees depend on an electromechanical device to generate electrolaryngeal (EL) speech for verbal communication. Compared with normal speech, EL speech suffers from severe distortion, limited phonetic variation, unnatural prosody, and temporal shifts, degrading naturalness and intelligibility. Although sequence-to-sequence (seq2seq) voice conversion (VC) based EL-speech-to-normal-speech conversion (EL2SP) is promising, substantial mismatches between EL and normal speech inevitably cause cumulative mapping errors that limit performance. To address this, we describe a novel representation learning framework integrating speech and text representations to improve mapping and reconstruction quality within a seq2seq VC model. Methods: our methodology comprises two main stages: 1) representation integration and learning, and 2) reconstruction training. A network capable of incorporating auxiliary text information is first constructed with pretrained modules to learn speech–text-based integrated representations. Then, an autoencoder-style reconstruction strategy finalizes EL2SP model to inherit these representations without increasing model complexity. Additional optimization designs are performed across these stages. We introduce three fusion strategies including middle-, input-, and hybrid-level fusion strategies that progressively enhance learning. Moreover, besides standard seq2seq VC objectives, an additional reconstruction loss on the integrated representation is introduced to refine representation transfer. Results: experiments under different EL2SP datasets consistently demonstrate that our methods, combined with data augmentations, outperform baselines relying solely on speech representations regarding both conversion quality and intelligibility. Furthermore, progressive improvements with system design depth validate the effectiveness of our methods. Significance: the proposed methods provide an extensible and practical methodology for EL speech enhancement and assistive communication technologies.

title: OpenEEG: Uncertainty-Aware Open Set Learning for EEG-Based Brain Disorders Diagnosis
authors: Li Xue, Weikang Gu, Jiaming Yu, Xuemei Qiu, Tao Wu, Lifang Wei
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Deep learning models for EEG-based brain disorder diagnosis have shown promise, but their clinical utility is hampered by the restrictive closed-set assumption and an inability to quantify predictive uncertainty. This limitation leads to overconfident misclassifications of both unseen disorders and challenging known cases, posing significant safety risks. To address these issues, we propose OpenEEG, an uncertainty-aware open-set learning framework designed to simultaneously reject unknown disease categories and quantify the epistemic uncertainty of its predictions. OpenEEG integrates a Transformer backbone with a UA-Classifier based on evidential deep learning, which learns a Dirichlet distribution over class predictions to derive uncertainty scores. We validate our framework on multiple public EEG datasets under subjectindependent splits. The results demonstrate that OpenEEG substantially outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both closed-set classification and open-set detection. By enabling principled rejection of unknown and uncertain diagnoses, OpenEEG represents a critical step towards trustworthy and clinically deployable EEG-based diagnostic systems.

title: Transformer-Based Foundation Model for Universal Ultrasound Image Analysis
authors: Zhikai Yang, Junjie Hu, Félix Nieto-del-Amor, Guangyuan Li, Rodrigo Moreno
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: We propose a transformer-based foundation model for unified multi-task, multi-organ, multi-disease ultrasound image analysis, targeting 27 subtasks across segmentation, classification, detection, and landmark regression. The model follows hard parameter sharing: the encoder of a Swin Transformer pretrained on ImageNet is shared by all tasks, while each task uses its own prediction head. For segmentation and detection tasks, we use feature pyramid network decoders with separate segmentation and detection branches; for classification and regression tasks, we use encoder-level features with specific heads. Compared to other existing methods in offline experiments, the proposed method has the best performance. At the Foundation Model Challenge for Ultrasound Image Analysis (FMC UIA) challenge, our method achieved 8th place among 24th participating teams. The code is public at https://github.com/HJJ-D/Foundation-Model-Challenge-for-Ultrasound-Image-Analysis.

title: Edge-Based LSTM for Hyper-Local Precision Weather Forecasting
authors: Simhadri Vadrevu, Deepak Kumar, K. Harika Sri Lakshmi, P. Prasad
publish: 2026 International Conference on Multidisciplinary Innovations For Smart &amp; Sustainable Future (MISSF)
abstract: This paper presents an edge-based hyper-local weather forecasting system that integrates multi-sensor Internet of Things (IoT) data with recurrent deep learning models to achieve higher prediction accuracy than regional weather services. The proposed system employs temperature, humidity, atmospheric pressure, air quality, ultraviolet (UV), wind speed, and precipitation sensors interfaced with an Arduino Due and Rasp- berry Pi edge platform to collect real-time meteorological data at a fixed geographic location (Latitude: <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$17.016300^{\circ} \mathrm{N}$</tex>, Longitude: <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$81.773435^{\circ}$</tex> E). Historical and live sensor data are preprocessed using standard scaling and label encoding and modeled using RNN-based LSTM architectures to forecast weather parameters over a 10-day horizon with low latency edge execution. The Edge- LSTM model has achieved prediction accuracy of 97.53% for temperature, 93.07% for humidity, 98.92% for pressure, and 99.43% for precipitation according to experimental evaluation. In comparing with Google Weather, there is a significant decrease in errors, with the temperature MAE decreasing from 10.88% to 2.47% (77.29% reduction) and the precipitation MAE decreasing from 17.51% to 0.57% (96.74% reduction). Further next, the proposed system achieves an average 10-day forecast coverage of 89%, compared to Google weather is 33%, demonstrating superior reliability in capturing micro-climatic variations. These results are validate that localized sensing combined with edge-based LSTM modeling provides substantial precision advantages over regional.

title: Prompt-Free Lightweight Sam Adaptation for Histopathology Nuclei Segmentation with Strong Cross-Dataset Generalization
authors: Muhammad Hassan Maqsood, Yanming Zhu, Alfred Lam, Getamesay Dagnaw, Xuefei Yin, Alan Liew
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Histopathology nuclei segmentation is crucial for quantitative tissue analysis and cancer diagnosis. Although existing segmentation methods have achieved strong performance, they are often computationally heavy and show limited generalization across datasets, which constrains their practical deployment. Recent SAM-based approaches have shown great potential in general and medical imaging, but typically rely on prompt guidance or complex decoders, making them less suitable for histopathology images with dense nuclei and heterogeneous appearances. We propose a prompt-free and lightweight SAM adaptation that leverages multi-level encoder features and residual decoding for accurate and efficient nuclei segmentation. The framework fine-tunes only LoRA modules within the frozen SAM encoder, requiring just 4.1M trainable parameters. Experiments on three benchmark datasets TNBC, MoNuSeg, and PanNuke demonstrate state-of-the-art performance and strong cross-dataset generalization, highlighting the effectiveness and practicality of the proposed framework for histopathology applications.

title: Title Page
authors: N/A
publish: 2025 Asian Conference on Artificial Intelligence Technology (ACAIT)

title: Towards a Superconducting Magnetic Thermal Modulator for Bolometric Detectors
authors: Enrico Petriella, Matías Hampel, Juan J. Bonaparte, Jesús Bonilla Neira, Gabriel Bosch, Juan M. Geria, Nahuel Müller, Manuel García Redondo, Lucía Sucunza, Luciano Ferreyro, Alan Fuster, Alejandro Almela, Sebastian Kempf, Manuel Platino
abstract: We present the preliminary development and characterization of a superconducting thermal switch designed to modulate the thermal response of cryogenic bolometers. The device uses an aluminum thermal link whose conductance is modulated by a niobium control coil. Initial measurements confirm the feasibility of the switching principle and demonstrate dynamic operation at least up to 50  kHz. These early results indicate that this approach can shift the signal spectrum away from 1/f noise, with ongoing efforts focused on integration with bolometric sensors and optimizing device geometry.

title: From Large Language Models to Reasoning and Agents
authors: Miroslaw Staron, Silvia Abrahão
abstract: Software engineering is shifting from standalone large language models to reasoning-driven and agent-based systems. Drawing on ASE 2025 research, this column highlights how AI agents now test, debug, adapt, and explain software, making a transition toward more autonomous and observable AI-assisted development.

title: Ultra-Wideband Graphene THz Antenna : Substrate Thickness Optimization
authors: Rida Biqaa, Asma Khabba, Ibtissam Bahlaouane, Asma Oirgari, Saida Ibnyaich, Hassan Belahrach, Abdelouhab Zeroual
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Global Aeronautical Engineering and Satellite Technology (GAST)
abstract: THz waves provide a secure, non-ionising option to conventional imaging for identifying unique molecular fingerprints and high water content in cancerous tissues. Graphene-based antennas utilize Surface Plasmon Polaritons (SPP) as well as dynamic tunability to make possible extreme miniaturization and high-sensitivity real-time diagnostics. A proposed study presents a rectangular microstrip patch antenna tailored for THz band operation with the goal of enhancing the performance of the THz antenna. The proposed paper presents an ultra-wideband graphene antenna based on a polyamide substrate with a relative permittivity of 3.5 and a loss tangent of 0.0027. It is used with a configuration of 100 × 100 × 10 μm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup>, and a graphene patch with a thickness of 1 μm and dimensions of 64 μm in length and 50 μm in width. Additionally, a full rectangular copper ground plane with a thickness of 1 μm and the same length and width as the substrate was utilized. The proposed antenna exhibits an ultra-wideband behavior of 1680 GHz (from 1.5 THz to 3.18 THz) and operates at 1.83 THz. This study’s main goal is to evaluate how different substrate thicknesses with modification of the h parameter can give us different results and modify the performance of the suggested antenna. Additionally, it examines the thickness that can yield positive results, such as efficiency, bandwidth, gain, and reflection coefficient.

title: A Current Source Gate Driver with Dual-Edge Adaptive Slew Rate for GaN Devices
authors: Giao Huu Thuc, Meng-Jen Tsai, Ching-Jan Chen, Jyan-Kai Fu, Wen-Xin Wu, Wei-Ru Lin
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: This paper introduces a current source gate driver with an adaptive slew rate capability to enhance the switching performance of discrete or co-packing GaN devices on a wide range of switching frequencies, f<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">sw</inf>. The proposed driver IC implements sophisticated principles of frequency to current conversion, current amplification, and current-based reverse recovery slope to control the gate driving strength and slew rate fully on-chip without external gate resistors and programmable slew rate resistors. The switching loss, ringing, and overshoot issues on both edges of the GaN power switches are effectively improved. The IC prototype is designed by the TSMC 0.18 μm HVG2 process supporting high-voltage Mosfets. According to the post-simulation at high f<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">sw</inf> of 2 MHz, dV<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">ds</inf>/dt reach 47.8 V/ns and 48.5 V/ns on falling and rising edges of a discrete GaN switch, respectively, without ringing problems. Compared with a separated path voltage source gate driver, the switching loss can be reduced by 22 %.

title: A Maximum Power Point Tracking Algorithm with the Adaptive Moment Estimation for a PV System
authors: Jianwu Zeng, Zichun Lin, Taesic Kim
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: The Perturb and Observe (P&O) algorithm is one of the most widely adopted Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT) methods in photovoltaic (PV) systems due to its simplicity and low computational requirements. However, the P&O method, which heavily relies on step-size selection, suffers from power oscillations around the MPP under steady-state conditions and may incorrectly reflect power variations under rapidly changing conditions. This paper presents an adaptive moment estimation (Adam) MPPT that estimates the first and second moments of the gradients. The insensitivity to the initial learning rate eliminates the influence of the step size selection. The simulation and experimental results show that it has a faster transient response and smaller steady-state power oscillations than the P&O method, regardless of the step size.

title: Dynamic On-Resistance and Dynamic Threshold Voltage of GaN-on-Si HEMTs With Varying Substrate Connections in Steady-State Hard Switching
authors: Hongchang Cui, Xin Yang, Zineng Yang, Hengbo Zhang, Hehe Gong, Yuhao Zhang
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: The dynamic stability of GaN-on-Si HEMTs with different substrate connections is a critical for understanding the device reliability and designing the substrate bias management circuits for monolithic bidirectional switches. Prior studies have mainly focused on dynamic on-resistance (R<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">ON</inf>) characterization using pulse I–V or single-pulse double-pulse tests, which deviate from practical operating conditions. In addition, dynamic threshold voltage (V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">TH</inf>)—essential to understanding R<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">ON</inf> shifts-has rarely been evaluated under different substrate conditions. This work presents a circuit test platform enabling in-situ characterization of dynamic R<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">ON</inf>, dynamic V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">TH</inf>, and substrate bias under steady-state hard-switching. Using this setup, GaN-on-Si HEMTs are tested under source-connected, drain-connected, and floating substrate configurations. Under the floating-substrate condition, we find the substrate bias exhibits oscillatory behaviors between positive and negative values after reaching the steady-state hard switching. A physics-based model is further developed to capture the substrate bias dynamics. The combined measurements and modeling clarify the mechanisms behind dynamic R<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">ON</inf> shifts under different substrate configurations and can guide the future development of active substrate bias management for GaN-on-Si HEMTs.

title: Bytecode-Level Ensemble Framework for V8 Vulnerability Detection in Regression Tests
authors: Deepa Thilak K, Muhammed Sahal M N, Abdur Rahman
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Research Methodologies in Knowledge Management, Artificial Intelligence and Telecommunication Engineering (RMKMATE)
abstract: JavaScript engine vulnerabilities pose critical security risks to billions of web users, yet automated detection in regression test suites remains challenging due to high false-positive rates and limited semantic understanding of execution behavior. This paper presents a bytecode-level machine learning framework for vulnerability detection in V8 engine regression tests, extracting 41 interpretable features from Ignition bytecode including instruction diversity metrics, constant pool characteristics, and proximity-based semantic patterns. A soft-voting ensemble combining Random Forest and Gradient Boosting achieves 78.8% accuracy in balanced mode and–critically–94.1% precision at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1. 4 3 \%}$</tex> FPR in low-noise triage mode, the operating point most relevant for CI deployment. The ensemble outperforms LSTM-based deep learning by 10.2 percentage points, while SHAP analysis confirms instruction diversity as the primary discriminative feature. The framework supports threshold-based deployment enabling automated prioritization of regression tests requiring manual security review.

title: A Pipeline for DTI Indices and Connectivity Analysis Applied to a Longitudinal Multiple Sclerosis Cohort
authors: Liwei Zhou, Zhangxing Bian, Jinwei Zhang, Blake E. Dewey, Shiv Saidha, Peter A. Calabresi, Aaron Carass, Jerry L. Prince
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Multiple sclerosis (MS) causes progressive white matter damage, which can be sensitively assessed using diffusion MRI. However, existing analyses often rely on manual delineation, limiting scalability. We present an automated diffusion-MRI pipeline that computes global lesion-averaged DTI indices, tract-specific measures within tract-lesion intersections, and whole-brain structural connectivity metrics without manual intervention. The framework integrates lesion segmentation and filling, DWI preprocessing and DTI index computation, tractography reconstruction, bundle extraction, brain network analysis, and statistical analysis. Applied to a longitudinal MS cohort, the pipeline produced diffusion and connectivity patterns consistent with previously reported trends, supporting its validity. These results demonstrate the feasibility and practical utility of the proposed framework for scalable, longitudinal MS analysis.

title: DMC-SM: A Fast System Matrix Calibration Method for High-Quality Magnetic Particle Imaging Reconstruction
authors: Jian'an Ye, Ziwei Chen, Gen Shi, Zhongwei Bian, Yu An, Jie Tian
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Magnetic Particle Imaging (MPI) is a novel imaging technique that detects the magnetic response of superparamagnetic iron oxide nanoparticles, offering high sensitivity and temporal resolution. However, the system matrix (SM) calibration, which is crucial for quantitative analysis in MPI, remains complex, time-consuming, and prone to artifacts. To address these challenges, we propose a Distance Map Consistent System Matrix (DMC-SM) generation method. By utilizing a one-dimensional distance-domain function, DMC-SM effectively calibrates the system matrix, eliminating distortions and interpolation discontinuities. Specifically, experiments conducted with actual measurement data in closedbore MPI demonstrate that the proposed method enables a larger Field of View (FOV). Additionally, it reduces computational complexity, significantly accelerates calibration speed, and enhances image reconstruction quality. For a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$64 \times 64$</tex> grid, the system matrix construction time was reduced from 112.64s to just 2.18s, indicating substantial improvements in both speed and efficiency.

title: Unleashing Tool Engineering and Intelligence for Agentic AI in Next-Generation Communication Networks
authors: Yinqiu Liu, Ruichen Zhang, Dusit Niyato, Abbas Jamalipour, Trung Q. Duong, Dong In Kim
abstract: Nowadays, agentic AI is emerging as a transformative paradigm for next-generation communication networks, promising to evolve large language models (LLMs) from passive chatbots into autonomous operators. However, unleashing this potential requires bridging the critical gap between abstract reasoning and physical actuation, a capability we term tool intelligence. In this article, we explore the landscape of tool engineering to empower agentic AI in communications. We first analyze the functionalities of tool intelligence and its effects on communications. We then propose a systematic review for tool engineering, covering the entire lifecycle from tool creation and discovery to selection, learning, and benchmarking. Furthermore, we present a case study on tool-assisted uncrewed aerial vehicles (UAV) trajectory planning to demonstrate the realization of tool intelligence in communications. By introducing a teacher- guided reinforcement learning approach with a feasibility shield, we enable agents to intelligently operate tools. They utilize external tools to eliminate navigational uncertainty while mastering cost-aware scheduling under strict energy constraints. This article aims to provide a roadmap for building the tool-augmented intelligent agents of the 6G era.

title: PESGRE 2025 Copyright
authors: N/A
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Power Electronics, Smart Grid, and Renewable Energy (PESGRE)

title: A Reconfigurable Series / Parallel IGBT-Based DC Solid-State Circuit Breaker
authors: Prasanth Pithanisetty, Daniel Dsa, Moein Ghadrdan, Satish Naik Banavath, Edivan Laercio Carvalho, Giovanni De Carne
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Power Electronics, Smart Grid, and Renewable Energy (PESGRE)
abstract: Modern Direct-Current (DC) power systems operate with increased load demand as well as high system voltage. Ultrafast and reliable fault isolation is a necessity in DC systems due to the absence of natural current zero-crossing points and the rapid rise of fault current. This paper presents a modular reconfigurable DC Solid-State Circuit Breaker (SSCB) using two IGBT switches that can operate either in series, offering high voltage blocking capability, or in parallel for high current carrying capability. The key challenges in such a reconfigurable SSCB, such as unequal static and dynamic voltage balancing in series connection of switches and current sharing imbalance in parallel operation, are discussed. A hardware prototype of the reconfigurable SSCB rated at 400 V, 25 A is experimentally validated against overcurrent events, considering the series as well as parallel connection of IGBTs.

title: Integrated Magnetics Design of a Two-winding HF Transformer Achieving Load Current-dependent Variable Leakage Inductance Characteristics
authors: Annoy Kumar Das, Baylon G. Fernandes
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: Leakage inductance adjustment is pivotal for optimizing the performance of magnetically integrated soft-switched dc-dc converters. This work aims to achieve current-dependent variable leakage inductance profile in a high-frequency transformer using integrated magnetics design, which is simple, scalable, avoids any airgap in the main magnetic path, offers partly decoupled control of the magnetizing and leakage inductances, and eliminates bias/control windings and moving/changeover mechanisms, thereby ameliorating the limitations of existing methods reported in literature. First, the proposed methodology using high permeability ribbon/tape wound core is discussed; next, a magnetic equivalent circuit incorporating the load current-dependent variable leakage inductance is derived. Finally, the operating principle is verified using 2D FEM simulation based on a 7.2 kW, 360/360 V, 500 kHz transformer modeled in FEMM with ferrite as the main core and amorphous as the leakage layer. In comparison to conventional design, for the same interwinding isolation thickness, using the proposed scheme the integrated leakage inductance increases from 9.85 µH to 13.7 µH, corresponding to a 37.7% change, while the load current decreases from 100%–10% of its rated value. The proposed technique is also demonstrated experimentally using laboratory-scale prototypes built using amorphous- and nanocrystalline-based ribbon/tape-wound leakage layers. It shows that amorphous-and nanocrystalline-based designs can offer 15.5 and 30.1 times higher leakage inductance, respectively, compared to the conventional design; in addition, these exhibit respectively 7.67 and 13.2 times increment in its leakage inductance, corresponding to a decrement of 87.8% in the applied volt-second, thus exhibiting variable leakage inductance characteristics over a wide range.

title: A Multi-Stage Feature Selection Framework for Low-Cost CKD Screening
authors: Shaimaa I. Mostafa, Mahmoud M. Abdelhamied, A. M. M. Madbouly
publish: 2026 1st International Conference on Emerging Technologies and Engineering Systems (ICETES)
abstract: Chronic Kidney Disease is identified as a serious public health issue, especially because most cases are diagnosed at a later stage. Early diagnosis and monitoring of chronic kidney disease are critical as it helps defer the progression and minimize serious complications. However, in most resource-poor settings, it is not always possible to have early diagnoses as most comprehensive lab workups are not readily available. In this respect, there is an opportunity with the use of machine learning, which helps derive relevant information from available healthcare data. We present a multistage machine learning framework designed to balance diagnostic accuracy with cost and availability of clinical information. The proposed approach involves the sequential incorporation of patient data, beginning with a basic clinical history, followed by reasonable urinalysis results, and lastly comprehensive laboratory measurements when available. The framework was evaluated using the UCI Chronic Kidney Disease dataset, which contains medical records from 400 patients. Three analytic phases were built: the first phase used only clinical historical features, the second combined history with urinalysis, and the third integrated the full laboratory report. At each step, a random forest classifier was developed, and its performance was evaluated using standard metrics including accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score. Results show that reliable chronic kidney disease estimate can be realized even with limited information. The model based solely on clinical history achieved an accuracy of 89.7%, which improved to 97.5% after incorporating urine test features. When complete laboratory data were included, the model achieved accurate classification.

title: Multimodal Post-Disaster Building Damage Assessment with Weakly Supervised Learning
authors: P Vivekanandhan, Mithuna R, Tharun Kumar V, Swetha D, Denesh Kumar S
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Research Methodologies in Knowledge Management, Artificial Intelligence and Telecommunication Engineering (RMKMATE)
abstract: Rapid post-disaster building damage assessment is critical for effective humanitarian response [1]. We present an edge-optimized framework that fuses optical and Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) satellite imagery with crowd-sourced point annotations to produce real-time damage maps. An adaptive fusion module dynamically adjusts optical-to-SAR weighting based on estimated cloud cover, preserving all-weather capability [11]. A tiered Independent Bayesian Classifier Combination (IBCC) strategy scales computational load to available hardware [7], while progressive neural architectures span devices from smartphones <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\mathbf{4 7 ~ M B})$</tex> to workstations <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\mathbf{8 9 0 ~ M B})$</tex> [14]. Experiments on the xBD and BRIGHT datasets show 78% accuracy retention under total cloud occlusion, 64% annotation throughput improvement over expert-only labeling, and robust cross-disaster generalization-establishing practical viability for time-critical disaster scenarios.

title: Agentic AI–Driven Autonomous Traffic Orchestration System for Sustainable Urban Mobility
authors: Delsi Robinsha S, Vanusha D, Vinoth Nas, Anbarasi A
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Research Methodologies in Knowledge Management, Artificial Intelligence and Telecommunication Engineering (RMKMATE)
abstract: City traffic jam is not a new issue within the urban environment that is associated with the consumption of high amounts of fuel, release of carbon dioxide, and even low productivity of the commuters. The old system of traffic lights that is fixed time does not adapt effectively to the dynamic nature of the distribution of traffic; hence, it does very poorly at the intersection. In this paper we introduce an AI based orchestration model of the traffic whereby the orchestration is an agentic environment which is able to automatically adjust the timing of the signals, depending on the present traffic volume. The system incorporates a very simple adaptive control algorithm, which can be used to compute the optimal periods of the green-times, without the necessity of using complex machine learning algorithms or pre-training. The offered Agentic AI controller is contrasted to a conventional fixed-time controller with the Metro Interstate Traffic Volume data comprising more than 48,000 timestamped observations of traffic on the entire time scale of the data. The methodology estimates the real time flow of traffic and applies the two control strategies to each time period and a performance measurement is done by a delay estimation model which measures the quantity of traffic that is not served at each time period. Experimentally, it has been established that the Agentic AI controller is superior to fixed-time control in most traffic situations. Performance is steadily improved on a monthly, weekly and peak hour basis and the Agentic AI can minimize traffic delay by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$27-42$</tex> percent, depending on the congestion. It is important to observe that the system is highly sensitive to abrupt demand fluctuations hence it can ensure low amounts of delay despite the need to compete in high volume operation in the event that the fixed time control is an issue. The discussed results bear the implication of the potential potential of lightweight Agentic AI systems to bring the efficiency of intersection to a higher level, provide a method of sustainable urban mobility, and serve as a means of scaling up the traditional traffic signal kind of functions. The paper forms a sound grounds of introducing autonomous traffic controllers in smart transports.

title: Split-PCB Planar Inductor Design for High-density High-efficiency Power Converters
authors: Ashwini Kumar Dubey, Sayan Paul, Yanghe Liu, Shailesh Joshi, Dragan Maksimović
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: Magnetic components often contribute to a significant portion of both the volume and power loss in power converters. This paper proposes a split-printed-circuit-board (split-PCB) design approach for planar inductors, which achieves a significant reduction in winding loss compared to a single-PCB design by mitigating fringing flux and proximity losses. The resulting decrease in total inductor loss enables smaller thermal management and higher power density. Both single-PCB and split-PCB planar inductor designs are modeled in Ansys Maxwell, and their performance is compared. Experimental validation is performed using a 40kW boost converter at input voltages of 100V, 120V, and 150V, with ac ripple currents of 40A, 50A, and 60A, respectively, operating at a light load with an average input current of 9A and a switching frequency of 200kHz. The experimental results confirm that the proposed split-PCB planar inductor achieves a 35% reduction in total converter loss compared to a conventional single-PCB planar inductor when the boost converter is operating at the input voltage of 150V, the output voltage of 288V, and the ripple current of 60A.

title: Prediction of Extrusion Forming Performance and Finite Element Simulation and Analysis of ZK60 Magnesium Alloy Shells
authors: Shuai Zhang
publish: 2026 International Conference on Vision, Advanced Imaging and Computer Technology (VAICT)
abstract: Given the significant impact of mold temperature on the extrusion forming of magnesium alloy shells, this paper conducts a finite element simulation analysis of the extrusion forming process of magnesium alloy shells, studies the effect of mold temperature on the extrusion forming of magnesium alloy shells, and carries out experiments. The research results indicate that when the mold temperature ranges from 260 to $350^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the crack defects on the inner wall of the shell decrease with increasing temperature; when the mold temperature is $320^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the mold surface is relatively smooth and intact, and the overall stress value of the mold is small, around $\mathbf{8 3. 4 M P a}$; the mold temperature has a minor effect on the equivalent strain of the test piece. Both the experimental results and simulation analysis of the mold temperature’s impact on the surface morphology of the test piece suggest that a suitable mold preheating temperature for extrusion forming of magnesium alloy shells is $320^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.This paper further integrates material forming mechanisms with numerical simulation methods to model and analyze the temperature field and deformation behavior of ZK60 magnesium alloy. It provides data support and theoretical basis for optimizing intelligent forming process parameters of magnesium alloys, holding significant practical implications for advancing the application of lightweight components in intelligent manufacturing.

title: DiffusionQC: Artifact Detection and Quality Control in Histopathology Images via Diffusion Model
authors: Zhenzhen Wang, Zhongliang Zhou, Zhuoyu Wen, Jeong Hwan Kook, John B. Wojcik, John Kang
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Digital pathology plays a vital role across modern medicine, offering critical insights for disease diagnosis, prognosis, and treatment. However, histopathology images often contain artifacts introduced during slide preparation and digitization. Detecting and excluding them is essential to ensure reliable downstream analysis. Traditional supervised models typically require large annotated datasets, which is resourceintensive and not generalizable to novel artifact types. To address this, we propose DiffusionQC, which detects artifacts as outliers among clean images using a diffusion model. It requires only a set of clean images for training rather than pixel-level artifact annotations and predefined artifact types. Furthermore, we introduce a contrastive learning module to explicitly enlarge the distribution separation between artifact and clean images, yielding an enhanced version of our method. Empirical results demonstrate superior performance to state-of-the-art and offer cross-stain generalization capacity, with significantly less data and annotations.

title: Grid Voltage Sensorless Predictive Power Control of an Off-Board Battery Charger With Adaptive Inductance Estimation
authors: Durga Prasad Pilli, Deepak Ronanki, Apparao Dekka
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: Due to the fast expansion of electric vehicles (EVs), the development and implementation of reliable and cost-efficient charging systems has become increasingly important. The typical two-stage off-board EV charger employs four voltage sensors and three current sensors for the AC–DC conversion stage. This considerably increases the total cost of the charger hardware. Additionally, performance loss and reliability issues may occur if any sensing device fails. To overcome these issues, this work introduces a grid-voltage sensorless predictive power control (SPPC) scheme for an off-board charger with adaptive grid-inductance estimation. In this scheme, the concept of instantaneous power theory, together with a predictive control framework, is utilized to estimate the grid voltages. Furthermore, adaptive grid-inductance estimation is incorporated to strengthen the robustness of the proposed SPPC strategy. The simulation analysis is performed to validate the feasibility of the proposed SPPC scheme for an off-board EV charger.

title: Source Code Vulnerability Detection and Personalized Password Recovery Tool
authors: T Shyam Ganesh, T Vignesh, R Vasanthi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Multidomain Integrated Learning Environments (ICSMILE)
abstract: The paper presents an intelligent machine learning-based security tool, which is used to process uploaded source code and detect possible vulnerabilities with the help of a feedforward neural network model. The system uses the method of token extraction, feature selection, model training and classification as the techniques of the system that are used to efficiently identify risky blocks of code. The application is trained on labelled datasets using a simple user interface and it helps developers and organisations to improve the security of their software by detecting flaws in real-time. Also, a password-recovery module is incorporated that provides possible passwords according to the keywords that were used in the past and typed in by the user. This project enhances cybersecurity, minimizes manual auditing, and allows identifying and addressing security vulnerabilities in time due to automatic password suggestions and vulnerability detection.

title: Adaptive AI Security for Healthcare IoT with Deceptive Defence & Dynamic Firewall Optimization
authors: Metilda Florence S, Austin Powers Newton, Yogesh K R
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Research Methodologies in Knowledge Management, Artificial Intelligence and Telecommunication Engineering (RMKMATE)
abstract: The presence of IoT technology in the medical field has increased tremendously reformed the way data is being collected and analyzed. Monitoring of the vital signs is possible in a hospital premises, which can now be done using small, interconnected devices that can be used throughout different locations.
