By mapping normalized three-phase current waveforms into two-dimensional (2D) feature-space “images” the proposed DIViT framework captures global dependencies and subtle harmonic distortions that traditional Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) overlook. Simulation demonstrates that the model achieves a classification accuracy for exact fault localization. Most notably, the system achieves a total diagnostic latency of less than 10 milliseconds seconds enabling fault localization within one-fourth of a fundamental power cycle T/4. This approach effectively bridges the gap between deep learning complexity and real-time industrial requirements, providing a scalable solution for self-healing intelligent motor drives.

title: SmartSort Bin: AI-Based Solar-Powered System for Automated Waste Segregation Using IoT
authors: Md Yasin Zihad, Khawja Imran Masud, Arobindo Paul, Md Sajedul Islam, Md Obaidur Rahman, Sahara Ali
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Multidomain Integrated Learning Environments (ICSMILE)
abstract: A solar-powered, AI-driven smart waste management system has been proposed and developed in this research to resolve the inefficient waste management in rapidly urbanizing areas of developing countries. The system is a touchless, automated waste-segregation device that incorporates the MobileNetV2 model. The use of solar energy, along with a battery backup, enables continuous grid-independent operation. In addition, real-time email alerts help operators schedule collections efficiently by notifying them when bins are full. The tests conducted show that the system can classify waste with an accuracy of 99.28%, and that the processing is fast. Empirical testing on the Raspberry Pi 4 demonstrated susceptibility to variations in lighting and diminished classification accuracy for remote objects attributable to inferior image quality. The incorporation of a lightweight deep learning model with renewable energy makes the SmartSort Bin not only a device that can clean up urban areas but also a tool that can raise recycling rates and lower the operational costs of cities, thus providing a viable and scalable way to achieve cleaner and more sustainable urban areas in the future.

title: BASSL-MI: Batch-Agnostic Self-Supervised Learning Uncovers Clinically Relevant Tumor Niches in Multiplexed Imaging
authors: Alexander Lin, Shunxing Bao, Ken Lau, Simon Vandekar, Daniel Moyer, Qi Liu, Siyuan Ma
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Multiplexed imaging enables rich, high-resolution characterization of the tumor microenvironment but relies on labor-intensive and error-prone cell segmentation and phenotyping pipelines. We present BASSL-MI, a batch-agnostic, self-supervised framework for discovering tissue niches directly from multiplexed imaging data. BASSL-MI operates directly on image patches, eliminating the need for explicit cell segmentation while mitigating image-, sample-, or batch-specific artifacts. Built on a modified contrastive block disentanglement architecture, BASSL-MI learns dual latent representations that separate biologically informative features from batch-dependent factors through spatially guided augmentations and batch-invariance objectives. Applied to a 56 -marker colorectal cancer CODEX dataset, BASSL-MI-trained embeddings markedly reduce image-specific variability and recover biologically interpretable spatial niches. Notably, it uncovers CD20-rich follicular regions associated with improved survival, outperforming published findings from cell segmentation-driven clustering. This work demonstrates that self-supervised, patch-based learning can capture clinically relevant spatial organization within tumor microenvironments, advancing toward automated, non-cell-based analysis of multiplexed imaging data.

title: Carrier Sense Multiple Access Protocols in Smart Multi-domain Integrated Learning Environments: A Comprehensive Analysis
authors: Salsabeel Suhail Theeb, Roaa Mohammad Alabdelal, Batool Mohammad Alrashdan, Obaida M. Al-Hazaimeh, Ashraf A. Abu-Ein
publish: 2026 International Conference on Smart Multidomain Integrated Learning Environments (ICSMILE)
abstract: The protocols of Carrier Sense Multiple Access (CSMA) underpin contemporary network communication. This study will examine CSMA/CD (Collision Detection) and CSMA/CA (Collision Avoidance) regarding their operational mechanisms, performance characteristics, and application domains. Alongside protocol mechanics, we present a comprehensive study of cybersecurity vulnerabilities inside both protocols and propose innovative defense solutions, including adaptive backoff checking, channel access fairness verification, and multi-layered security systems. Our efforts achieve a balance between enhancing security and optimizing efficiency, providing practical solutions for safeguarding CSMA-based networks against contemporary threats.

title: A Multimodal ResNet-BERT Fusion Model for Accurate Pet Dermatosis Diagnosis from Images and Natural Language Symptoms
authors: Pak-Hei Lam, MingHui Fan
publish: 2026 22nd IEEE International Colloquium on Signal Processing &amp; Its Applications (CSPA)
abstract: Pet skin diseases represent one of the most frequent health complaints in companion animals, yet early and accurate diagnosis remains challenging for pet owners and general practitioners due to visual similarity among conditions and the need to integrate lesion appearance with symptomatic descriptions. This paper presents a novel bimodal diagnostic framework that synergistically combines enhanced ResNet50 (augmented with CBAM attention modules) for dermoscopic lesion analysis and Language-BERT for processing naturallanguage symptom descriptions. Features from both modalities are fused using weighted, concatenation, and attention-based strategies before final disease and species classification. The system is trained on a large-scale dataset exceeding $\mathbf{5 0, 0 0 0}$ annotated pet skin images covering cats and dogs, supplemented by symptom texts. Experimental results demonstrate that the bimodal approach achieves a best validation accuracy of 92.59% and final training accuracy of 97.80%, significantly outperforming unimodal image-only $\boldsymbol{(} \boldsymbol{\approx} \mathbf{8 8} \boldsymbol{-} \mathbf{9 0 \%} \boldsymbol{)}$ and text-only baselines. The deployed Python system supports single-modality (image/text) and joint inference, providing top 3 differential diagnoses together with treatment suggestions. This work advances veterinary AI by incorporating multimodal reasoning in a language-sensitive manner, offering a practical, low-cost auxiliary tool for early pet dermatosis screening in Multiple-speaking regions and beyond.

title: A Strategic AI Governance Framework for Cybersecurity: Ensuring Security, Fairness and Accoutability
authors: Sundus Ayyaz, Sultan Mahmood Malik
publish: 2025 Asian Conference on Artificial Intelligence Technology (ACAIT)
abstract: The integration of Artificial Intelligence (AI) into business operations has revolutionized industries, enhancing efficiency, decision-making, and overall competitiveness. However, alongside its significance, AI adoption presents unique challenges, particularly in cybersecurity. AI-driven cybersecurity solutions have been widely deployed to detect threats, automate responses, and enhance overall system security. Yet, despite their advantages, these systems face significant challenges, including adversarial learning and data poisoning, where attackers manipulate inputs or corrupt training data to compromise system integrity. Additionally, ethical concerns, model exploitability, and the complexity of defense mechanisms highlight the need for transparency and continuous adaptation. The opaque nature of many AI models further complicates trust, error mitigation, and the development of effective security strategies. This highlights the need for strong AI governance to ensure responsible, ethical, and regulatory-compliant AI applications in cybersecurity. In this paper, we discuss the principles of AI governance in detail and introduce a structured AI governance framework specifically designed for cybersecurity. Given the growing complexity of AI-driven security measures, our framework ensures AI technologies are implemented securely, ethically, and in alignment with organizational and regulatory requirements. By addressing governance challenges, our proposed framework contributes to the responsible and sustainable development of AI in cybersecurity, mitigating risks while maximizing its potential benefits.

title: Study on Effects of Voltage Harmonics of Electric Propulsion based Ship Power System on Long Range Radar Power System
authors: Bhagam Guruvulu, Vijay Hiralal Bhosale, Suchith Rajagopal, M Sheik Althaf
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Power Electronics, Smart Grid, and Renewable Energy (PESGRE)
abstract: Long Range Radars require few Mega Watts of Power for detection and tracking of targets at hundreds or sometimes few thousands of kilo meters away. Ship based Long Range Radars require compact and highly reliable Power System of higher capacity. Power Systems of Ship Based Radars are designed to allow minimum leakage current. Low leakage currents are maintained by using circuit topologies which offer lower leakage currents and components having higher Insulation Resistance (IR). Electric Propulsion Systems inject harmonics into the Ship Power System which in turn affects the performance and reliability of Radar Power System. In this paper details of the voltage harmonics study carried out on Radar Power System are presented.

title: Manhole Cover Hazard Detection Based on the Improved YOLOv10s
authors: Donghua Yuan, Jun Tao, Wenjun Wu, Shunzhi Hou, Yifan Wei, Siyuan Meng
publish: 2025 Asian Conference on Artificial Intelligence Technology (ACAIT)
abstract: As a critical component in urban underground pipeline network maintenance, the management of manhole cover safety hazards faces operational challenges due to the wide distribution and large quantity of manhole pipelines. Traditional manual inspection methods can no longer meet the demand for efficient identification. In this study, we propose an intelligent detection optimization scheme based on YOLOv10s. The model's generalization ability is enhanced through multi-dimensional data augmentation strategies (e.g., hue perturbation, saturation adjustment, and exposure compensation). We innovatively integrate the lightweight and efficient Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM) with the Coordinate Attention (CA) module, constructing a dual-channel feature screening mechanism to suppress interference from complex backgrounds. Experimental results demonstrate that the improved model achieves significantly higher mean Average Precision (mAP) on the manhole cover hazard dataset compared to the baseline YOLOv10s model and traditional detection methods. This solution provides effective technical support for safety monitoring in urban underground spaces.

title: ITD-Aware CPU Power Modeling to Identify Peak-Efficiency Thermal Regions for Sustainable Data Center Operation
authors: Jason Crop, Hayden Moore, Sudeep Pasricha
abstract: Data center power consumption continues to grow rapidly with the increasing demand for cloud and enterprise computing. CPU active power and cooling account for a major share of this energy use, yet manufacturers provide no visibility into each CPU's unique power–thermal behavior. Process variations and inverse temperature dependence (ITD) cause individual CPUs to exhibit distinct electrical characteristics — including voltage, dynamic capacitance, and leakage — which together determine their optimal operating temperature for maximum performance-per-watt. This paper introduces a new ITD-aware CPU power modeling framework that integrates empirical voltage, leakage, and capacitance measurements to predict total power across temperature and frequency with high accuracy. The model is validated on 22 Intel Xeon server CPUs, achieving less than 3% error across operating conditions. Using the model, we identify each CPU's optimal temperature that minimizes power while sustaining performance. Analysis of real cloud servers reveals that around 50% of modern CPUs operate roughly 10°C below their optimal point, wasting approximately 15% in excess cooling energy. Classifying CPUs by their optimal temperature enables data centers to group and cool them more efficiently, reducing energy use and improving sustainability without performance degradation.

title: A Low Profile, Small Size Asymmetric Parabolic Cylindrical Vortex Antenna Based on Anisotropic Holographic Metasurface
authors: Zhaoneng Jiang, Xinzeng Gu, He Shuang, Hui Liang, Bingzan Sun, Lang Wu
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: In this paper, an asymmetric parabolic cylindrical antenna based on anisotropic holographic impedance modulation surface is proposed. The anisotropic unit incorporates a slotted octagonal patch with adjustable structural parameters, which enables independent and flexible control of tensor impedance. Based on the proposed anisotropic unit, a holographic impedance surface is designed for loading onto a parabolic cylinder. Using a monopole antenna as the feed, the structure can generate high-purity OAM beams with modes of l = +1, +2, and −3. For verification, the antenna with l = +1 mode is fabricated and measured. Both measurement and simulation results demonstrate that the proposed antenna achieves high-purity OAM beam generation with a compact size of 4.5λ×4.5λ and an extremely low profile of 0.05λ. The proposed structure breaks the geometric limits of conventional planar or symmetric conformal designs, making it suitable for curved conformal platforms such as UAV skins and wearable devices.

title: Reliability Class Testing Hypothesis for EBUCL*t 0 with Applications for Medical and Engineering Data Modeling
authors: Walid B. H. Etman, Rashad M. El-Sagheer, Mahmoud E. Bakr, Arwa M. Alshangiti, Farouq Mohammad A. Alam, Oluwafemi Samsonbalogun, Ali M. Sharawy
abstract: This paper introduces a new reliability class, the exponential better than used after age t0 in increasing convex Laplace transform order (EBUCL∗t0), and develops a corresponding statistical test for exponentiality. The test is constructed within the U-statistics framework and its theoretical properties, including asymptotic distribution and Pitman’s asymptotic efficiency, are established. Extensive Monte Carlo simulations are conducted to evaluate critical values and power performance under both complete and censored samples. Results demonstrate that the proposed procedure achieves higher efficiency and superior power compared to existing nonparametric tests. Applications to diverse engineering datasets (electrical insulation, glass fiber strength, polyester fibers, LED lifetimes, and windshields failure) and biomedical data (melanoma patients) confirm its practical utility in detecting deviations from exponentiality and modeling aging phenomena. Overall, the EBUCL∗t0 test provides a robust and versatile tool for reliability engineering and survival analysis, particularly in scenarios involving aging effects and censored data.

title: PCCA: A Hypothesis-Driven Prompt-Conditioned Causal Auditing Framework for Industrial Knowledge Graphs
authors: Hongwei Jiang, Jiapeng You, Jiayu Shi, Zhiyang Chen, Huaxing Gou, Xinguo Ming, Poly Z.H. Sun
abstract: Knowledge-driven industrial systems are brittle to dynamic conditions as their knowledge graphs (KGs) are static. This leads to costly decision failures (e.g., needless hardware replacement) and erodes operator trust. These KGs remain static because existing knowledge completion and causal discovery methods struggle with statistical correlations or complex industrial data, lacking a mechanism to proactively find their own knowledge deficiencies. To address this knowledge maintenance gap, this article proposes prompt-conditioned causal auditing (PCCA), a framework for the systematic, incremental auditing of existing KGs. PCCA translates operational hypotheses into prompts, guiding a deep graph model to efficiently evaluate their structural impact. This hypothesis-driven audit precisely locates overlooked or erroneous causal relationships. The framework is validated on both public benchmarks (Win95pts, DREAM4) and an industrial inkjet case study, demonstrating robust generalizability across diverse topologies. PCCA provides a practical process for evolving these knowledge assets, enhancing operational reliability and preventing unnecessary maintenance.

title: 2026 Second International Conference on Emerging Computational Intelligence (ICECI)
authors: N/A

title: Omnidirectional Wireless Power Transfer at MHz for Capsule Endoscopy
authors: Heng Zhang, Paul D. Mitcheson, Chi-Kwan Lee
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: The advancement of capsule endoscopy toward higher-resolution imaging and multifunctional capabilities has significantly increased power consumption, rendering traditional low-frequency (kHz) wireless charging methods and limited battery capacities insufficient. We present a miniaturized 6.78 MHz wireless power transfer (WPT) system that eliminates magnetic cores and leverages compact MHz coils to drastically reduce both transmitter and receiver size. A three-dimensional orthogonal receiving coil design achieves near-zero mutual coupling, enabling independent operation without interference, with outputs combined via full-bridge rectification and filtering. A prototype, validated through modeling, simulation, and experiments under varying positions and orientations, delivers up to 1.681 W with 23.0 % peak efficiency. These results demonstrate efficient high-frequency wireless charging in a compact form factor, offering a viable solution for next-generation capsule endoscopes.

title: A Self-Supervised Uncertainty-Hybrid Machine Learning Approach for Modeling Tropospheric Zenith Wet Delay_supp1-3694884.docx
authors: Weixing Zhang

title: Thermal Measurement of Asymmetric Losses in a Submodule of an Isolated Modular Multilevel DC-DC Converter
authors: José Andrés Aguilar Croston, Narges Moeini, Jean-Yves Gauthier, Cyril Buttay, Besar Asllani, Piotr Dworakowski, Maryam Saeedifard
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: In this work, asymmetric loss distribution between the high-side and low-side devices of an Isolated Modular Multilevel DC-DC Converter (IM2DC) submodule (SM) is identified and experimentally quantified, revealing that low-side devices can dissipate up to twice the losses of their high-side counterparts. This imbalance indicates unequal semiconductor utilization, with direct implications for thermal design and long-term reliability in modular DC-DC architectures. To investigate this phenomenon, a steady-state calorimetric method based on thermal imaging is applied directly to a half-bridge SM while the full converter operates under various voltage levels. The approach provides accurate, non-intrusive loss measurements and is validated through analytical estimations with an absolute error below 0.07 W. These findings lay the groundwork for future optimization of IM2DC converter design and control.

title: Pathology-Centric Region Exchange: Joint Carotid Plaque Segmentation and Vulnerability Assessment in Ultrasound
authors: Xuanqi Huang, Bodong Du, Xinrui Zhou, Xiaomeng Li
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Automated plaque segmentation and vulnerability assessment of carotid ultrasound images plays a critical role in stroke prevention and clinical risk stratification. However, developing robust models for this joint task is hindered by limited annotated dataset and sparse supervisory signals of classification task. In this paper, we propose our Pathology-Centric Region Exchange framework to address the task in CSV 2026 Challenge: Carotid Plaque Segmentation and Vulnerability Assessment in Ultrasound in ISBI 2026. Specifically, we utilize unlabeled data through a pathology-centric region exchange strategy, swapping plaque-centric patches between labeled and unlabeled samples across longitudinal and transverse views to preserve discriminative morphological cues. Subsequently, we employ a region-aligned loss to mitigate pseudo-label uncertainty in critical pathological regions and optimize joint supervision. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method significantly improves segmentation accuracy and classification performance compared with existing semi-supervised baselines. As a technical report for the challenge, this paper elaborates on our methodology and experimental findings in detail. The code will be made publicly available upon acceptance.

title: A Multistage Soft Start-up Technique for SHB-FB Type DAB Converter Considering Precharge of DC Blocking Capacitors
authors: Yanda Lyu, Ziwei Liang, Fred Wang, Hua Bai, Zhou Dong, Debanjan Chatterjee
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: Soft start-up in Dual-Active-Bridge (DAB) converters is a significant challenge due to the need to mitigate initial inrush currents. This challenge is even more pronounced in the Stacked Half-Bridge Full-Bridge (SHB-FB) converter incorporating an additional blocking capacitor and is a derivative of the conventional DAB converter. This paper initially assesses the start-up issues introduced by the dc blocking capacitor in the SHB-FB converter, which has received limited attention in existing literature. Then, a multistage soft start-up technique for the SHB-FB converter is proposed, considering the precharging process of the dc blocking capacitor. The technique is first validated through simulations and further experimentally verified with a Gallium Nitride (GaN)-based SHB-FB converter. The start-up challenges arising from the blocking capacitor are addressed, and experimental results show a 74% decrease in the initial inrush current compared with the hard start-up condition.

title: Synthesized Ultra-High B-Value Diffusion Images for Characterization of Neurite and Soma Organization
authors: Tenglong Wang, Zhonghua Wan, Yu Xie, Jiahao Yu, Yifei He, Ye Wu
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Diffusion MRI (dMRI) is an advanced neuroimaging technique that provides insights into the microstructural characteristics of the brain. However, acquiring high-b-value dMRI data presents significant challenges due to substantial hardware costs, specialized protocols, and the time-consuming nature of the process. Consequently, obtaining high-b-value dMRI data remains a significant challenge in neuroimaging. We propose a novel approach that employs a conditional diffusion model to accurately predict high-b-value images from a series of low-b-value diffusion-weighted images. Our method adaptively models signal decay patterns and captures essential microstructural details. Unlike conventional image generation techniques, our approach does not depend on a specific b -value acquisition protocol, enabling it to adapt to a range of low-b-value inputs. This flexibility significantly enhances its generalization capabilities across different datasets. We validate our method on multiple datasets, demonstrating its effectiveness and broad applicability. The code is available at https://github.com/1nlandempire/UHb-DWI-Synth.

title: Riva Cytology Classification and Detection: A Lesson on How “None of Us is as Smart as All of Us”
authors: Chris Kang, Vibujithan Vigneshwaran, Nils D. Forkert
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Early and accurate computer-aided detection of abnormal cervical cells in Papanicolaou smear images is essential for preventing cervical cancer, in particular for centers with limited access to expert cytotechnologists. The RIVA Cytology Challenge provides a large-scale, expert-annotated collection of high-resolution Papanicolaou smear images from Hospital Rivadavia, offering the unique opportunity to develop a highly accurate classification and detection model for automated cervical cancer screening. In this work, we describe our solution for the challenge in Track A, which involves both the classification and detection tasks. Our proposed solution achieved the mean Average Precision (mAP@0.50:0.95) score of 0.14183, and demonstrate many strategies, which were found to be successful in progressively improving the test scores, as well as those which failed to do so.

title: Osteo-Net: Mask R-CNN Based Osteoclasts Detector
authors: Yeonsoo Chung, Seung-Hyun Hong, Frank C. Nichols, David W. Rowe, Dong-Guk Shin
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Accurate segmentation of osteoclasts in whole-slide images (WSIs) is essential for quantifying cell counts and sizes, a prerequisite for studying bone diseases such as osteoporosis and osteopetrosis. We propose a WSI-to-instance pipeline, Osteo-Net, that integrates patch-level augmentation with seam-robust stitching (feathering, edge trimming, and mask enforcement). Osteo-Net uses Mask R-CNN (ResNet-50 FPN) with SE attention and a composite loss (focal loss for classification and BCE+Dice for masks). On validation patches, Osteo-Net achieves a Dice score of 0.95141 and F1 of 0.93636, and on held-out WSIs it attains Dice 0.9031, substantially outperforming a fine-tuned Cellpose-sam baseline (Dice 0.6623). Ablation analysis shows that focal loss and SE attention drive these gains, whereas CBAM and removal of augmentation degrade performance. These results indicate that tailored loss design with lightweight attention and WSI-aware stitching offers a practical solution for automated osteoclast quantification.

title: Magnetic Confinement of Dust Particles: Novel Instrumentation for Potash Mining Applications
authors: A. Okusanya, R. Paranjape
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: The control of dust particles in mining environments poses an occupational health and operational challenge. Traditional mitigation strategies, including water sprays, surfactants, and baghouse systems, face limitations due to high costs, maintenance requirements, and safety concerns. This paper introduces a novel instrumentation and measurement system designed to monitor triboelectric charging of moving dust particles and employ those measurements to evaluate magnetic confinement in potash mining. The setup combines mass, differential voltage, capacitive voltage, differential pressure and air quality monitoring measurements to monitor particle charging and the resulting transport behavior. The results from these experiments demonstrated that magnetic confinement improves dust capture efficiency and reduces the outlet particle concentration by up to 75%. These findings demonstrate that combining triboelectric charging with external magnetic fields provides an effective dry method for dust mitigation. It also showcases the depth of the instrumentation and its potential to support sustainable dust control in mining operations.

title: Statistical Delay Performance of STAR-RIS-Aided Short-Packet NOMA Systems for xURLLC Services
authors: Zixuan Xu, Xiong Li, Jin Xie, Qimei Cui, Xiaofeng Tao
abstract: The integration of non-orthogonal multiple access (NOMA) and simultaneously transmitting and reflecting re configurable intelligent surfaces (STAR-RIS) in short-packet communications offers a promising solution to meet the strin gent requirements of next-generation ultra-reliable low-latency communications (xURLLC). However, existing studies primarily focus on average performance metrics, which fail to capture the statistical characteristics of network performance. To address this gap, this paper proposes a novel STAR-RIS-assisted NOMA xURLLC network architecture that incorporates a stochastic network calculus (SNC)-based theoretical framework for statis tical delay performance analysis. Moreover, we adopt a novel correlated phase shift model for STAR-RIS, considering two distinct phase shift configuration strategies. Then, we derive the upper-bounded delay violation probability (DVP) for both phase shift configuration strategies. Extensive simulations validate the effectiveness of our theoretical analysis and comprehensively evaluate system performance under various network parameters, offering some insights for the STAR-RIS-assisted NOMA xURLLC system.

title: SilentNoise: Non-interactive Noise Generation for Differential Privacy with Malicious Security_supp1-3694826.pdf
authors: Reo Eriguchi
abstract: Differential privacy (DP) is a standard notion of privacy-preserving data analysis. Traditionally, DP has been studied in the central model, where a trusted curator collects all private inputs and releases aggregation results preserving DP. However, this model creates a single point of failure, as all private data are held by a single party. We aim to decentralize additive noise DP mechanisms by leveraging secure multiparty computation (MPC) techniques. The main technical challenge is how to obliviously sample secret noise from specific probability distributions in the presence of malicious parties. Most existing maliciously secure protocols require multiple rounds of interaction, resulting in high communication and computation overheads. Moreover, the only known maliciously secure, non-interactive protocol suffers from a utility loss that grows exponentially with the number of computing parties. In this work, we propose <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">SilentNoise</small>, a general framework for maliciously secure, non-interactive protocols for sampling DP noise, and present several instantiations that improve utility and efficiency both theoretically and empirically. In our protocols, any incorrect noise contributed by malicious parties is rejected and meaningful utility is guaranteed whenever the protocols succeed. After a one-time interactive setup that can be amortized over multiple uses, parties can non-interactively generate an unbounded number of shares of noise. This leads to a significant improvement in efficiency compared to the previous interactive protocols. Furthermore, our protocols improve the utility loss of the prior maliciously secure, non-interactive protocol by an exponential factor in the number of computing parties. They can even achieve a constant utility loss that is comparable to central-model DP mechanisms, under a strong honest-majority setting.

title: Fault Level Considerations in Tomorrow’s Wind and PV-Rich Power Grids: A Review
authors: Rafat Aljarrah, Mazaher Karimi, Hesamoddin Marzooghi
abstract: The increased penetration of Renewable Energy Sources (RESs), like Photovoltaic (PV) and wind energy, in transmission and distribution networks is changing features of current and future power systems. RESs may alter the characteristics of power systems due to their different dynamic behavior and limitations compared to centralized conventional Synchronous Generators (SGs). This is mainly attributed to Power Electronics (PE) interfaces (i.e., converters) utilized for integration with the power grid. As PE-based RESs show low and significantly different fault current contributions compared to SGs, fault level reduction is deemed to be one of the main challenges in future power systems. Besides, such RESs may bring new dynamics to short-circuit (SC) current characteristics of power systems, which in turn might cause challenges related to the strength, stability, operation, and protection of the grids. To tackle such challenges, it is essential to understand better the SC characteristics of the different RESs, specifically Wind and PV systems. In addition, evaluating their impact on the system fault level is necessary, especially in future power scenarios where most of the power is expected to be met by RESs. Hence, this study provides a comprehensive review of the SC current contributions of RESs and their impact on SC dynamics and the system fault level, addressing key challenges and gaps in the literature for researchers and utility engineers.

title: SkyDSafe: Federated Learning Driven Security for Drone Data
authors: Soniya Rohhila, Kedar Nath Singh, Amit Kumar Singh, Brij B. Gupta
abstract: With the rapid development of unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs), such as drones, data can be gathered from multiple sources across various sectors. Compared with other media, images are the most vital carriers of information and are more lifelike. As illegal access to these images can result in serious privacy breaches, ensuring the authenticity of digital data remains a challenging task for drones. This paper proposes a method, called SkyDSafe, for securing image data based on federated learning and advanced encryption techniques. To address data poisoning attacks, this paper employs a cryptographic Merkle tree commitment over the training dataset, allowing auditors to detect data poisoning via Merkle proofs and each client’s training transcript. Using a multi-scale deep neural network, tamper-proof image features are effectively divided into abstract representations and secret hierarchical tensors. Subsequently, only the abstract representations of the image are encrypted through a cryptographic embedder to balance the efficiency and visual security. At the receiver end, the encrypted abstract image is decrypted and combined with the corresponding hierarchical tensors retrieved from the central server to reconstruct the original image. A comparative analysis with existing techniques highlights the advantages of SkyDSafe, including tamper-proof learning and reduced time and cost, as demonstrated by the experimental results. This aspect makes SkyDSafe a robust security solution for protecting drone data and other critical applications.

title: Discrete Time Delay Control for Dexterous Hand Joint Position Tracking Under Unknown Kinematics
authors: Gan Wang, Hongjie Li, Changliang Xia
abstract: Precise joint position control in tendon-driven dexterous hands with unknown kinematics is challenging due to the difficulty in accurately mapping joint angles to electric cylinder displacement. Furthermore, existing time delay control methods present inherent trade-offs, such as large overshoot due to integral windup in step responses or nonzero steady-state errors during time-varying signals tracking, limited by fixed forgetting factors. To address these issues, this article establishes a dynamically accurate mapping model by lumping hard-to-model parts into an uncertainty term, which is estimated in real time. After analyzing the causes of the limitations of existing methods, an enhanced method incorporating a forgetting factor and a prediction term is proposed, immune to tendon wear and elasticity. The forgetting factor is adjusted automatically to suppress integration during large errors and restore it near steady state, thereby balancing overshoot and accuracy. The prediction term, designed based on the residual error, adds an immediate proportional compensation component that accelerates system response. Then, the compensation mechanism is theoretically analyzed, and parameter tuning guidelines are provided under explicit stability constraints. Comparative experiments on a dexterous hand validate the superiority of the proposed method in achieving low overshoot, short settling time, and high tracking accuracy.

title: A Simplified and Low-Cost Voltage Balancing Control Method for Series-Connected SiC MOSFETs
authors: Xianglai Zeng, Hamood-Ur-Rehman, Daixin Chen, Yue Zhao, Xiaoqing Song
publish: 2026 IEEE Applied Power Electronics Conference and Exposition (APEC)
abstract: High-voltage (>10 kV) SiC MOSFETs enable compact and efficient medium-/high-voltage (MV/HV) power conversion, however, their high cost and limited availability restrict widespread adoption. Series connecting low-voltage (LV) SiC MOSFETs provides a practical alternative, but ensuring reliable dynamic voltage balancing remains a key challenge. This paper proposes a simplified active voltage balancing method using a low-voltage bipolar junction transistor (BJT) integrated into the Kelvin source path of each SiC MOSFET. The BJT conduction is dynamically regulated by a closed-loop PI controller, which adjusts the effective gate driving current based on the sampled drain–source voltage. This enables accurate voltage sharing during switching transients without sacrificing high-speed operation. The proposed voltage balancing circuits share a common reference ground between the SiC MOSFET and BJT, thereby eliminating additional isolated power supplies and isolation components typically required in prior active balancing circuits. The method is scalable and validated in both simulations and experiments with three 1.2-kV devices operating at up to a 1.8-kV DC bus, across a wide range of current levels, reaching peak values exceeding 90 A. Results confirm effective voltage balancing with reduced circuit complexity and cost, highlighting the potential of the proposed technique for high-voltage SiC converter applications.

title: SPARNet: A Framework for Airway Invasion Tracking from Fluoroscopic Videos of Dysphagia Patients
authors: Sanjeevi G, Uma Gopalakrishnan, Rahul Krishnan Pathinarupothi, Arya CJ, Kaustuv M. Talukdar, Subramania Iyer, Srisairam Achuthan
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: The videofluoroscopic swallowing study (VFSS) is the clinical gold standard for evaluating dysphagia and detecting airway invasion. However, manual interpretation is time-consuming, subjective, and prone to rater variability due to the rapid and complex nature of swallowing events. Existing deep learning approaches for airway invasion are limited by relying on manual frame selection, a lack of interpretability, and black-box decision-making. In this study, we propose SPARNet (Swallowing Phase Airway Risk Network), AI pipeline integrates swallowing-phase classification, bolus and airway delineation, and airway-invasion tracking to provide a clinically interpretable assessment. SPARNet introduces a novel Airway Invasion Tracker (AIT) that partitions the visible laryngo-tracheal air column in VFSS into quadrants, tracks the location of the bolus, and computes temporal invasion parameters (event duration, total exposure time, and number of invasion episodes) to characterize airway invasion during the pharyngeal phase. We curated a VFSS-MultiLabel dataset comprising 125 clips from 75 patients, annotated for multiple tasks. SPARNet achieved strong performance across all components, including the pharyngeal phase detection (F1-score = 88%), bolus segmentation (Dice score = 0.83), laryngo-tracheal air column detection (mAP = 0.95), and airway invasion detection (F1-score = 92%). Unlike prior black-box models, SPARNet provides transparent spatial and temporal cues that align with clinical VFSS reasoning. These results demonstrate the feasibility of an system-level AI pipeline for VFSS analysis, highlighting the potential to support clinical decision-making in the management of dysphagia.

title: Design and Modeling of a Human-Powered Pneumatic Supply System: Application as a Pneumatic Source for Peristaltic Pumps
authors: Miku Tsubouchi, Masaki Kawano, Chiaki Yamazaki, Fumio Ito, Taro Nakamura
abstract: This study proposes a manually operated pneumatic supply system that operates without an external power source. Soft robots, known for their high compliance, are increasingly used to be utilized in various fields such as manufacturing, healthcare, and agriculture. However, they often rely on bulky pneumatic systems that require external power, limiting their applicability in extreme environments such as outer space and disaster-stricken areas. To overcome this limitation, this study introduces a pneumatic supply system that eliminates the need for conventional compressors and electric vacuum pumps by utilizing a manually operated pump to pressurize an air tank. A theoretical model was developed to determine the required tank specifications based on the air consumption and operating pressure of the actuator. Furthermore, a model was formulated to estimate the number of manual pumping cycles necessary to achieve the target pressure. Experimental validation identified key parameters and confirmed the model’s accuracy. The results demonstrated that the model is particularly effective for tanks with 1 L or larger. To assess its feasibility in extreme environments, the system was tested using a soft peristaltic pump designed to mimic intestinal peristalsis. The drive experiment verified that the pump successfully completed the target of five cycles, thereby validating the predictions of the model.

title: Co-design of Low-Profile Leaky-Wave-Fed Transmit-Array Antennas Using the Generalized Risley Prism Concept for Side-Lobe Reduction
authors: Sérgio A. Matos, Margarida Fernandes, João M. Felício, Jorge R. Costa, Carlos A. Fernandes, Nelson J. G. Fonseca
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: Emerging millimeter wave communications systems require compact, low-mass, low-cost, and energy-efficient antennas, capable of high-gain, wide-angle beam steering. Recent advances in mechanical beam-scanning techniques make them attractive for the above requirements, compared to the phased array antennas, but are still bulkier. A leaky-wave-fed transmit-array (TA) antenna with two-dimensional (2D) beam scanning based on the Risley prism concept (RP), with coaxial rotation of the planar feed and the TA, is a promising approach for reducing the antenna profile to about 1-2λ, while maintaining a good beam scanning performance, comparable to other planar solutions. However, these structures suffer from the same side-lobe (SLL) level limitation known for the spatially fed RPs. Another problem is the efficiency/complexity of the feeding antenna, which may limit antenna height reduction. To mitigate these impairments, we propose a novel co-design approach for the feed and the TA structures, enabled by the recent generalized Risley prism formulation. It allows using a Radial Line Slot Antenna (RLSA) both as the leaky-wave feed and first prism efficiently, thus benefiting from its simplicity and beam-forming flexibility. As an example, the TA is implemented as a perforated 3D-printed dielectric slab, which favors a large bandwidth, with 206 mm diameter and 2λ; total thickness (1 mm prism separation). It steers the beam up to 58° over 360° azimuth, with SLL below -15 dB, scan loss below -4.0 dB, and 29.3 dBi maximum gain at 29 GHz. The full-wave results were validated experimentally. The proposed formulation is generic, not tied to this example.

title: Digital Group Therapy: Interdisciplinary Challenges and Opportunities for Biomedical Circuits and Systems
authors: H. F. Jelinek, H. Al-Nashash, F. V. Kusmartsev, J. Barreiro-Gomez, T. Aminosharieh Najafi, I. M. Elfadel
abstract: Ahstract Digital Group Therapy (DGT) combines clinically-led group psychotherapy with continuous multimodal sensing, edge AI analytics, and online interaction platforms. This article synthesizes five perspectives presented at the IEEE CASS Workshop on Digital Group Therapy: neuro-dynamic modeling of group processes, cognitive workload management, next-generation edge—AI wearables, game-theoretic modeling of group behavior, and physics-inspired models of social interaction (sociophysics/social physics). We frame DGT as an open, multi-layer system of systems in which neural, physiological, cognitive, and social dynamics co-evolve under continuous feedback. We then connect this framework to recent evidence on online and mixed-reality group therapy and internet-delivered Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT), and to classical and modern work in sociophysics and opinion dynamics. The resulting architectural view reveals concrete circuits-and-systems challenges in scalable signal acquisition, robust edge intelligence, human-in-the-loop adaptation, incentive design, and validation of closed-loop DGT interventions.

title: Efficient Wavelength Conversion in Telecom Band using Active Silicon Microring Resonators
authors: Abdou Shetewy, Weizhong Zhang, Menglong He, Kambiz Jamshidi
abstract: An active silicon microring resonator is designed to suppress nonlinear losses and enable efficient, voltage-controlled nonlinear operation in a silicon-on-insulator platform. Sweeping out the free carriers from a silicon microring resonator enhances wavelength conversion efficiency from <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{- 1 8 ~ d B}$</tex> up to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{- 7. 5 ~ d B}$</tex>.

title: paraDOX™: A New Photonic Switching Architecture
authors: Jose Garcia Echeverria, Matthew Posner, David Rolston
abstract: ParaDOX<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">TM</sup> <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">${ }^{\text{TM}}$</tex>—parallel Digital Optical Cross (X) Connect is an entirely new packet switching architecture that scales Radix to thousands of ports by using distributed photonic technology. paraDOX <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">${ }^{\text{TM}}$</tex> still targets <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$10 / 100 \mathrm{G}+$</tex> Ethernet protocols while also maintaining the data center network interconnects, servers and GPU nodes.

title: PE-TSFM: Self-Supervised Time-Series Learning for Generalizable Power Converter Health Monitoring Under Unseen Conditions
authors: Xinyuan Liao, Xinyue Zhang, Xing Wei, Junwei Liu, Shuai Zhao, Siqi Bu, Yi Zhang
abstract: Data-driven health monitoring of power converters remains limited by poor generalization to unseen operating conditions. This work addresses this out-of-distribution (OOD) challenge by building a domain-specific time-series foundation model (PE-TSFM) that learns representations directly from large-scale unlabeled converter data. Unlike generic TSFMs trained on broad time-series datasets, the proposed PE-TSFM is pretrained entirely on domain data, enabling it to learn the physical relationships unique to power electronics. To further tailor the model to this domain, we introduce a dual-attention mechanism that captures both temporal patterns and inter-channel dependencies. While generic TSFMs primarily model temporal dependencies, the added channel attention (CA) captures inter-sensor physical relationships essential for converter degradation analysis. A dataset containing 141 million unlabeled timestamps from an operating power converter is used for pretraining. Experiments show that PE-TSFMs achieve 76%–92% accuracy under unseen operating conditions. In contrast, generic TSFMs achieve around 60% and conventional time-series models achieve around 40% accuracy. This result confirms the strong OOD generalization of the proposed PE-TSFM. Ablation studies further verify that the introduced CA mechanism significantly improves model performance. In addition, we conduct detailed studies on model scalability, hyperparameter sensitivity, and interpretability to provide a comprehensive understanding of the proposed approach.

title: Dynamic Group Joint ICA (Dyna-gjICA) Uncovers Neural Mechanisms Underlying Depression with and without Anxiety
authors: Souvik Phadikar, Martini P. Paulus, Rayus Kuplicki, Masaya Misaki, Vince D Calhoun
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Multimodal fusion of electroencephalography (EEG) and functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) remains challenging because EEG and fMRI differ in structure, and most existing methods compress their dynamics into static summaries. Previously, we developed a group joint independent component analysis (gjICA) framework to jointly decompose EEG and fMRI, but it did not capture spatially evolving fMRI networks. Here, we extend gjICA to dynamic gjICA (Dyna-gjICA) to incorporate spatial dynamic features of fMRI, enabling joint analysis of time-resolved fMRI networks and EEG spectral dynamics. Applied to simultaneous EEG-fMRI data from 65 anxious-depression (AD) and 65 non-anxious-depression (NAD) patients, the model identified one significant subcortical, two dorsalattention, and one language component discriminating AD from NAD. Further AD shows a pattern of increased fMRI loadings coupled with reduced EEG spectral power in subcortical and language domain, indicating an inverse EEGfMRI relationship.

title: Forecasting Future Anatomies: Longitudinal Brain MRI-TO-MRI Prediction
authors: Ali Farki, Elaheh Moradi, Deepika Koundal, Jussi Tohka
publish: 2026 IEEE 23rd International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI)
abstract: Predicting future brain state from a baseline magnetic resonance image (MRI) is a central challenge in neuroimaging, and has important implications for studying neurodegenerative diseases such as Alzheimer's disease (AD). Most existing approaches predict future cognitive scores or clinical outcomes, such as conversion from mild cognitive impairment to dementia. Instead, here we investigate longitudinal MRI image-to-image prediction that forecasts a participant's entire brain MRI several years into the future, intrinsically modeling complex, spatially distributed neurodegenerative patterns. We implement and evaluate five deep learning architectures (UNet, U<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">${ }^{2}$</tex>-Net, UNETR, Time-Embedding UNet, and ODEUNet) on two longitudinal cohorts (ADNI and AIBL). Predicted follow-up MRIs are directly compared with the actual follow-up scans using metrics that capture global similarity and local differences. The best performing models achieve high-fidelity predictions, and all models generalize well to an independent external dataset, demonstrating robust crosscohort performance. Our results indicate that deep learning can reliably predict participant-specific brain MRI at the voxel level, offering new opportunities for individualized prognosis.

title: A Collision-Aware Kinematic Model of the qb SoftHand 2 in CoppeliaSim
authors: Inês Costa, Daniel Rodrigues, Hugo Remelgado, Luís Louro, Eliana Costa e Silva, Estela Bicho
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Autonomous Robot Systems and Competitions (ICARSC)
abstract: Soft anthropomorphic robotic hands offer intrinsic compliance, adaptability, and passive safety, making them well suited for manipulation tasks in unstructured human environments. Their integration into robotic systems is essential to enhance the capabilities of modern anthropomorphic robots and promote acceptance in human–robot collaboration. This paper presents a kinematic and collision-aware simulation model of the qb SoftHand 2 to support human-like manipulation planning, despite the lack of direct joint sensing. A forward kinematic model based on a modified Denavit–Hartenberg formulation and a hierarchical sphere-based hand representation are introduced to enable efficient fingertip pose computation and collision avoidance. The model is implemented in CoppeliaSim and validated through simulated manipulation tasks involving objects with diverse physical properties, demonstrating realistic, collision-free grasping behavior.

title: Self-Supervised Learning for Energy-Efficient Traversability Prediction in Natural Terrain Using Proprioception
authors: Afonso E. Carvalho, David Portugal, Paulo Peixoto
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Autonomous Robot Systems and Competitions (ICARSC)
abstract: A key step for successful navigation in outdoor environments is traversability analysis - the ability to compute the feasibility and/or effort associated with the traversal of a certain portion of terrain. Traversability analysis is often hindered by multiple factors, such as the unstructured nature of the environment and sensor occlusion, rendering the estimates inaccurate. We propose P-STEER, a method to analyze the long-term traversability of a ground robot considering its proprioceptive data. Using the robot’s instantaneous current draw while traveling through previously observed terrain, the system is able to reliably learn and accurately predict future traversability estimates, enabling autonomous robots to operate in large-scale environments with increased effectiveness. Our method is evaluated experimentally using a quadruped robot, in real-world natural settings, including a dense forest. We compare it to a state-of-the-art approach and show that it outperforms it in both known and unseen rough terrain conditions. The open-source software is available at https://github.com/Forestry-Robotics-UC/P-STEER.

title: Multiparameter Testing Method for the Armature Muzzle-Leaving Time of the Augmented Electromagnetic Railgun
authors: Jie Yan, Yi Chen, Chengdong Liu, Yi Wang, Weisheng Ye, Juxiang Li, Wanying Wang, Sui Wang, Kai Huang, Tao Zhang, Zhun Li, Zhengzheng Yang
abstract: The armature muzzle-leaving time is an important index for the consistency evaluation of the electromagnetic railgun launching performance. At the same time, it is also a key parameter that affects the damage performance when multiple barrels of the electromagnetic railgun launch simultaneously. To obtain this key parameter with high precision, high efficiency, low cost, and in a simple and quick manner, a multiparameter testing method for the armature muzzle-leaving time of the augmented electromagnetic railgun is proposed, which is based on the conventional testing methods of electromagnetic rail launching. This method utilizes the time-domain and time–frequency domain signal characteristics of three signals (launching rail current, muzzle voltage, and arc suppression current) at the instant of armature exit from the muzzle to achieve multiangle testing of the armature muzzle-leaving time. Moreover, their relative precision is analyzed. Through the analysis of the results of launch tests at different energy levels and multiple groups, it is found that this testing method can achieve high-precision measurement of the armature muzzle-leaving time. It has satisfactory prospects for engineering application and practical value. It can provide reliable data support for the comprehensive damage performance evaluation and consistency evaluation of the electromagnetic railgun.

title: An Air-Jet Continuum Robot for Industrial Integrated Fluidic Circuit Inspection_supp8-3682403.mp4
authors: Yongchang Zhang
abstract: Industrial fluidic circuits operating under long-term extreme conditions are prone to defects such as cavitation, corrosion, and cracks, raising a critical demand for in-situ robotic inspection. Traditional manual disassembly-based methods are inefficient, while existing robots lack the ability to simultaneously adapt to complex geometries and maintain stable adsorption on surfaces of varying curvature. This study proposes an air-jet continuum robot (AJCR) that integrates three core modules: air-jet modules, a unit flexible continuum skeleton (UFCS), and stiffness-adjustable flexible suction cups (FSCs), enabling effective crawling and inspection inside integrated industrial fluid circuits. The UFCS provides omnidirectional bending capability of up to 196° enabling navigation through complex channels including abrupt turns of 120° inside stop valves. Under an input pressure of 0.8 MPa, the AJCR generates a maximum air-jet counter-thrust of 4.72 N for adsorption and 4.16 N for driving, ensuring reliable locomotion on unstructured, slippery, and discontinuous surfaces. Based on the sliding suction strategy, the FSC offers a maximum static adsorption force of 35.1 N, reduces sliding friction to 2.25 N on stainless steel, and adapts to curved surfaces with radii as small as 125 mm, facilitating stable sliding adsorption even across welded seams. Experimental results demonstrate that the AJCR smoothly navigates pipeline systems, enters stop valves, mixed-flow pumps and pressure vessels, and operates in liquid-filled environments, significantly improving inspection coverage and operational safety in fluidic delivery systems.

title: A Fractional-Order Model-assisted Reduced-order ADRC for Photoelectric Stabilized Platforms
authors: Guoyu Lin, Jiangong Wang, Hongwei Di, Xu Chen
abstract: To enhance the dynamic performance and disturbance rejection capability of photoelectric stabilized platforms, this study proposes a Fractional-Order Model-assisted Reduced-order Linear Active Disturbance Rejection Control (FO-MRLADRC) algorithm. First, a Model-assisted Reduced-order Linear Extended State Observer (MRLESO) was designed to improve observation efficiency and accuracy by leveraging the measurability and reliability of system output signals. Next, a fractional-order PD controller served as the control law to strengthen system stability and control performance. These improvements are closely interrelated and collectively contribute to the algorithm’s performance. Comparative experiments were conducted by simulation experiments and real platform experiments to evaluate dynamic response, input tracking and disturbance rejection, benchmarking against conventional linear ADRC and reduced-order linear ADRC algorithms reported in the literature. Results demonstrated that the proposed FO-MRLADRC outperformed the reference algorithms in terms of accuracy, dynamic response speed, and resistance to external disturbances.

title: Integrated System for Wireless Power Transfer and Auxiliary Charging of High-Voltage and Low-Voltage Batteries in Electric Vehicles
authors: Yiming Zhang, Wei Xu, Chao Liu, Ronghuan Xie, Xiaoying Chen, Xiangpeng Cheng, Xingkui Mao, Yuanchao Wu
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Industrial Electronics
abstract: Wireless Power Transfer (WPT) technology can be used to charge the high-voltage (HV) power battery of electric vehicles (EVs), while the low-voltage (LV) auxiliary battery is charged via a separate auxiliary power module (APM). Maintaining two distinct charging systems leads to increased costs, bulkier designs, and added weight for the EV. This study introduces a novel integrated architecture that combines the WPT and APM systems by sharing key components. A compact pickup coil is integrated into the receiving coil of the APM, enabling it to directly draw power from the WPT system. This innovative approach allows the DC/AC converter, compensation network, and receiving coil to serve dual purposes, supporting both WPT and APM functionalities. Moreover, it enables power transfer from the HV side to the LV side while the EV is in motion. The system achieves a significant reduction in weight and size while boosting the overall power density of the EV charging infrastructure. A prototype was developed and tested to verify the feasibility of the proposed design. This integrated solution offers a more compact, efficient, and cost-effective approach to EV charging.

title: Simulation of the Longwave Radiance Tendency on Sea Surface Temperature Observed from Moon-based Platform
authors: Yuhan Wei, Guoqiang Chen, Mingyang Lv, Wenhui Wan, Dan Song, Pei Tan, Huadong Guo
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: Longwave radiance (LWR), a fundamental measure of Earth’s thermal radiation, serves as a key indicator of global climate change. A future Moon-based radiation observatory, characterized by stable orbital configuration, unique spatial environment, and etc., facilitates the acquisition of longwave radiance data and underpins associated climate research. This study has developed a model to simulate earth LWR observed from Moon and corresponding coefficient of determination (R²) with sea surface temperature (SST) in critical zone, utilizing outgoing longwave radiation based on CERES Edition 4 ADMs, and MCD12C1 data. Results show (1) relatively small monthly variations (standard deviation ~0.5 W/(m²·sr)) and stable, periodic interannual fluctuations ranging from approximately 70.5 to 80.5 W/(m²·sr); (2) the oceans contribute the largest share (~70%) to earth's total LWR; (3) long-term data from 2004–2022 has no significant trend in global LWR; (4) R² between SST and global oceanic LWR exhibits a distinct zonal distribution pattern. During El Niño events, R² in affected regions varied synchronously with event intensity, exhibiting a proportional relationship between event strength and the magnitude of R² enhancement. In general, this study lays a foundation and a novel approach for interpreting earth radiation features in future missions.
